docs.title: Conduvia Player Guide
docs.text: THE FIELD MANUAL

docs.title: Rebuild the modern world. For real.
docs.text:
You woke up here with nothing — stone, sticks, and the next few hours. Conduvia is about climbing from a twisted-fiber rope all the way to a kerolox rocket on the pad, where every recipe mirrors a real industrial process. You already know how the modern world works. Now build it from scratch.

Your first hour →See the climb

docs.title: Start here
docs.text:
What is Conduvia?

The pitch, the loop, and why the order is forced.

Your first hour

A survival walkthrough: cordage, water, fire, sleep.

Crafting & stations

How recipes, stations, and the inventory work.

Win-path vs branches

What’s on the critical line and what’s optional.

docs.title: The climb
docs.text:
Eight tiers, each gated by the one before it. Click a rung to jump in.

0SurvivalFiber, fire, water, a cutting edge — a night you survive. 1StoneKilns, fired clay, charcoal, quicklime. 2Metal AgesCopper, bronze, wrought iron. 3SteamCoke, coal tar, mechanical power. 4Steel & DCSteel, shapes, DC dynamo, motors. 5ElectricAC grid, electrolysis, arc furnace — the chemistry gateway. 6Solid StatePolysilicon, wafers, ICs, automation. 7SpaceCryogenics, titanium, the rocket. ORBITAL LAUNCH (win).

docs.title: What's New
docs.text: The newest additions to Conduvia, pulled straight from the live build. If you just updated and something here is new to you, this is where to look first.

docs.title: Latest build — Heat, energy & early crafting
Energy is now real
docs.text: Fired stations are calibrated, so recipes now carry meaningful energy costs (in kJ) alongside their craft time. You'll see these on every recipe card — for example, a basic-oxygen-furnace steel heat runs ~335 kJ, while a copper matte smelt runs ~1,850 kJ. Plan your power and fuel accordingly.

docs.title: New early-game crafting
docs.text:
Three new recipe packs fill in the early game — camp infrastructure, pottery & vessels, and rigging & haulage. Highlights now buildable:

  • Vessels & pottery: clay pot (coiled or thrown), wooden tub, rawhide waterskin, potter's kick wheel (a new station), clay tuyere.
  • Rigging & haulage: windlass, capstan, block & tackle, pulley, treadle, wheel & axle, wooden screw jack, counterweight, a-frame gin pole, rope-walk jig.
  • Camp & site: fire pit, holdfast, sieve, coarse screen, sluice trough, coiled basket, thatch roof, wattle-and-daub wall, steam drill.

Many of these were previously place-only with no way to build them — they now all have proper recipes.

docs.title: Previously added
docs.text:
• Power generation: Gas Engine Genset, Steam Turbine, Francis Water Turbine, Modern Wind Turbine, Solar PV Array.
  • Storage & electronics: Lead-Acid Battery Bank, Lithium-Ion & NiCd batteries, Soldering Iron, Cadmium, Silver Oxide.

docs.title: At a glance
docs.text:
Catalogue Count Craftable recipes 1,334 Items & materials ~1,150 Stations 182 Loot bags 10 Field-note item entries 167

How this page stays current

Everything above is generated from the same live data that powers the recipe, item, and station pages, so the catalogue counts always match what's actually in the game. Item counts are an as-of snapshot and may shift slightly as data is refined.

docs.title: The Climb
docs.text:
Eight chapters, gated in a fixed order. Each one unlocks a real capability — and the order is not arbitrary. You cannot skip a rung, because the physics of the next rung depends on the one below it. No silicon without industrial electricity; no electricity at scale without steel; no steel without coke-grade heat.

0SurvivalFiber, fire, water, a cutting edge — a night you survive. 1StoneKilns, fired clay, charcoal, quicklime. 2Metal AgesCopper, bronze, wrought iron. 3SteamCoke, coal tar, mechanical power. 4Steel & DCSteel, shapes, DC dynamo, motors. 5ElectricAC grid, electrolysis, arc furnace — the chemistry gateway. 6Solid StatePolysilicon, wafers, ICs, automation. 7SpaceCryogenics, titanium, the rocket. ORBITAL LAUNCH (win).

docs.title: The single critical win-path
docs.text:
Everything below is one honest chain. Everything off this line — farming, fishing, trapping, nuclear power, satellites — is optional. The guide marks Win-path vs Branch on every quest.

sticks + plant fiber -> cordage -> stone knife -> fire -> kiln + charcoal
  -> copper smelt -> bronze (Cu+Sn) -> bloomery -> wrought iron
  -> coke (from coal) -> steam power -> blast furnace -> steel
  -> DC dynamo -> AC three-phase grid
  -> electrolysis: aluminum, chlor-alkali (Cl2/NaOH), ferroalloys, MG-silicon
  -> trichlorosilane -> polysilicon -> Czochralski wafer -> ICs / PLC automation
  -> cryogenic air separation (LOX); Kroll titanium; Inconel turbopump; Cu-Cr chamber
  -> rocket engine -> Al-Li tanks + RP-1 -> stage -> vehicle -> ORBITAL LAUNCH (win)

docs.text: ·  [Stone →](1-stone.md)

Stone, sticks, and the next few hours. Solve water, fire, food, and sleep before they solve you.

docs.title: Conduvia
docs.text:
You woke up here with nothing. Happens to the best of us.

Stone, sticks, and the next few hours -- that's the starting inventory. The long-term plan is to build systems that build systems, but first: survive tonight.

Four things will get you if you ignore them:

  1. Water
  2. Fire
  3. Food
  4. Sleep

In that order, roughly. Try not to lose track of the first one while solving the fourth.

The rungs: Tier 0 (stone) → Tier 1 (camp) → Tier 2 (steam + steel) → Tier 3+ (electric + automation). The bottom is slow on purpose.

Tip · Open Settings → Controls to rebind anything.

docs.title: Controls
docs.text:
Action Key Quest Log ` (backtick) Inventory E Interact / Pick up F Menu / Back Backspace Recipes for item R (hover item) Uses for item U (hover item) Drop one Q Map M Sprint Left Shift Crouch Left Ctrl

Backspace will almost always go back or close a panel.

docs.title: How to gather things
docs.text:
Most materials come from walking up to something and pressing F.

  • Plant fibers -- F on tall grass, ferns, or leafy shrubs
  • River stones -- F on rocks near water or rocky ground
  • Bark slabs / bark strips -- F on tree trunks (aim at the bark, not the canopy)
  • Twigs / sticks -- F on fallen branches and forest floor debris
  • Berries / nuts -- F on shrubs and tree drip lines
  • Dry logs -- F on dead snags (the bare-trunk dead trees)

If nothing happens when you press F: try crouching (Left Ctrl) or getting closer.

Tip · Hover anything in your inventory and press U to see every recipe that uses it. R goes the other way -- "what recipes make this?"

docs.title: Quest book
docs.text:
The left rail is the chapter list -- each chapter is one tier of progression. Nodes inside a chapter are quests; lines between them are prerequisites. Click any node to see what it needs and what it rewards.

Bookmarks (left rail of the crafting pane) let you pin favorite items and recipes you're actively tracking.

docs.title: Crafting + items
docs.text: Press R on any item to see recipes that produce it. Press U to see what it's used in. Press A to favorite it. Some recipes need a station -- the quest will say which one. Drop a station from your inventory to place it.

docs.title: Death + respawn
docs.text:
Dying leaves a gravestone at the death site holding your inventory. A waypoint auto-marks it on the map so you can walk back. The grave doesn't expire and is open to anyone nearby.

Craft a bedroll and hold F on it to claim a spawn point. You'll respawn there instead of the default pad.

docs.title: What kind of game is this?
docs.text: Conduvia is a survival → automation → industry game. The loop: gather → craft → build → automate → repeat, at larger scale each time. The challenge isn't combat -- it's managing complexity without letting things fall apart.

docs.title: Cordage
docs.text:
Rope is the duct tape of prehistory. It shows up in basically every recipe that isn't just "rock."

Get some. More than you think you need.

Tip · Plant fibers come from three places: (1) fern shrubs drop them directly when you press F, (2) willow shrubs at riverside drop them, OR (3) if neither grows nearby, ret bark: pick forest_bark_strip (F on a tree trunk -- aim at the bark, not the canopy), put 3 strips + 1 bark_bucket_water in the camp marker and craft plant_fibers. The water soaks the bast fiber loose from the woody cortex; the bucket comes back empty.

Tip · Rope itself is hand-crafted from 3 plant_fibers. R on rope in your inventory shows it.

docs.title: Cordage isn't flashy -- it just keeps showing up.
docs.text: The three obtain paths for plant fibers (fern, willow, bark-retting) mean you're never truly stuck for cordage as long as you have a forest to work with.

docs.title: Get a Starter Pile + Mark a Spot
Get a starter pile + mark a spot
docs.text:
Grab a bit of everything, then pick somewhere to work from.

The temptation early on is to do "just one more trip." Resist it. Grab a bunch now while you're already out.

docs.title: Batches, not crumbs
docs.text: Every trip should bring back more than you need right now. Early survival falls apart when every task becomes a separate emergency.

docs.title: Get a cutting edge
docs.text:
Your hands are terrible scissors. Fix that.

A stone flake is the first tool that earns its name -- something that actually cuts on purpose.

docs.title: Real tech
docs.text: A flake is the sharp piece you get when you strike stone against stone the right way. Knapping is a genuine skill. In-game you just craft it -- appreciate the shortcut.

docs.title: Hunting basics
docs.text:
A stone flake is also your first hunting tool. To kill an animal:

  1. Equip the stone flake (or any tool) from your hotbar.
  2. Walk up to an animal and left-click to strike.
  3. Keep swinging -- most animals take several hits. Predators (wolves, bears) fight back.

When an animal dies it leaves a carcass on the ground. Press F to pick it up. Take it to a butchering block (basic) and press F to break it down into meat, hide, and bones.

Start with passive animals: Rabbit, Bird, Deer, Cow, or Sheep won't attack you. Wolves and bears will. Start passive and upgrade your tools before picking fights with predators.

Tip · The hide you get from butchering is how you make rawhide_cord -- essential for the trapping quest and better tools.

docs.title: Solve water (for now)
docs.text:
Find a water source, bring it back, make it safe.

The real problem isn't finding water -- it's the ten round trips after your camp is set up somewhere else. Make a bark bucket. Carrying water in cupped hands is technically possible but deeply inefficient.

docs.title: Carrying water is a container problem
docs.text: Early camps don't fail because there's no water. They fail because water is a long walk, five times a day.

docs.title: Boiling guidance
docs.text:
• Rolling boil for 1 minute at low altitude.
  • Rolling boil for 3 minutes at high altitude.

If your container can't sit in flame (like a bark bucket), use hot-stone boiling.

docs.title: Make fire repeatable
docs.text:
Getting fire isn't the hard part. Keeping it alive is.

Three-layer stack -- each one depends on the last: - Tinder -- catches the spark (dry fiber tinder) - Kindling -- builds it into a flame (dry kindling sticks) - Fuel -- keeps it going long enough to matter (small dry sticks or logs)

To light the campfire: equip the fire_drill_kit, walk up to a placed campfire, and hold RMB. An animation plays; after a moment the fire catches. The kit wears with use -- craft a spare.

Tip · The fire ring (6 river stones at camp marker) is optional -- a campfire without a ring still works, it just doesn't look intentional.

docs.title: Controlled fire
docs.text:
You want: small fire, steady feed, easy to kill. If you don't have enough fuel, cooking and boil timers pause -- and that is annoying.

The fire_drill_kit is a usable tool: equip it, hold RMB near the campfire. It spins, uses tinder, and the fire catches.

docs.title: Set Up a Night That Doesn't Wipe You
Set up a night that doesn't wreck you
docs.text:
The ground steals your body heat. Put something between you and it.

Make a straw bedroll, find somewhere reasonably sheltered, and put it down.

Tip · To sleep: place the bedroll, walk up to it, and press E to interact. You'll get a rest prompt. Sleeping restores stamina and skips to morning.

Tip · Hover bedroll_straw and press R to see the crafting recipe (2× thatch bundle + plant fibres, at a camp marker).

docs.title: Bedding is layered
docs.text:
Grass, leaves, dry boughs -- piled thick. If the ground's cold, you'll feel it through almost anything thin.

Wind and wet can drop your temperature even above freezing. A windbreak and a roof matter as much as the bedding itself.

docs.title: Tier 0 -- Stable
docs.text:
Not bad for someone who started with nothing.

You can now:

  • get water without a trip every hour
  • make fire on purpose
  • keep food coming
  • sleep without bleeding heat all night

Reward: First Camp Gift -- a starter kit to get Tier 1 moving. Includes a fire ring, bedroll, storage pits, waste midden, firewood rack, water level gauge, and rope. Nothing duplicates what you already have.

Next: turn that marked spot into an actual camp.

docs.title: This is the line
docs.text: The next chapter (Tier 1) assumes you can stay alive while you build things.

docs.title: A Quieter Hunt
docs.text:
Not everything is worth killing.

A lasso and a basket let you move small animals around -- relocate prey closer to camp, or just keep a few around for the mid-game when you need steady yields without burning through your hunting stock.

How it works:

  1. Craft a rope lasso (2 rope + 1 rawhide cord) at the hand-crafting grid.
  2. Craft a wicker basket (2 willow + 1 plant fibers).
  3. Equip the lasso. Walk within about 15 studs of a Rabbit, Bird, or Owl and left-click.
  4. The animal is packed into a basket in your inventory.
  5. Equip the occupied basket and left-click to release the animal wherever you want it.

Getting rawhide cord: To craft the lasso you need rawhide_cord. Kill a passive animal (Rabbit, Deer, Cow) by equipping your stone flake and left-clicking it. Pick up the carcass with F, take it to a butchering block, and press F to get hide. Then hand-craft: hide + stone_flake → rawhide_cord.

Alternative -- snares: Rather than chasing animals, set a passive snare. Craft a snare_simple (rope + 2 forest_twigs_fine, hand grid) and drop it near an animal trail. Small game will walk into it and you'll find a carcass when you come back.

Tip · Rope lasso is single-use -- it's consumed on a successful catch. Bring a few if you plan to move a group.

Tip · For medium animals (Cow, Sheep, Deer, Red Fox) you need a wooden cage (2 wood + 1 rope + 1 rawhide cord). Same mechanic.

Tip · Wolves and bears can't be lassooed. Craft a wolf lure (cooked meat + rope) or bear lure (2 safe berries + rope) instead -- place it and step back. The animal arrives on its own, then you deal with it however you like.

docs.title: Why bother?
docs.text:
Lasso-and-basket is a soft version of ranching. You're not taming the animals -- they're still wild when you release them. But being able to relocate them means you can consolidate livestock near camp, seed a pond with birds, or just move a cow somewhere convenient before the mid-game farms come online.

The wooden cage opens up deer relocation, which feeds into leather supply chains before you get consistent hunting going.

docs.title: Snare vs lasso
docs.text: Snares are for food. Lassos are for relocation. Both require rawhide cord, but snares are lighter to set up and let you trap passively while you do other things. A few snares set before bed means meat in the morning without a hunt.

docs.title: First Cast
docs.text:
Still water holds food.

Craft a basic fishing rod at your camp marker and try your luck near a river or pond. It takes patience -- the float bobs, then dips. Press F when it dips.

How it works:

  1. Craft a fishing rod at the camp marker (2 dry sticks + 3 plant fibers + 1 stone knife flake).
  2. Stand within 14 studs of water. Right-click to cast.
  3. Wait for the float to dip -- then press F to reel in.

Tip · The bite window is short (~1.8 s). Watch the timing bar and don't wait too long.

Tip · Missed the window? Right-click to cast again. Rod has limited durability -- around 20 catches.

docs.title: Fishing is patience, not luck.
docs.text:
The float dips when the fish strikes. React within the window or the fish spits the hook.

A good riverside camp near the water means you can fish between other tasks without burning much time.

docs.title: Get some calories in
docs.text:
Go find food. Cook some of it.

Different shrubs and trees drop different fruits and nuts -- blueberry, blackberry, hawthorn, sea buckthorn, juniper, rowan; hazelnut, acorn, nut bundles. Any pair of the same kind sorts at hand into one safe-edible portion (R-key the berry to see the recipe). Cooked food beats raw food.

docs.title: Don't eat it all immediately
docs.text:
If you're always running at zero food, every other task gets harder.

Cook a small batch, eat one, keep the rest. Running a surplus sounds boring -- it isn't, once you see what it unlocks.

Tip · The world has an assortment of fruits and nuts, not just one generic kind. Pick whichever your area happens to grow -- they all funnel into the same food_berries_safe / food_nuts_safe output.

docs.title: Dirt Under Your Nails
docs.text:
Foraging works today. Next week it won't.

Gathering seeds now means you hit Tier 0.5 with something ready to plant.

Getting seeds:

Flowers and spent grass heads drop forest_seed_mix_dry when you press F. Take 3 handfuls to your camp marker and sort them into crop seeds (recipe: "sort seed mix" in the camp marker's crafting list). This gives flax, turnip, AND carrot seeds in one pass.

The full farming loop (needs hoe, available in Tier 0.5):

  1. Equip a hoe and swing it at open ground (grass or dirt) to till a patch.
  2. Walk up to the tilled soil with seeds in your inventory and press F to plant.
  3. Wait for the sprout to change colour -- it's ripe when it glows bright.
  4. Press F to harvest: you get crop + a seed back, so the supply sustains itself.

Tip · Turnip is the staple starter crop -- fast and hardy. Carrot is a bit slower but useful for variety.

docs.title: Why bother with farming?
docs.text:
Hunting and foraging are unreliable and biome-dependent. Farming gives you a controlled, renewable food loop that works regardless of how far you've explored.

One tilled row of turnips takes less time per calorie than any hunting route.

Starting the seed collection now means you hit Tier 0.5 with something to plant immediately rather than having to backtrack for seeds after you've built the hoe.

docs.title: Keep What You Find
docs.text:
Your pack fills fast. A bark storage crate holds 27 stacks and keeps its contents between sessions -- everything you place in it stays there when you log off.

To craft one, open your camp marker and queue a bark storage crate: - 8 × forest bark strip - 2 × branch bundle - 2 × rope

The camp marker shows a progress bar while it builds (~50 s). When done, place the crate near your bedroll.

To use it, left-click the crate to open it. Shift+click moves items between your inventory and the crate instantly.

docs.title: Tier 1 — Stone
docs.text: [← Survival](0-survival.md)  ·  [Metal Ages →](2-metal_ages.md)

docs.title: Tier 1 — Stone
docs.text:
Camp industry. Build the first heat you control — kilns turn wood into charcoal and clay into ceramics.

1 Tier 1 — Stone Unlocks after: A stable camp and a cutting edge.→Leads to: Kilns, fired clay, charcoal, quicklime.

16 quests · 14 on the win-path · 1 branches

Briefing Background Win-path

Unlocks after: Camp Industry, Without Metal

Do this: - retrieve Have or place a food storage pit in camp. yields: FSfood storage pit×1 - retrieve Have a small buffer of safe foods ready. yields: SBsafe berries×10 SNsafe nuts×5 - produce Cook a couple simple rations at the hearth.

Do this: - retrieve Have the full basic stone toolkit available (crafted or obtained). yields: H(hammerstone (hafted)×1 SFstone flake×1 HShafted stone knife×1 GSground stone axe×1

Background Win-path

Unlocks after: Basic Stone Toolkit

Do this: - retrieve Have seeds ready (carrot or turnip -- sort seed mix at the camp marker if needed). yields: CScarrot seed×2 TSturnip seed×2 - retrieve Harvest your first crop. yields: WCwild carrot×2 - retrieve Optional: also harvest turnips for a mixed food buffer. optional yields: TUturnip×2

Rewards: CScarrot seed×6 TSturnip seed×6

Background Win-path

Unlocks after: Food Buffer (Tomorrow Isn't Luck)

Do this: - retrieve Have or place a butchering block and a drying rack. yields: DRdrying rack (survival)×1 BBbutchering block (basic)×1 - retrieve Have some rawhide cord (butcher an animal, put the hide on the drying rack). yields: RCrawhide cord×2 - retrieve Optional: also have some raw venison ready to cook. optional yields: RVraw venison×2

Rewards: RCrawhide cord×4 DRdrying rack (survival)×1

Background Checkpoint

Unlocks after: Food Buffer (Tomorrow Isn't Luck), Camp Zones + Sanitation, Basic Stone Toolkit

Rewards: PPPioneer's Pouch×1

Background Exchange

Unlocks after: Tier 1 -- Stone Camp Online

Exchange Tier I survival tokens for Tier II industry tokens. Repeatable.

Do this: - do

Costs: STSurvival Token I×5

Rewards: ITIndustry Token II×1

docs.title: Stone camp -- routine, buffers, and controlled heat
docs.text:
Still no metal. That's fine -- this tier is about turning your scrappy survival setup into something that actually runs:

  • cordage + basic tools
  • a controlled hearth with real fuel discipline
  • charcoal for reliable high heat
  • clay containers fired into proper ceramics
  • dry storage and grain grinding
  • food buffers + basic sanitation

Tip · If your camp runs on purpose, Tier 2 can start asking for real metallurgy.

docs.title: What changes between Tier 0 and Tier 1
docs.text:
Tier 0 proves you can survive a night.

Tier 1 proves you can stay stable while doing actual work: - fewer emergency trips - more stuff in reserve - better heat control - tools that save time

That's the bridge to metallurgy.

docs.title: Formalize the marker (Camp Utility I)
docs.text:
Reinforce the marker spot with a small kit: rope, bundled branches, stones.

Not magic -- you're just establishing "this is where tools and materials live." That one habit saves more time than any single tool.

Tip · Branch bundle: gather sturdy branches or forest twigs, then bind them with rope or plant fiber. Hover branch_bundle in your inventory and press R to see the exact recipe.

docs.title: What this is (historically)
docs.text:
Not an upgrade -- just a fixed place where tools and habits live: - a known working surface - ties always on hand - materials kept where you use them

Tip · Quest consumes the kit to represent building/reinforcing the station at camp.

docs.title: Fuel discipline (dry buffer)
docs.text:
You will need dry wood constantly. Get ahead of it now.

Build a rack, keep it stocked. Rainy days don't get to delete your progress if you have a buffer.

docs.title: The point
docs.text:
You are not hoarding. You are buying reliability.

Tip · A buffer also lets you focus on building things instead of constantly re-gathering fuel.

docs.title: Charcoal clamp (predictable heat)
docs.text:
Charcoal is wood transformed into portable, reliable heat -- it burns hotter and steadier than raw wood.

Build an earth-mound clamp and run a first batch. This isn't smelting fuel yet -- it's just "better heat for the kiln."

Load it. Start it. Walk away. It doesn't need you standing there.

docs.title: Why charcoal first
docs.text:
You can fire ceramics more reliably with charcoal than raw wood -- hotter, steadier, more controllable.

Making charcoal takes time and a lot of wood. That's intentional. It keeps things moving at a realistic pace.

docs.title: Clay work (greenware)
docs.text:
Gather workable clay and shape some pots.

Unfired clay is fragile -- it's called greenware, and yes, it will break if it gets wet. This step is about forming, not firing. The kiln comes next.

Tip · Where to get clay: at the camp marker, craft e1_wash_dirt_for_clay -- 4× dirt + 1× bark_bucket_water + 1× plant_fibers -> 1× clay. The dirt is mined from any grass/soil terrain; the plant fibers act as a sieve to settle the heavy clay fraction out of the dirt slurry. Slow and lossy on purpose -- real clay-from-soil yields ~25%. Hover clay and press R to see the recipe.

Tip · Hover clay_pot and press R to see the shaping recipe (4 clay -> 1 pot at the camp marker).

docs.title: Honest early ceramics
docs.text:
The earliest pots were hand-coiled, sun-dried, and fragile. People used them anyway -- even a leaky clay bowl beats carrying water in cupped hands.

Firing came later, once someone noticed that pots left near a fire got harder.

The dirt-wash method (elutriation) is how clay was actually separated from soil for thousands of years before commercial clay deposits were exploited. The water + fiber sieve is older than agriculture.

docs.title: Fire clay into ceramics
docs.text:
Build a simple earthen kiln and run your first firing.

Unfired clay is mud with ambition. Fired clay is actually durable. This is the difference.

Load it. Start it. Walk away. It doesn't need you standing there.

docs.title: What firing changes
docs.text:
Unfired clay hates water and cracks from impacts. Fired clay survives both.

Once ceramics exist, storage and cooking stop being constant emergencies.

docs.title: Containers online
docs.text:
A container at camp means one fewer trip per day. Stack enough and suddenly you're not running back for water every hour.

Keep a water bucket at camp and some fired vessels for storage and carry.

docs.title: What changes
docs.text: When you can carry and store reliably: - you stop re-fetching basics constantly - you can buffer food and water - your hands are free to actually do work

docs.title: Dry storage (pit cache)
docs.text:
Grain you gathered last week can still feed you today -- if you stored it right.

Get a starter batch and put it somewhere covered and dry.

docs.title: Reality
docs.text: Grain and seeds store well when kept dry and away from moisture. The cache is the invention: it makes time and distance cheaper.

docs.title: Grinding (saddle quern)
docs.text:
Stored grain is potential. Ground grain is food you can actually cook with.

Get a saddle quern and grind a starter batch of meal and flour. It's one of the oldest labor tools in human history -- probably for good reason.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text:
Meal for porridge and simple breads. Flour for better recipes later.

The quern is a genuine bottleneck early on -- which is the point.

docs.title: Food buffer (tomorrow isn't luck)
docs.text:
Put food somewhere on purpose and leave it there.

A bad day shouldn't erase a week of progress. Stock enough to cover a couple rough days.

docs.title: Buffer definition
docs.text:
Not endless stockpiles -- just enough that a single bad day doesn't delete progress.

Tip · This is also where spoilage mechanics can come into play later.

docs.title: Camp zones + sanitation
docs.text:
Keep sparks away from bedding. Keep waste away from everything. Keep food storage separate from both.

That's it. That's the whole quest. It's boring, it's historically accurate, and it genuinely matters.

docs.title: Why this is historically honest
docs.text:
People learn quickly: - don't sleep next to sparks - don't store food next to waste - don't let the whole camp become a trash smear

A midden is primitive sanitation -- unglamorous, but real.

docs.title: Basic stone toolkit
docs.text:
Build the set of tools you'll reach for every day:

  • Hammerstone -- strikes, cracks, and flakes
  • Stone flake -- cuts and scrapes
  • Hafted knife -- more control than a bare flake, safer too
  • Ground axe -- actual wood processing without suffering

You've been improvising. Stop that.

Tip · Two stone types, two paths: smooth stone_river_rounded drops from rocks (and is the preferred form for knapping flakes). Rougher generic stone also drops from rocks, OR you can hand-cleave a river stone into chunks (t0_hand_cleave_stone, 1:1). The ground axe and quern saddle recipes ask for plain stone; the hammerstone and knife flake recipes use stone_river_rounded. Hover each tool and press R to see which form its recipe wants.

docs.title: Tool roles
docs.text:
• Hammerstone: percussion, shaping, cracking
  • Flake: cutting and scraping
  • Hafted blade: stronger, controllable, safer grip
  • Ground axe: wood processing at a reasonable pace

The "river-rounded vs cleaved chunk" distinction mirrors real stone-age toolmaking: tumbled cobbles knap predictably, rougher fragments are better stock for grinding because they hold a flat face when worked.

docs.title: First Furrow
docs.text:
You have seeds from the survival chapter. Now actually use them.

The hoe is in your stone toolkit. Equip it and swing at any grass or dirt terrain to till a patch. Walk up to the tilled soil with seeds in your inventory and press F to plant.

Crops grow over time. Come back when the color changes to bright -- press F to harvest. You get the crop plus a seed back, so the loop sustains itself.

Recommended start: plant turnips first (fastest growth), then carrot once you have the hang of it.

Tip · Crops will be eaten by nearby animals if there's no scarecrow. Craft one from 2 forest twigs + 2 bark strips at the hand grid and place it within ~20 studs.

Tip · Farm plots are excluded from the erosion system -- tilled soil won't revert while seeds are planted.

docs.title: Why farm?
docs.text:
Hunting and foraging work, but they're unreliable and distance-dependent. A farm works while you're doing other things.

Two rows of turnips takes less time per calorie than any hunting route, doesn't require knowing biomes, and produces seeds so the supply is self-sustaining from the first harvest.

Once farming is running, food becomes a background resource rather than a daily emergency.

docs.title: Meat on the Rack
docs.text:
Foraging covers calories. Hunting covers protein, hide, and tools.

Set up a butchering block and a drying rack. These two stations close the hunting loop: kill an animal → butcher for carcass → drying rack for rawhide cord, dried meat, or hides.

How to hunt: Equip a stone flake (or any tool) from your hotbar. Walk up to a passive animal and left-click to strike. Collect the carcass with F.

At the butchering block (press F with carcass): - Meat → cook at campfire for food - Hide → drying rack for rawhide cord (strong lashing; better than rope for some items) - Bones → tools, meal, or decoration

At the drying rack: - Raw hide → rawhide cord (drops in a few in-game hours) - Raw meat → dried meat (preserves without a campfire)

docs.title: Why rawhide?
docs.text:
Rawhide cord is stronger than twisted plant fiber rope for some applications. It's also how the bedroll_basic (the better bedroll) gets made, and how the iron-age wire-drawing bench gets lashed together later.

Every hide you dry is a tool component you don't have to go looking for later.

docs.title: Tier 1 complete -- Stone camp online
docs.text:
Your camp actually runs now:

  • marker confirmed and reinforced
  • controlled hearth, sorted fuel
  • dry fuel buffer stocked
  • charcoal production running
  • kiln firing ceramics
  • water carried, vessels for storage
  • grain ground and buffered
  • food stored and a buffer ahead of you
  • zones laid out, sanitation handled
  • stone toolkit complete

Next: Tier 2 can ask for real metallurgy and heavy heat work without your camp falling apart while you're busy.

docs.title: The line this draws
docs.text:
From here forward, the game can assume you have: - buffers (fuel, food, water) - containers - controlled heat - basic grain processing - a readable camp layout - tools that save labor

That's enough stability to start real industry.

docs.title: Tier 2 — Metal Ages
docs.text: [← Stone](1-stone.md)  ·  [Steam →](3-steam.md)

docs.title: Tier 2 — Metal Ages
docs.text:
Charcoal smelting. Pull the first metal out of rock and learn the alloy that named an age.

2 Tier 2 — Metal Ages Unlocks after: Charcoal and crucibles.→Leads to: Copper, bronze, wrought iron.

13 quests · 12 on the win-path · 0 branches

Briefing Background Win-path

Unlocks after: The Metal Ages

Do this: - retrieve Mine native copper (already visible -- no survey needed). yields: RNraw native copper×20 - produce Hammer native copper into workable nuggets at a stone anvil.

Rewards: HChammered copper nugget×4 RNraw native copper×10

Background Win-path

Unlocks after: Cold-Hammered Copper

Do this: - produce Craft clay crucibles for holding a metal charge. - produce Carve a stone ingot mold. - produce Smelt native copper in the crucible and cast copper ingots.

Rewards: CIcopper ingot×4 SCsmall clay crucible×1

Background Win-path

Unlocks after: Crucible Smelting

Do this: - retrieve Stockpile copper ingots for toolmaking and alloying. yields: CIcopper ingot×6

Rewards: CIcopper ingot×6 SIstone ingot mold×1

Background Win-path

Unlocks after: Copper Tools

Do this: - retrieve Collect cassiterite ore from stream gravels. yields: RCraw cassiterite ore×12

Rewards: RCraw cassiterite ore×8

Background Win-path

Unlocks after: Find the Tin

Do this: - produce Smelt cassiterite into tin ingots in the crucible.

Rewards: TItin ingot×4

Background Win-path

Unlocks after: Smelt Tin

Do this: - produce Alloy copper and tin to produce bronze ingots. - produce Forge a bronze pickaxe (unlocks Tier 2 mining).

Rewards: BIbronze ingot×4 BPbronze pickaxe×1

Background Win-path

Unlocks after: Copper Tools

Do this: - retrieve Gather bog iron from wetland deposits. yields: RBraw bog iron×30

Rewards: RBraw bog iron×20

Background Win-path

Unlocks after: Bog Iron

Do this: - retrieve Build and place a roasting pit. yields: ORore roasting pit×1 - produce Roast bog iron to prepare it for the bloomery.

Rewards: RBroasted bog iron×6

Background Win-path

Unlocks after: Roasting Pit

Do this: - retrieve Build and place a clay bloomery furnace. yields: CBclay bloomery furnace×1 - retrieve Have bellows ready for forced air. yields: BEbellows×1 - retrieve Stock charcoal for the bloomery charge. yields: CHcharcoal×20

Rewards: CHcharcoal×20

Background Win-path

Unlocks after: Build a Bloomery

Do this: - produce Run the bloomery and extract iron blooms.

Rewards: IBiron bloom×2

Background Win-path

Unlocks after: Extract the Bloom

Do this: - retrieve Build and place a forge hearth. yields: FHforge hearth×1 - produce Hammer blooms into wrought iron bars at the forge. - produce Forge an iron hammer head and haft it.

Rewards: WIwrought iron bar×4 IHiron hammer×1

Background Checkpoint

Unlocks after: Cast Bronze, Forge Wrought Iron

Rewards: PPPioneer's Pouch×1

Background

docs.title: The Metal Ages
docs.text:
You have fire, clay, and a kiln that reaches over 1,000 degrees. You have everything you need to work metal.

Three metals. Three ages. Each one harder to make and more useful than the last.

  • Copper: hammer it cold, then learn to melt it
  • Bronze: find tin, smelt it, alloy it with copper
  • Iron: gather bog iron, build a bloomery, hammer out wrought iron

The Steam chapter will ask you for a lot of iron. This is where you learn to make it.

Tip · Use the recipe browser throughout this chapter. Quest tasks show what to produce but not the exact ingredients. Hover any item and press R to see its recipe, or press U to see what uses it. Every process in this chapter is discoverable that way -- you don't need to guess.

Tip · Both paths reward you equally. You must complete the Bronze path (bronze pickaxe) AND the Iron path (iron hammer) to reach the checkpoint. Both tools unlock Tier 2 ore mining -- coal, hematite, cassiterite in harder deposits. You will need them for the Steam chapter.

docs.title: What changes here
docs.text: You stop finding materials and start producing them. That distinction -- found vs. produced -- is the conceptual leap behind all of metallurgy.

docs.title: Cold-hammered copper
docs.text:
Native copper doesn't need a furnace. It needs a hammerstone and patience.

Cold hammering work-hardens copper -- each strike makes it stronger, but eventually it becomes brittle. Anneal it in your campfire to restore workability, then hammer again.

This is the Copper Age. It predates smelting by thousands of years.

docs.title: Annealing
docs.text: Heating metal and letting it cool slowly was discovered before smelting. Copperworkers noticed that fire softened their work-hardened pieces. This observation -- that heat changes metal -- is the conceptual leap that eventually leads to all of metallurgy.

docs.title: Crucible smelting
docs.text:
Your kiln can reach 1,100 °C. Copper melts at 1,085 °C.

You already have everything you need.

Build a clay crucible. Load it with native copper. Fire it in the kiln. Pour the liquid copper into a stone mold.

docs.title: This is the moment your game changes
docs.text: You can now produce a material that is liquid and can be shaped. From here, every metal follows the same logic: heat, reduce, cast. The only variables are temperature requirements and reduction chemistry.

docs.title: Copper tools
docs.text:
Cast copper is soft but workable. Shape it into simple tools -- awls, chisels, small blades.

Copper tools wear quickly compared to stone, but they can be re-cast when they break. Stone can't.

That's the real advantage: repairability.

docs.title: Why copper matters
docs.text:
Copper is not better than stone for cutting. It's better than stone for remaking.

A broken flint blade is waste. A broken copper awl goes back in the crucible.

docs.title: Find the tin
docs.text:
Bronze isn't copper. It's copper + tin.

Tin comes from cassiterite -- a heavy, dark pebble found in stream gravels. It looks like nothing. It changes everything.

Look for dark, dense pebbles in stream deposits. Cassiterite is visible to the naked eye if you know what you're looking for.

docs.title: Why tin matters
docs.text: Tin occurs in relatively few places, unlike common copper. Bronze Age "globalization" happened because of this single alloy requirement. Trade routes formed specifically to supply tin to copper-producing regions.

docs.title: Smelt tin
docs.text:
Tin melts at 232 °C -- well within campfire range, far below your kiln's capacity.

Load cassiterite into a crucible with charcoal. The carbon reduces the tin oxide to metallic tin. Pour it into your stone mold.

Tin by itself is too soft for tools. But mixed with copper...

docs.title: The ratio
docs.text: Bronze is approximately 90% copper, 10% tin. Too much tin makes it brittle. Too little and you haven't improved on pure copper.

docs.title: Cast bronze
docs.text:
Melt copper and tin together in a crucible. The ratio matters: roughly 5 copper to 1 tin for gameplay.

Pour into molds. Cool. You have bronze.

Bronze is harder than copper, holds an edge better, and casts more precisely. It's the first engineered material -- an alloy designed for performance.

docs.title: What bronze unlocks
docs.text:
The bronze pickaxe unlocks Tier 2 ore mining -- hematite, magnetite, and sulfide ores that stone and copper tools can't touch.

You now have a reason to go deeper.

docs.title: Bog iron
docs.text:
Bog iron forms in wetland areas where iron-bearing groundwater meets organic acids.

It's impure, spongy, and weak compared to later iron. But it's everywhere. A village smith can collect it without mining equipment.

Look for orange-brown precipitate at the margins of standing water. Bog iron is auto-surveyed -- you can see it without a geology hammer.

docs.title: Why iron matters
docs.text: Iron broke the palace monopolies on metallurgy that bronze had created. Before bog iron, having metal tools required either living near a mine or being part of a trade network. After bog iron, any community with a wetland could make iron.

docs.title: Roasting pit
docs.text:
Raw bog iron is full of moisture and volatile impurities. Roasting drives these off before the bloomery step.

Build a simple roasting pit -- a shallow fire bed with ore layered on top. The heat doesn't melt anything. It just dries and pre-treats the ore so the bloomery can reduce it more efficiently.

docs.title: Not optional
docs.text: Without roasting, your bloomery will consume more charcoal and produce a smaller, slag-heavy bloom. Roasting is where "I found some ore" becomes "I prepared a charge."

docs.title: Build a bloomery
docs.text:
A bloomery is a shaft furnace -- not a kiln.

The key difference: you force air through the base using bellows. This gets the temperature high enough to reduce iron oxide to metallic iron, even though it's not hot enough to melt iron.

Build the shaft from clay and stone. Add a tuyere (air inlet). Connect bellows. Prepare charcoal.

docs.title: What the bloomery does
docs.text: Carbon monoxide from burning charcoal strips oxygen from the iron ore. The iron collects as a spongy mass called a bloom. It never melts. It just... consolidates.

docs.title: Extract the bloom
docs.text:
Charge the bloomery: roasted ore and charcoal in alternating layers. Work the bellows. The reduction reaction takes time.

The result is a spongy mass of iron and slag -- a bloom. It is NOT liquid iron. It never melts. It just consolidates.

Pull the bloom out while it's still red-hot. Work fast.

docs.title: The bloom isn't finished iron
docs.text: It's iron contaminated with slag. The hammering is what removes the slag and aligns the iron grain structure. Without hammering, the bloom is useless.

docs.title: Forge wrought iron
docs.text:
The bloom needs to be worked.

Reheat it to orange-red. Strike it with a heavy hammer. The slag squirts out as sparks and scale. Fold it. Reheat. Repeat.

This process is called consolidation. What you're making is wrought iron -- the same material every pre-industrial metalwork is built from.

docs.title: The payoff
docs.text: The iron hammer unlocks Tier 2 mining alongside the bronze pickaxe. Both the Bronze and Iron paths open the same deeper ores -- historically accurate, and it gives you two routes to the Steam chapter.

docs.title: The Metal Ages complete
docs.text:
Both paths required. Bronze AND iron. Check your quest map -- one of them isn't done.

You now understand metal.

Not as a found object -- as a produced material.

Copper from native deposits. Bronze from two ores combined in a specific ratio. Iron from a reduction furnace that took all day to run. Each one is harder to make and more useful than the last.

The Steam chapter is going to ask you for a lot of iron. You know how to make it.

docs.title: What you've learned
docs.text:
• Metal can be shaped by hammering, melting, or both
  • Alloying (bronze) creates materials better than either component
  • Reduction (bloomery) extracts metal from ore using carbon chemistry
  • Every metal process is: ore + heat + chemistry = metal + waste

The Steam tier builds on all of this.

docs.title: Tier 3 — Steam
docs.text: [← Metal Ages](2-metal_ages.md)  ·  [Steel & DC →](4-steel.md)

docs.title: Tier 3 — Steam
docs.text:
Mechanical power. Coke gives you furnace-grade heat; steam gives you work that never gets tired.

3 Tier 3 — Steam Unlocks after: Bronze and wrought iron tools.→Leads to: Coke, coal tar, mechanical power.

11 quests · 9 on the win-path · 1 branches

docs.title: Tier 2 is where "site" starts to matter
docs.text:
You are no longer just a camp with better tools. You are a works:

  • waterpower with line-shaft distribution
  • coal access (the fuel that breaks woodland limits)
  • coke production for ironworks-grade heat
  • a boiler house with a real steam loop
  • your first serious steam use is mine drainage (pumping), then expanded into rotary shop drive

Tip · You need a bronze pickaxe or iron hammer (from the Metal Ages chapter) to mine coal and hematite. Make sure you have one before tackling coal access.

docs.title: Keep this tier honest
docs.text: Steam starts as stationary power. Not trains. Not magic torque. Pumps first. Rotation second.

docs.title: Put a stream to work
docs.text:
Build an overshot wheel where you have usable head and couple it to a short shaft.

Waterpower is old -- and that's the point: it's reliable when the site supports it.

docs.title: What "good placement" means
docs.text:
• you need fall (head), not just flow
  • you need a stable channel intake
  • you need a safe spot for the rotating assembly

You could have built a wooden wheel centuries ago. You couldn't have built the iron shaft, stone millrace, and timber gearing that make it actually useful. Now you can.

docs.title: Line-shaft workshop bay
docs.text:
Extend shafting and use belts + sheaves to distribute power to tools.

This is the "factory floor" pattern: one prime mover → many driven stations.

docs.title: What this unlocks
docs.text:
You stop asking: "can I power this tool?"

You start asking: "how many tools can this shaft feed?"

docs.title: Open the coal measures
docs.text:
Find and work a coal seam until you have a steady supply flowing to your works.

Coal changes scale: it's why steam becomes practical on sites that outgrow woodland fuel.

docs.title: Reality check
docs.text: Mines flood. That problem is why early steam engines get built at all.

docs.title: Make coke for industrial heat
docs.text:
Convert coal into coke: hotter, cleaner-burning, and suited for ironworks-scale firing.

This is the fuel bridge between "some coal" and "serious heat on demand".

Tip · Don't skip the coal buffer. Producing 40 coke consumes roughly 80 coal, and your blast furnace (built in the Steel chapter, but usable now) will need even more. The 80-coal stock task below is not optional -- treat it as the minimum working supply.

docs.title: About the oven form
docs.text: Early coke can be made in clamps/heaps; beehive ovens are a later high-throughput form. Functionally, the point is the same: controlled off-gassing to turn coal into coke.

docs.title: Coal ratio reality
docs.text: The coal→coke conversion is roughly 2:1 by mass in a beehive oven. 40 coke costs you ~80 coal. When the blast furnace is running it will consume coke continuously -- keep your coal supply well ahead of your coke demand.

docs.title: Low-pressure boiler house
docs.text:
Assemble a conservative stationary boiler plant: shell, firebox, pipes, feedwater loop, condensation/cooling hardware, and basic safety.

This is low-pressure practice: enough to run early engines, not a racing locomotive.

Tip · The cast-iron firebox and cylinder both require pig iron. Pig iron needs a small blast furnace (refractory bricks + coke + limestone → blast_furnace_small). Build that first -- hover blast_furnace_small and press R for the recipe.

Tip · Limestone gotcha: mining limestone nodes yields raw_ore_limestone (the geologic ore form). The blast furnace recipe expects limestone_rock (discrete nodules). Convert at hand with raw_ore_limestone -> limestone_rock (1:1, free). Same trick applies to most "raw_ore_X" + "X_rock" pairs.

docs.title: Non-negotiables
docs.text:
• you must control pressure (relief)
  • you must control water level (don't expose iron to direct fire dry)
  • you must move water back in (feedwater loop)

docs.title: Pig iron in the Steam era
docs.text: Early boiler makers used small cupola-style furnaces long before the large Bessemer converters of the Steel age. Your blast_furnace_small recipe reflects this -- it is buildable now, not gated behind the Steel chapter. Build it, charge it with coke + hematite + limestone, and tap enough pig iron to cast the firebox and cylinder.

docs.title: Why this gate exists
docs.text: Safe pressure vessels require repeatable metallurgy, tight machining tolerances, and standardized fasteners. If you can't make consistent plates, rivets, and seals, you don't get steam.

docs.title: Atmospheric Pump for a Wet Shaft
Atmospheric pump for a wet shaft
docs.text:
Put steam to work where it historically earns its keep first: mine drainage.

Build a simple pumping engine set (atmospheric-style behavior, condensation-driven cycle) and show it can keep a wet shaft workable.

docs.title: What makes it "early steam"
docs.text: It's not about high pressure. It's about cycling steam and condensation to drive a piston and pump rod reliably.

docs.title: Turn steam into rotation
docs.text:
Upgrade from "pumps only" to general drive: add flywheel, rotary conversion, speed regulation, and belt/shaft outputs.

Now the steam plant can drive the same shop logic as waterpower: shafts, belts, and stations.

docs.title: What changes
docs.text:
• flywheel smooths cyclic power
  • rotary conversion enables continuous drives
  • governor prevents runaway and stabilizes machine work

docs.title: Mechanised stamp milling
docs.text:
Connect your powertrain to an ore crushing station. Cams lift and drop stamps to break raw ore into fine feed for furnaces.

Route hematite from your ore supply into the mill input. Crushed ore flows out the other side.

This is throughput -- not glamour.

docs.title: Why this sits here
docs.text:
Your ironwork stops being "small batch heroic" and becomes "repeatable processing".

That is the real steam-era shift.

docs.title: Tier 2 complete -- Steam Age online
docs.text:
You have a real early-industrial works package:

  • waterwheel + line-shaft workshop distribution
  • coal access as a sustained fuel supply
  • coke production as an industrial heat chain
  • a conservative boiler house + steam loop discipline
  • steam justified first by mine drainage, then extended into rotary shop power
  • mechanised ore crushing throughput (stamp milling)

Next: Steel takes this into high steam and DC power.

docs.title: The line this draws
docs.text:
From here forward, the game can assume: - prime movers exist (water + steam) - fuel is industrial (coal + coke chain) - power distribution exists (shafts/belts now; wires later) - ore prep is mechanised

That's the on-ramp to high-pressure steam and electrical systems.

docs.title: Tier 4 — Steel & DC
docs.text: [← Steam](3-steam.md)  ·  [Electric →](5-electric.md)

docs.title: Tier 4 — Steel & DC
docs.text:
The blast furnace and the first electricity. Steel structure, then a dynamo to spin it all.

4 Tier 4 — Steel & DC Unlocks after: Coke and steam power.→Leads to: Steel, shapes, DC dynamo, motors.

12 quests · 10 on the win-path · 1 branches

Briefing Win-path

docs.title: From Steam Ironworks to Steel & Power
From Steam Ironworks to Steel & Power
docs.text:
Your steam plant has proven the principle: controlled heat, moving pressure, turning shafts.

Tier 3 asks you to scale all of it.

  • Blast furnace replaces your bloomery and wrought-iron forge with coke-fired pig iron production
  • Bessemer and open-hearth converters turn pig iron into steel -- harder, tougher, weldable
  • Rolled products become structural material for bridges, ships, and boiler shells
  • High-pressure boilers push your engines far beyond Tier 2 limits
  • Compound engines do more work with less coal
  • Dynamos are steam turned to electricity
  • DC networks wire your site for the first time
  • Motors put power exactly where you need it, not just where a shaft happens to run

This is the tier where your works stop being a collection of machines and start being a site.

docs.title: Coke blast furnace
docs.text:
Your bloomery reduced iron without melting it. This is different.

A coke blast furnace runs hot enough to produce liquid pig iron -- iron with 3–4% dissolved carbon, poured from a tap hole at the base. It's not wrought iron. It's brittle cast metal that feeds the converters.

The hot-blast stove preheats the incoming air, cutting coke consumption by 30–40% compared to cold-blast operation. It's one of the most important efficiency upgrades in 19th-century metallurgy.

Load ore, coke, and limestone flux in alternating layers from the top. Tap pig iron from the bottom. The furnace runs continuously -- never cools.

Tip · Plan ahead: this chapter needs ~200+ pig iron total. This quest proves the furnace works (40 pig iron). The Bessemer quest that follows consumes 80, and the open-hearth quest consumes another 80. Keep your blast furnace running between quests and build a stockpile before you start converting.

docs.title: Bessemer converter: fast bulk steel
docs.text:
Pig iron is brittle because it contains too much carbon. The Bessemer process fixes this in minutes, not hours.

Load the converter with molten pig iron. Tilt it upright. Blast cold air through the tuyeres at the base. The air burns out excess carbon -- the heat is generated by the carbon combustion, not from external fuel. The heat is self-sustaining.

In 15–20 minutes: bulk steel.

Bessemer is fast and cheap. It's not precise -- composition control is poor. Use it for rails and structural shapes where exact carbon content matters less.

Tip · Pig iron demand for this chapter is high. This quest alone consumes 80 pig iron, and the open-hearth furnace quest that follows consumes another 80. Before you start, make sure your blast furnace can keep up -- tap and stockpile at least 200 pig iron before you begin converting. Falling short mid-quest is frustrating.

docs.title: Siemens–Martin open-hearth furnace
docs.text:
Bessemer is fast. Open-hearth is careful.

The furnace uses regenerators -- alternating chambers of heated brickwork that pre-heat combustion air by recycling exhaust heat. This lets the furnace reach temperatures high enough to fully melt the charge rather than just blowing air through it.

Full melting means you can add scrap, control the slag chemistry, and sample the heat repeatedly before tapping. The result: better composition control and higher-quality steel for pressure vessels and load-bearing structures.

Slower than Bessemer, but the furnace that built bridges, battleships, and boilers.

docs.title: Rails, beams, and boiler plate
docs.text:
Steel is only useful when it can be shaped into precise structural forms. A rolling mill squeezes hot steel between shaped rollers to produce uniform cross-sections: rails, I-beams, angles, flat plate.

Rolling replaces forging for bulk production. The shapes are consistent. The tolerances are tight enough to bolt together.

What this unlocks: - Steel rails: your transport network scales with them - Steel beams: buildings and bridges that don't rely on timber or cast iron - Boiler plate: steel pressure vessels for the next stage of engine upgrades - Gusset plates: structural joints that actually hold under dynamic load

The rolling mill is one of the loudest, most impressive machines in the works. It should look like it.

docs.title: Steel boilers: higher pressure
docs.text:
Your Tier 2 boilers ran on wrought iron plate at moderate pressure. That's a hard ceiling -- wrought iron fatigues at high pressure cycles.

Steel plate is stronger, more consistent, and can be rolled thicker. Riveted steel shells with proper stays can safely run at 2–3× the pressure your old boilers could handle.

Higher pressure means the same mass of steam does more work per stroke. Your engines are about to get significantly more powerful for the same fuel.

The weighted safety valve isn't cosmetic. Overpressure kills people and destroys sites.

docs.title: Compound engines: do more with the same steam
docs.text:
A simple engine uses steam once, then exhausts it at near-boiler pressure -- wasting most of its energy.

A compound engine uses steam in stages: a high-pressure cylinder takes the initial expansion, then exhausts into a larger low-pressure cylinder for a second expansion. Same boiler. Same coal. Significantly more shaft power.

The Corliss valve gear adds variable cut-off: steam admission stops partway through the stroke, letting expansion do more work before exhaust. This is the engine that made 19th-century mills competitive.

The flyball governor keeps shaft speed stable under varying load -- important for workshops, rolling mills, and eventually dynamos.

docs.title: Steam-driven dynamo house
docs.text:
Your engines turn shafts. A dynamo turns a shaft into direct current.

The principle: coils of wire rotating in a magnetic field induce voltage. Early dynamos use wrought iron cores for the field magnets and laminated armature cores to reduce eddy current losses.

Belt-couple a reliable engine to two dynamos in a dedicated bay. Wire the outputs to copper bus bars at the switchboard. The machine shop now has electrical capability.

This is the moment your site produces electricity. Everything that follows -- lights, motors, instruments -- is downstream of this.

docs.title: Low-voltage DC network and lights
docs.text:
Electricity is only useful when it goes somewhere.

Run insulated copper wire from your dynamo switchboard to lamp fixtures across the works. The circuit is simple: dynamo → switchboard → fuse → switch → lamp. The voltage drop over distance limits how far you can push a DC network -- this is not a long-distance transmission system, it's a local lighting and small-power grid.

The incandescent lamps use carbon filaments (1879-era technology). They're fragile, short-lived, and dim by later standards. But they are electric light. Your site can now operate after dark.

The wooden switchboard, knife switches, and rewireable fuses are early electrical safety discipline. They are not decorative.

docs.title: Motors on the shop floor
docs.text:
A DC motor is a dynamo run in reverse: send current through it and the shaft turns.

The first practical DC motors were installed in factories in the 1880s. They did not immediately replace line shafting -- the transition was decades-long and gradual. But they changed what was possible:

  • A machine tool could now run independently of the main shaft
  • A hoist could be placed exactly where lifting was needed
  • A department could be stopped for maintenance without halting the whole plant

Install DC motors on at least four key tools or hoists. Keep some shafting -- the transition is real, not instant. The remaining shafting will disappear in Tier 4 and beyond.

docs.title: Tier 3 online: steel and DC power
docs.text:
Your site is no longer a steam mill with iron parts.

It is a steel plant with electric light.

Confirm what's running: - Coke blast furnace producing pig iron continuously - Bessemer converter for bulk steel heats - Open-hearth furnace for controlled, high-quality steel - Rolling mill producing rails, beams, and boiler plate - High-pressure riveted steel boilers - Compound or mill engines with governors on the main shafts - DC dynamo house with a working switchboard - DC distribution network with lighting and fuses - DC motors installed on key machines and hoists

This is the mature 1880s–1890s heavy industrial site. Tier 4 adds AC, transformers, induction motors, vacuum tube electronics, and the first centralised control systems.

docs.title: Tier 5 — Electric
docs.text: [← Steel & DC](4-steel.md)  ·  [Solid State →](6-solidstate.md)

docs.title: Tier 5 — Electric
docs.text:
The chemistry gateway. An AC grid powers electrolysis: aluminum, chlor-alkali, ferroalloys, metallurgical silicon.

5 Tier 5 — Electric Unlocks after: Steel and a DC dynamo.→Leads to: AC grid, electrolysis, arc furnace — the chemistry gateway.

17 quests · 15 on the win-path · 1 branches

docs.title: Tier 4 is the "site becomes a power system"
docs.text:
Steel/DC got you: heavy plant, dynamos, local DC networks, and motors. Now you add what makes power go far and scale:

  • AC generation and switching practice
  • transformers (step up / step down)
  • three-phase transmission for distance + motors
  • rectification back to DC for old loads and heavy processes
  • early electronics (vacuum tubes) as the bridge into instrumentation + control
  • plant-wide meters, relays, alarms
  • early regulation and automatic control
  • a central control room that makes the plant readable

docs.title: Build a small AC test bay
docs.text:
Goal: a controlled place where you can safely energize circuits, switch loads, and prove you can run lamps without "mystery wiring".

This is where AC becomes a tool instead of a theory.

docs.title: Build a transformer yard
docs.text:
Place step-up near generation and step-down near loads. Run a short "high voltage" span between them using proper insulators.

This teaches the real AC advantage: distance without absurd copper loss.

docs.title: Switchgear and protection discipline
docs.text: Before you push power farther, you need safe isolation and protection: switches, fuses, bus bars, and insulating hardware.

docs.title: Upgrade to three-phase
docs.text:
Three-phase is the serious industrial standard: smooth rotating fields for motors, and copper-efficient transmission.

Bring up a three-phase alternator and a district AC network segment.

docs.title: Put motors where the work is
docs.text: Replace some shaft-only dependency with local induction motors. Not everything becomes electric overnight -- but critical tools stop being hostage to long line shafts.

docs.title: Make the plant readable and protected
docs.text:
Install meters, relays, and alarms so operators can see load and get warned before failures cascade.

Target at least: - one boiler house feed - one generator feeder - one outgoing line/load bay

docs.title: Feed DC loads from the AC plant
docs.text:
You already own DC infrastructure from Tier 3. Now you build the bridge: a rectifier bay that takes incoming AC and produces heavy DC.

This is historically honest: AC doesn't delete DC -- it feeds and extends it.

docs.title: Build the first electronics capability
docs.text:
Set up a glass + vacuum bench and produce simple diodes and triodes. You're not building a full radio stack here -- you're proving: - one-way conduction (diode) - controlled gain/switching behavior (triode)

This becomes a bridge into sensing, amplification, and control.

docs.title: Regulators and closed-loop control
docs.text:
Bring in early automatic regulation (pneumatic/analog controllers): - sensors read process value - regulators compare to a setpoint - control valves move steam or feedwater

This is the shift from "watching gauges" to supervising systems that hold steady on their own.

docs.title: Centralize supervision
docs.text:
Build a control room wall: - mimic diagram showing boilers, generators, transformers, feeders, and key loops - meters and alarms that pull plant state into one place

This is the shift from "walking the site" to "running the site".

docs.title: Tier 4 online
docs.text:
Confirm the full integration:

  • three-phase AC grid and distribution
  • induction motors on the floor
  • rectifier bay feeding heavy DC loads
  • vacuum tube electronics capability
  • plant-wide instrumentation and protection
  • regulators and automatic control
  • a control room that makes the whole site readable

Next tier can go solid-state (diodes/transistors), electronic control, and then digital.

docs.title: Trade In: Industry IV → Tech V
Electrolysis opens the chemical frontier
docs.text:
Your rectifier bay now outputs heavy DC. The next step: using that current to split matter into things that don't occur naturally in pure form.

  • Chlor-alkali cell (DiAPHRAGM or membrane): NaCl brine + DC → Cl2 + NaOH + H2
  • Downs cell (fused-salt): molten NaCl + DC → Na metal + Cl2
  • Müller-Kühne HF: CaF2 (fluorite) + H2SO4 → HF + CaSO4

These are the three master reagents for all Tier 4+ chemistry: Cl2 for chlorination, NaOH for leaching/digestion, HF for niobium/zirconium/fluorite processing.

docs.title: Electric arc furnace chemistry chain
docs.text:
The electric arc furnace does more than make steel: its extreme temperatures drive carbothermic and thermite reactions conventional furnaces cannot reach.

  • MG-Si (98% silicon): SiO2 + 2C → Si + 2CO at ~2000°C -- gateway to semiconductor silicon
  • Ferrochrome: chromite + coke → FeCr -- essential for stainless and superalloy grades
  • Ferronickel: pentlandite + coke + limestone → FeNi -- nickel source for stainless, Inconel
  • Stainless steel 18-8: Fe + FeCr + FeNi in argon-oxygen decarburization converter

docs.title: Smelt aluminium from bauxite
docs.text:
Aluminium is the most abundant structural metal on Earth but exists only as oxides locked in bauxite. Two electrochemical steps liberate it:

  1. Bayer process: NaOH dissolves bauxite → aluminium hydroxide → filter → calcine → Al2O3
  2. Hall-Heroult cell: Al2O3 dissolved in molten cryolite + DC + carbon anodes → liquid Al + CO2

Result: one of the lightest, most corrosion-proof structural metals. Essential for aircraft, vehicles, overhead power cables, and all future rocketry.

docs.title: Purify metals to industry-grade purity
docs.text:
Copper electrorefining: blister copper (~98.5%) cast as anodes, dissolved in CuSO4/H2SO4, and re-deposited onto stainless cathodes → 99.99% Cu. Precious metals (Ag, Au, Se) fall as valuable anode slime.

Nickel electrorefining (Sherritt Gordon process): pentlandite concentrate → ammoniacal leach → purification → electrodeposition → 99.9% Ni cathode.

Nickel cathode is the input for all downstream superalloy VIM melts: stainless, Inconel.

docs.title: Tier 6 — Solid State
docs.text: [← Electric](5-electric.md)  ·  [Space →](7-space.md)

docs.title: Tier 6 — Solid State
docs.text:
Semiconductors and automation. Grow a silicon crystal, slice wafers, and let machines run the line.

6 Tier 6 — Solid State Unlocks after: Industrial electricity and pure silicon.→Leads to: Polysilicon, wafers, ICs, automation.

13 quests · 11 on the win-path · 1 branches

Briefing

Introduces Tier 5 as the moment your heavy-electric plant learns solid-state electronics, programmable controllers, CNC and the first industrial robots.

Win-path

Unlocks after: Transistors, PLCs and Robots

Set up a small electronics bench to make basic silicon diodes, rectifier bridges and low-power transistors for switching and amplification.

Do this: - retrieve Have or place a glass electronics bench. yields: GBglass bench×1 - retrieve Process raw silicon wafers on the bench. yields: RSraw silicon wafer×8 - retrieve Produce diodes, rectifier bridges and transistors. yields: SSsmall silicon diode×16 SBsilicon bridge rectifier×4 SNsignal NPN transistor×16

Rewards: SSsmall silicon diode×8 SNsignal NPN transistor×8

Win-path

Unlocks after: Diodes, Rectifiers and Transistors

Assemble small logic modules that bundle transistors and diodes into reusable AND, OR, NOT and flip-flop building blocks for control circuits.

Do this: - retrieve Craft AND/OR logic modules from transistors and diodes. yields: ALAND/OR logic module×12 - retrieve Craft NOT gates and flip-flop modules. yields: NLNOT logic module×4 FFflip-flop logic module×4 - retrieve Have electromagnetic relays for hybrid control circuits. yields: ERelectromagnetic relay×8

Rewards: ALAND/OR logic module×6 ERelectromagnetic relay×4

Win-path

Unlocks after: Logic Gates and Flip-Flops

Build a basic programmable logic controller rack with a CPU and several I/O modules that can scan inputs and drive outputs in a fixed cycle.

Do this: - retrieve Have or place a PLC rack with CPU and I/O modules. yields: BPbasic PLC rack×1 PCPLC CPU module×1 PIPLC I/O module×4 - retrieve Install a switchboard panel with metering instruments. yields: WSwooden switchboard panel×1 PApanel ammeter×2 PVpanel voltmeter×2

Rewards: PIPLC I/O module×2

Win-path

Unlocks after: First PLC Rack

Use your PLC to fully automate at least one production line, handling start, stop, interlocks and alarms without manual switch juggling.

Do this: - retrieve Write and load program cartridges for the PLC. yields: CPcontroller program cartridge×2 - retrieve Install relays and alarm bells for interlock circuits. yields: ERelectromagnetic relay×12 EAelectric alarm bell×2 - retrieve Place level and speed sensors on the automated line. yields: TLtank level sensor×2 LSline speed sensor×2

Rewards: CPcontroller program cartridge×2 ERelectromagnetic relay×4

Win-path

Unlocks after: Automate a Line with a PLC

Upgrade your machine shop with a CNC mill and CNC lathe that follow stored programs to cut parts to precise dimensions. This only works once you have stable power, precision measurement, and standardized fasteners.

Do this: - retrieve Have or place a CNC mill and CNC lathe. yields: BCbasic CNC mill×1 BCbasic CNC lathe×1 - retrieve Have an electric machine shop and industrial computer online. yields: MSmachine shop (electric)×1 RIrugged industrial computer×1

Rewards: RIrugged industrial computer×1

Win-path

Unlocks after: CNC Machines in the Shop

Install a simple programmable robot arm in a fenced cell to load and unload hot or repetitive work from a machine, such as a press or CNC station.

Do this: - retrieve Have or place a robot arm with a safety cage. yields: BIbasic industrial robot arm×1 ISindustrial safety cage×1 - retrieve Install a powered conveyor and position sensors for the cell. yields: PCpowered conveyor segment×1 LPlimit position sensor×4

Rewards: LPlimit position sensor×4

Win-path

Unlocks after: Automate a Line with a PLC

Add a digital overview panel and simple HMI screens that collect status from PLCs and sensors, so you can watch lines and acknowledge alarms from a central point.

Do this: - retrieve Have or place HMI panels and a rugged industrial computer. yields: BHbasic HMI panel×2 RIrugged industrial computer×1 - retrieve Install the AC grid mimic panel with metering instruments. yields: AGAC grid mimic panel×1 PApanel ammeter×4 PVpanel voltmeter×4

Rewards: PApanel ammeter×2 PVpanel voltmeter×2

Checkpoint

Unlocks after: Digital Monitoring and Alarms, First Industrial Robot Cell, Czochralski Wafers and Logic Chips, Carbon Fiber and CFRP

Confirm that discrete semiconductors, logic modules, PLC racks, at least one PLC-run line, CNC machines, robot cells and digital plant monitoring are all running together.

Rewards: SSSolid-State Cache×1

Exchange

Unlocks after: Tier 5 -- Solid-State and Automation Online

Exchange Tier V tech tokens for Tier VI. Repeatable.

Do this: - do

Costs: TTTech Token V×5

Rewards: TTTech Token VI×1

Win-path

Unlocks after: Diodes, Rectifiers and Transistors

Upgrade from raw silicon wafers to device-grade monocrystalline wafers by building the full trichlorosilane / Siemens CVD / Czochralski pull sequence: MG-Si + HCl → SiHCl3 → (CVD) → 9N poly → (CZ pull) → ingot → (wire saw + CMP) → polished wafer.

Do this: - retrieve React MG-Si with HCl gas to produce trichlorosilane (SiHCl3). yields: TRtrichlorosilane×4 - retrieve Run the Siemens CVD bell-jar reactor to deposit 9N polysilicon on hot rods. yields: EGelectronic-grade polysilicon×4 - retrieve Pull a Czochralski monocrystalline ingot and saw it into polished device-grade wafers. yields: CSCzochralski silicon ingot×1 PSpolished silicon wafer×8

Rewards: PSpolished silicon wafer×4

Win-path

Unlocks after: Trichlorosilane and Pure Silicon

Fabricate integrated circuit logic chips from polished Czochralski wafers: define active regions by ion implantation, grow gate oxide, pattern and etch multiple metal interconnect layers by photolithography, then dice into individual chips.

Do this: - retrieve Fab logic chips from polished wafers and copper interconnects. yields: LClogic chip×8

Rewards: LClogic chip×4

Win-path

Unlocks after: CNC Machines in the Shop

Produce carbon fiber reinforced polymer (CFRP) structural panels: coal tar → acrylonitrile (Sohio process) → polyacrylonitrile (PAN) fiber → stabilize → carbonize at 1200°C → impregnate with epoxy → autoclave cure at 130-180°C.

Do this: - retrieve Produce acrylonitrile via the Sohio process, then polymerize and spin PAN fiber. yields: ACacrylonitrile×4 PPPAN precursor fiber×2 - retrieve Stabilize PAN fiber and carbonize at 1200°C in nitrogen to form carbon fiber tow. yields: CFcarbon fiber tow×4 - retrieve Produce epoxy resin and autoclave-cure CFRP structural panels. yields: ERepoxy resin×2 CSCFRP structural panel×2

Rewards: CSCFRP structural panel×2

docs.text: [← Solid State](6-solidstate.md)

docs.title: Tier 7 — Space
docs.text:
The win. Cryogenic oxygen, titanium, superalloys, composites — assemble an engine, a stage, a vehicle, and launch.

7 Tier 7 — Space Unlocks after: Wafers, automation, superalloys.→Leads to: Cryogenics, titanium, the rocket. ORBITAL LAUNCH (win).

14 quests · 12 on the win-path · 1 branches

Briefing Win-path

Unlocks after: Rockets, Reactors and Orbit

Refine kerosene and liquid oxygen, build a vertical engine test stand and fire modern liquid-fuel rocket engines under instrumentation.

Do this: - retrieve Have or place a vertical engine test stand. yields: VEvertical engine test stand×1 - retrieve Produce and load propellants for test firing. yields: RKrefined kerosene propellant×40 DLdistilled LOX propellant×40 - retrieve Fire kerolox test engines with high-pressure instruments. yields: KTkerolox test rocket engine×2 HPhigh-pressure gauge×4

Win-path

Unlocks after: Propellants and Test Stands

Construct a launch pad and three-stage rocket capable of placing a small uncrewed satellite into low orbit, then prove you can reach space reliably.

Do this: - retrieve Have or place a launch pad complex and tracking radar. yields: BLbasic launch pad complex×1 GTground tracking radar×1 - retrieve Assemble a three-stage orbital rocket with a tech demo satellite. yields: OTorbital three-stage rocket stack×1 TDtechnology demonstrator satellite×1

Win-path

Unlocks after: First Orbital Launch

Deploy a small constellation of communication or relay satellites and tie them into ground stations so your world has continuous coverage over key regions.

Do this: - retrieve Launch three communication satellites into orbit. yields: BCbasic comms satellite×3 - retrieve Have or place ground stations, tracking radar and a relay station. yields: GSground station dish×1 GTground tracking radar×1 HRhilltop relay station×1

Win-path

Unlocks after: Propellants and Test Stands

Build a grid-connected nuclear power station that can provide steady base-load electricity for launches and heavy industry without relying on fossil fuels.

Do this: - retrieve Have or place a light water reactor and cooling towers. yields: LWlight-water nuclear reactor×1 CTcooling tower pair×1 - retrieve Load enriched uranium fuel rods into the reactor. yields: EUenriched uranium fuel rod×40 - retrieve Connect the reactor output to a high-voltage switchyard. yields: HVhigh-voltage switchyard×1

Win-path

Unlocks after: Satellites for Comms and Sensing

Assemble a modular orbital station with power, life support and docking, turning orbit from a fly-through into a place where work can happen continuously.

Do this: - retrieve Launch and assemble the core station module with solar trusses. yields: SCstation core module×1 SSstation solar truss module×2 - retrieve Install docking ports and a life support loop. yields: SDstandard docking port×2 BLbasic life-support loop×1

Win-path

Unlocks after: Orbital Workshop and Habitat

Send an uncrewed lander to a lunar site, drill regolith or ice, run a small in-situ resource utilisation demo and return data or samples to orbit or ground.

Do this: - retrieve Build a robotic lunar lander with a regolith drill. yields: RLrobotic lunar lander×1 RRrobotic regolith drill×1 - retrieve Equip the lander with an ISRU demo unit and sample return capsule. yields: DIdemo ice ISRU extractor×1 SRsample return capsule×1

Win-path

Unlocks after: Robotic Lander and Lunar ISRU Demo

Construct a prototype electromagnetic mass driver on the Moon to fling processed regolith or ore pods into lunar orbit instead of launching them by rocket.

Do this: - retrieve Build the mass driver track with guidance coil arrays. yields: LMlunar mass driver track×1 GCguidance coil array×4 - retrieve Install a lunar solar power plant and bulk regolith hopper. yields: LSlunar solar power plant×1 BRbulk regolith hopper×1

Win-path

Unlocks after: Orbital Workshop and Habitat

Build a solar power satellite with large arrays in orbit that beams energy down to a rectenna field, supplementing your ground grid with space-based power.

Do this: - retrieve Build and launch a solar power satellite with attitude control. yields: HShuge SPS solar array×1 ACattitude control array×1 - retrieve Install the microwave beamer and ground rectenna field. yields: MPmicrowave power beamer×1 RGrectenna ground field×1

Checkpoint

Unlocks after: Solar Power from Orbit, Mass Driver for Bulk Lunar Exports, Zircaloy and Inconel Superalloy

Confirm that liquid-fuel rocketry, orbital launch, satellite networks, nuclear base-load power, an orbital station, lunar ISRU, a mass driver and space-based solar power are all operating together in your ecosystem.

Rewards: OCOrbital Crate×1

Exchange

Unlocks after: Tier 6 -- Space Age and High-Energy Online

Exchange Tier VI tech tokens for Tier VII. Repeatable.

Do this: - do

Costs: TTTech Token VI×5

Rewards: TTTech Token VII×1

Win-path

Unlocks after: Propellants and Test Stands

Process ilmenite ore through the full titanium chain: fluidized-bed chlorination (TiO2 + 2Cl2 + 2C → TiCl4 + 2CO) → fractional distillation → Hunter reduction (TiCl4 + 4Na → Ti + 4NaCl) → vacuum arc remelting (VAR, triple-melt for rotating parts) → Ti-6Al-4V alloying → plate forging and rolling.

Do this: - retrieve Chlorinate ilmenite ore in a fluidized bed to produce TiCl4 ('tickle'). yields: TTtitanium tetrachloride×4 - retrieve Run Hunter reduction (TiCl4 + 4Na) and vacuum arc remelt the sponge. yields: TStitanium sponge×4 TItitanium ingot×2 - retrieve VAR-alloy Ti-6Al-4V and hot-roll titanium plate for structural use. yields: T6Ti-6Al-4V titanium alloy×2 TAtitanium alloy plate×4

Rewards: TAtitanium alloy plate×4

Win-path

Unlocks after: Propellants and Test Stands

Produce refractory and strategic metals: wolframite → NaOH pressure digest → solvent extraction → APT precipitation → calcine WO3 → H2 reduction → W powder → press/sinter/swage W rod; and columbite → HF digest → MIBK solvent extraction → Nb2O5 calcine → aluminothermic reduction → ferroniobium.

Do this: - retrieve Digest wolframite in NaOH, solvent-extract, and calcine APT to produce WO3. yields: TTtungsten trioxide×4 - retrieve H2-reduce WO3 to W powder, cold-press, sinter at 2300u{00b0}C, and swage into dense W rods. yields: TMtungsten metal powder×4 STsintered tungsten rod×2 - retrieve HF-digest columbite, MIBK-extract Nb, calcine to Nb2O5, and aluminothermically reduce to ferroniobium. yields: NPniobium pentoxide (Nb2O5)×2 FEferroniobium×2

Rewards: STsintered tungsten rod×2 FEferroniobium×2

Win-path

Unlocks after: Titanium Processing Chain, Tungsten and Ferroniobium

Complete the high-performance alloy matrix. Zirconium chain: zircon → carbo-chlorination → ZrCl4 → Hf separation (< 100 ppm Hf for nuclear grade) → Kroll reduction → vacuum-induction melt → zircaloy-2 cladding alloy (Zr + 1.3% Sn + 0.1% Fe + 0.1% Cr). Superalloy chain: nickel cathode + ferrochrome + steel_ingot + ferroniobium → VIM then VAR → Inconel 718-like ingot.
