Do this: - retrieve Carbo-chlorinate zircon to ZrCl4 and Kroll-reduce to hafnium-free sponge. yields: ZTzirconium tetrachloride×4 ZSzirconium sponge×4 - retrieve Vacuum-induction melt zircaloy-2 from Hf-free sponge (nuclear-grade fuel rod cladding). yields: ZCzircaloy cladding alloy×2 - retrieve VIM + VAR melt Inconel 718-like superalloy from nickel cathode + ferrochrome + ferroniobium. yields: ISInconel superalloy ingot×2

Rewards: ZCzircaloy cladding alloy×2 ISInconel superalloy ingot×1

docs.title: Tier 6 is where the factory looks outward
docs.text:
You have an automated, solid-state industrial base. It produces electricity, refined materials, and precision components without you touching most of it.

Tier 6 uses that base to do something no earlier tier could: leave.

The arc of this chapter:

  • Propellants and test stands -- liquid-fuel rocketry. Kerosene and LOX. Instrumented engine runs before anything leaves the ground.
  • First orbital launch -- a three-stage rocket placing hardware in orbit. Not a suborbital hop. Actual orbit.
  • Satellite network -- communications and sensing infrastructure. Your world gains persistent orbital coverage.
  • Nuclear base-load power -- the grid problem at this scale is real. Nuclear solves it without burning anything.
  • Orbital station -- orbit becomes a place where work happens, not just a trajectory.
  • Lunar ISRU demo -- a robotic lander extracts resources in-situ. The Moon starts feeding the supply chain.
  • Mass driver -- bulk lunar material launched without rockets. Energy cost drops by an order of magnitude.
  • Orbital solar power -- the grid reaches into space. Base-load from orbit, beamed down.

This is only possible because T3 through T5 automated the supply chain that feeds it. Without the blast furnace chain, the ore processing line, the solid-state fab -- none of this lifts off.

docs.title: Robotic Lander and Lunar ISRU Demo
Mass Driver for Bulk Lunar Exports
Tier 6 -- Space Age and High-Energy Online
Recipe balance review — the GregTech lens
docs.text:
Dev / design note

This is a design document, not a player guide page. It records a full pass over the merged recipe graph — 685 recipes (469 from the explicit RecipeDB packs + 216 item-embedded recipes) — measured against the question: does this feel like a GregTech-grade industrial chain?

docs.title: The numbers
docs.text:
Metric Conduvia today GregTech-like target Max crafting depth (steps from raw) 7 12–18+ Depth distribution 100 @ d1 · 97 @ d2 · 64 @ d3, then falls off a fat middle at d5–d10 Recipes with byproducts (>1 output) 24% 40–60% (ore lines especially) Trivial 1-in → 1-out taps 19% (128 recipes) near zero Inputs per recipe (mean / median / max) 2.12 / 2 / 6 4–9 common Recipes with a time cost 60% (median 40s, max 720s) ~100%

The tell is in the keystone metals:

copper_ingot   = depth 2
bronze_ingot   = depth 2
iron_bloom     = depth 2
wrought_iron   = depth 3
steel_ingot    = depth 3
charcoal       = depth 1

In GregTech, copper alone is crush → wash → centrifuge → smelt, and steel is a whole saga. Right now raw_ore_native_copper → smelt → ingot is essentially the entire copper economy.

docs.title: Verdict
docs.text: The chemistry is honest, but the graph is shallow and byproduct-poor. It currently reads closer to Minecraft+ than GregTech. The good news: the realism is already there, so deepening the chain is mostly adding the real intermediate steps that actually exist in metallurgy and chemistry — not inventing fake busywork.

docs.title: Where it's thin — and how to deepen it
1. Ore → metal is one tap. Make it a line.
docs.text:
This is the single biggest, most realistic win. Replace ore → smelt → ingot with the real pipeline:

crush → wash / sieve → concentrate (gravity or froth flotation) → roast / calcine → smelt → refine / cast

Every stage is real metallurgy, and every stage naturally produces byproducts — stone dust, gangue, slag, and secondary metals from polymetallic ores (chalcopyrite → copper + pyrite/sulfur; galena → lead + silver). One change raises depth, byproduct rate, and realism at once. The T1_OreProcessing pack (16 recipes today) is the seed — it wants to be ~50.

docs.title: 2. Add the GregTech "material forms" fan-out.
docs.text:
Once you have an ingot, real fabrication makes a family, each at a distinct station:

ingot → plate → foil
ingot → rod → bolt / screw
ingot → wire → fine wire

Low design cost, high step-density, and exactly how GT stretches one metal into 20–30 recipes.

docs.title: 3. Lean into byproducts you already have the chemistry for.
docs.text:
• Charcoal kiln → charcoal + wood tar + pyroligneous acid
  • Smelting → slag (the waste_slag / crushed_slag items already exist — wire them in everywhere)
  • Chlor-alkali → Cl₂ + H₂ + NaOH (triple output)

We're at 24% multi-output; the chemistry to reach ~40% is already real.

docs.title: 4. Kill or enrich the 128 trivial taps.
docs.text: Free clicks like stone_river_rounded → stone, hammerstone → tool_hammerstone_basic, and 2× tar → pitch should either be merged upstream or gated behind a tool + time + a byproduct so they earn their slot.

docs.title: 5. Put time (and eventually energy) on everything.
docs.text: About 40% of recipes are instant. GregTech's entire pacing is time × power per step. Even before an EU-style energy system, giving every processing recipe a time tied to its station tier will make the climb feel industrial. The existing range is sane (median 40s, max 720s) — just extend coverage from 60% → ~100%.

docs.title: What's already good (don't lose it)
docs.text:
• Zero broken references — every recipe input resolves to a real item. The graph is structurally sound.
  • ~85% honest chemistry holds — bronze casting reusing a stone_mold_ingot, blood → blood-meal drying, fat → tallow rendering are all real and correctly modeled.
  • The survival/food tier is already deep (88 food recipes with real preservation). The shallowness is specifically in ore → metal → industry — which is exactly the spine to thicken for the rocket climb.

docs.title: Reachability sanity (not a bug)
docs.text: From the world-gather outputs plus world-supplied primitives, the large majority of the 832-item set is reachable. A small number are gated behind a handful of keystones — water_raw, steam_charge, steel_plate_rolled, sodium_hydroxide, metal_aluminum_ingot, plc_* — and a few remaining items are true orphans (in no recipe and unreachable). The keystones are items to audit, not missing recipes; the most load-bearing one is water_raw.

docs.title: The 85% rule
docs.text: 85% real-process fidelity is a floor, not a ceiling. Where a mechanic feels shallow or weird, we go above it — more steps, more honest byproducts, more intermediate forms — rather than capping the realism to hit a number.

docs.title: Missing-chains roadmap — cleared
docs.text:
This roadmap is complete

Every chain this page used to flag as “still owed” is now built and in-world. The list below is kept as a record of the build order that was followed; each entry now links to the field guide for the finished chain. There are no outstanding new-chain gaps — the current world ships 832 items · 685 recipes · 153 stations across all 27 chains.

This page was originally generated by tools/missing_chains.py, which walks the recipe graph from mineable ore + world-gathered inputs and flagged every recipe that couldn’t bottom out. All of those blocking inputs now have real chains.

docs.title: Chains that were built (former priorities)
docs.text: Chain Real process Now documented in Sulfuric acid (acid_sulfuric) Contact Process: SO₂ → SO₃ → oleum → H₂SO₄ Sulfuric acid Iron → steel → rolled stock Blast furnace → BOF/EAF → rolling Iron & steel Chlor-alkali (sodium_hydroxide, gas_chlorine, gas_hydrogen) Brine electrolysis: 2NaCl + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + Cl₂ + H₂ Chlor-alkali Aluminum (metal_aluminum_ingot) Bayer + Hall-Héroult Aluminum Air separation (O₂/N₂/Ar) Cryogenic distillation of liquefied air Air & ammonia Ammonia (gas_ammonia) Haber-Bosch: N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃ Air & ammonia Ferroalloys (ferrochrome, ferronickel) Chromite → ferrochrome; laterite/sulfide → ferronickel Ferroalloys Copper oxide / SX-EW Acid leach → solvent extraction → electrowinning Copper oxide / SX-EW

docs.title: Cascade roots (resolved)
docs.text:
The big graph-blockers — steel_plate_rolled, steel_beam, metal_aluminum_ingot, alloy_aluminum_aerospace, and the late-tier electronics/space assemblies (avionics_flight_computer, plc_*, motor_ac_induction) — all resolved once the metals + chemistry chains above landed. They are reachable in the live graph today.

Not real gaps

Wildlife carcasses, berries, acorns, hides, and tallow were only ever flagged as “NEEDS NEW CHAIN” because they are world-gathered, not crafted. They are not processing-chain gaps and never were.

docs.title: TODO / Backlog
docs.text:
The running list

What we've found and will tackle, in priority order. This mirrors the Conduvia Dev Tracker in Notion. Check things off as we go; add new items at the bottom of the right section.

All 27 industrial chains are built

The current world ships 832 items · 685 recipes · 153 stations across all 27 chains — survival → stone → metal ages → steam → steel → electric → solid-state → space. Copper (sulfide and oxide/SX-EW), sulfuric acid, chlor-alkali, ammonia, air separation, iron→steel, ferroalloys, aluminum, silicon, titanium, zirconium, uranium and the rest each have a field guide. The remaining work below is balance, docs, and presentation — not missing content.

docs.title: Now / next
docs.text:
• [ ] Docs navigation pass. The catalogue surface (items/, recipes/, stations/) is almost entirely un-hyperlinked — chips render as inert text. Make :ci[…] chips link to the item/station they name. (in progress this session)
  • [ ] Station-name normalization. Recipe cards and the item “Made at” column show a mix of raw snake_case ids and drifted display names that don’t match the station catalogue. Reconcile every label to the canonical stations displayName.
  • [ ] Audit the water_raw keystone. It gates 15+ downstream recipes (buckets, brine, treated water, ammonium line, electrolysis). Confirm there's an early, obvious, low-friction way to get it. Most load-bearing single item in the graph.

docs.title: Recipe balance — GregTech-ification  ·  High
docs.text:
From the balance review:

  • [ ] Deepen the remaining shallow taps. Merge or gate the trivial 1-in → 1-out conversions (e.g. stone_river_rounded → stone, hammerstone → tool_hammerstone_basic, 2× tar → pitch) behind a tool + time + byproduct.
  • [ ] Material-forms fan-out. Continue ingot → plate → foil, → rod → bolt/screw, → wire → fine wire, each at its own station, for metals that don’t yet have it.
  • [ ] Time + energy coverage. Extend processing-time coverage toward ~100% and tie duration to station tier; finish the energy/EU pass and settle on one unit convention per card (see balance review).
  • [ ] Multi-output target. Keep pushing realistic byproducts (stone dust, slag, secondary metals, kiln tar, chlor-alkali co-products) toward the multi-output target tracked in the balance review.

docs.title: Content gaps  ·  Medium
docs.text:
• [ ] Orphan items. A handful of items still appear in no recipe — decide per item: wire into the graph, or cut.
  • [ ] Token / reward economy — confirm the category-token upgrade and bundle exchange rates feel earned (Survival/Industry/Tech/Food, tiers I–X).
  • [ ] Geology timeline entries are generated in-game — confirm the world data drives them correctly on the live site.
  • [ ] Formula column fill. The item catalogue’s Formula column is mostly empty; backfill chemical formulae where they apply (raw gathered items legitimately have none).

docs.title: Bugs / risks
docs.text: • [ ] 224 code TODOs flagged across the codebase — triage which block the win-path vs. which are cosmetic.

docs.title: Done
docs.text:
• [x] Copper chain — full real-life processing. Complete end-to-end copper line (mine → crush → float → roast → matte smelt → convert → fire/electro-refine → forms) plus the oxide SX-EW route. This was the GregTech pilot template the other metals followed.
  • [x] Build all priority industrial chains. Sulfuric acid, iron→steel→rolled stock, chlor-alkali, aluminum (Bayer + Hall-Héroult), air separation + ammonia, ferroalloys, and the late-tier chemistry/electronics/space assemblies are all in-world. (Closes the old missing-chains roadmap.)
  • [x] Item icon art. The icon set is in docs/assets/icons/ and wired through the manifest; chips show real art (category-tinted monogram only as a fallback).
  • [x] Display-case convention. Item names render Title-cased across the catalogue and chips.
  • [x] Parse all 685 recipes into structured data (469 explicit packs + 216 item-embedded), 0 broken references.
  • [x] Build the full reachability + orphan analysis across the 832-item set, keystones + orphans identified.
  • [x] Run the GregTech balance pass (depth, byproducts, input cardinality, time coverage) — see the balance review.
  • [x] Publish the full Recipes browser on this site (every recipe, byproducts flagged).
  • [x] Fix the docs-deploy bugs (Unicode escape artifacts in 15 files; 404s from directory-URL mismatch).
  • [x] Site hole-audit: 0 broken links; registered missing byproduct items (CO₂, ash, wood gas, slag, refining slag); replaced the empty geology-timeline page with a real field-manual page.
  • [x] Math rendering. Wired pymdownx.arithmatex + MathJax so the chemistry equations render instead of printing as literal $$…$$.

docs.title: Industrial gases — cracking the air & fixing nitrogen
docs.text:
Two of the most useful chemicals in the world are quite literally free — nitrogen and oxygen make up 99% of the air you're breathing right now. The hard part is splitting the air into the two gases, and then — for nitrogen — bullying the most stubborn molecule in nature into reacting. This pack builds the two plants that do that, plus the plain steel cylinder that bottles the results.

Why these unlock so much

Three whole branches of the tree were stuck waiting on one gas: nothing produced nitrogen, nothing produced ammonia, and the empty gas cylinder that every fill recipe consumes had no source at all. Build these two plants and the nitrogen cylinders, ammonia cylinders, ammonium hydroxide, and the entire acrylonitrile → carbon-fibre → CFRP tail all come alive at once.

docs.title: 1 · Air separation — distilling the sky
docs.text:
The air is a free mine you never have to dig: ~78% N₂, ~21% O₂, ~1% Ar. To split it you chill the air until it turns into a liquid, then gently boil it back off — because each gas boils at a slightly different temperature, so they come off one at a time.

Gas Boiling point Share of air Nitrogen −196 °C 78% Argon −186 °C ~1% Oxygen −183 °C 21% # Step · station In → Out Tier · time · energy 1 Liquefy · ASU 10 air → 10 liquid air T3 · 60s · 220 kJ 2 Rectify · ASU 10 liquid air → 7 N₂ + 2 O₂ + 1 Ar T3 · 90s · 120 kJ

Turning the air into a liquid is the pricey part (all that squeezing and chilling); the splitting that follows is almost free. One tall column does it in a single pass — one input, three clean gases out — the tidiest multi-output in the whole plant.

docs.title: 2 · Haber-Bosch — fixing nitrogen into ammonia
docs.text:
Nitrogen gas is famously lazy: that N≡N triple bond is one of the strongest grips in all of chemistry, which is exactly why the air around you doesn't just catch fire. To force it to react you need brute force — a hot, high-pressure reactor with an iron helper (a catalyst) inside.

\[ N_2 + 3\,H_2 \rightarrow 2\,NH_3 \] Step · station In → Out Conditions Tier · time · energy Synthesise · Haber-Bosch converter 1 N₂ + 3 H₂ → 2 NH₃ promoted iron cat., ~450 °C, >100 bar T4 · 150s · 320 kJ

Only a little of the gas reacts each time through, so a real plant keeps looping the leftover gas back around again and again. The neat part is where the ingredients come from: the hydrogen comes straight from your salt-water plant, the nitrogen from the air plant — the two new gas plants feeding into one. This single reaction is the root of fertilizer, nitric acid, explosives, and household ammonia.

flowchart LR
  AIR["air (free)"] -->|liquefy| LA["liquid air"]
  LA -->|rectify| N2["nitrogen"]
  LA -->|rectify| O2["oxygen"]
  LA -->|rectify| AR["argon"]
  H2["hydrogen (chlor-alkali)"] --> HB["Haber-Bosch"]
  N2 --> HB
  HB --> NH3["ammonia"]
  NH3 -->|+ water| AOH["ammonium hydroxide"]
  NH3 -->|+ coal tar| ACN["acrylonitrile"]
  ACN --> PAN["PAN fibre"] --> CF["carbon fibre"] --> CFRP["CFRP panel"]
  STEEL["steel billet"] -->|deep-draw| CYL["gas cylinder"]
  CYL --> FILL["O2 / N2 / H2 / NH3 cylinders"]
  N2 --> FILL
  O2 --> FILL

docs.title: 3 · Gas cylinders
docs.text:
Every bottled gas needs a bottle. A high-pressure cylinder is a steel bottle punched from a single slug of steel — no welded seam that could burst — so we make cylinder_gas_basic from a steel billet, and the existing fills (oxygen, nitrogen, hydrogen, ammonia) snap right into place. (This also retired a broken placeholder that tried to make an "empty" cylinder by using up a full one.)

Why no helium?

Helium is only a tiny trace in the air — in real life it's pulled out of natural-gas wells, not the sky. Rather than fake it, cylinder_helium is left unfinished until a natural-gas chain exists. We'd rather be honest than cheat.

docs.title: Aluminum — Bayer process & the Hall-Héroult cell
docs.text:
Aluminum is the most abundant metal in the crust and yet it was a precious metal until 1886 — Napoleon III supposedly served his best guests on aluminum plates while everyone else made do with gold. The reason is chemistry: alumina (Al₂O₃) is one of the most stable oxides there is, and fire alone can't pull the oxygen off it. No furnace will ever make aluminum, however hot you build it. It took electricity, and lots of it, to finally break that grip — and that's the whole story of this chain.

The fix this chain makes

The old version of this recipe made aluminum by roasting it with charcoal, like iron. That simply doesn't work — it's the classic example of why you need electricity to make aluminum at all. This chain fixes it with the real method: melt the cleaned ore into a special bath and run electricity through it (the Hall-Héroult process), plus the real four-step Bayer cleanup before it.

docs.title: Stage 1 — Bayer process (bauxite → alumina)
docs.text:
Bauxite (aluminum's ore) is only about half good stuff; the rest is iron, sand, and other junk. You can't sort it out by hand, so instead you dissolve the aluminum part out with hot lye — a strong cleaner, also called caustic soda — and everything that won't dissolve sinks out as red mud.

# Step · station In → Out Chemistry Tier · time · energy 1 Crush · stamp mill 2 bauxite → 2 crushed + stone dust mill for caustic access T2 · 30s · 16 kJ 2 Digest · Bayer autoclave 3 crushed + 2 NaOH → 3 aluminate liquor + 2 red mud Al(OH)₃ + NaOH → NaAlO₂; Fe/Si/Ti → mud T3 · 120s · 140 kJ 3 Precipitate · precipitation tank 3 liquor → 2 gibbsite + 1 NaOH NaAlO₂ + 2H₂O → Al(OH)₃ + NaOH T3 · 180s · 35 kJ 4 Calcine · rotary kiln 3 gibbsite → 2 alumina 2Al(OH)₃ → Al₂O₃ + 3H₂O T3 · 90s · 120 kJ

Two honest details worth noting:

  • Red mud is the industry's biggest unsolved trash problem — more than a tonne of it for every tonne of aluminum, sitting in giant ponds around the world.
  • The lye gets reused: a later step makes the lye fresh again, and it's pumped right back to the start. The process drinks its own cleaner.

docs.title: Stage 2 — Hall-Héroult (alumina → aluminum)
docs.text:
Alumina melts above 2000 °C, far too hot to work with on its own. The trick Hall and Héroult found independently in 1886: dissolve it in molten cryolite (Na₃AlF₆), which drops the working temperature to ~960 °C, then run a huge electric current through it to pull out pure aluminum. (Splitting a chemical apart with electricity like this is called electrolysis.)

# Step · station In → Out Chemistry Tier · time · energy 5 Bake anode · prebake furnace 2 coke → 1 carbon anode coke + pitch, baked T3 · 75s · 90 kJ 6 Electrolyse · Hall-Héroult pot 2 alumina + 1 anode + 1 cryolite → 2 aluminum + 2 CO₂ 2Al₂O₃ + 3C → 4Al + 3CO₂ T4 · 200s · 600 kJ

The carbon block (the anode) slowly burns away — the oxygen pulled off the alumina eats it up — so you're forever swapping in a fresh one. This is the single most power-hungry step in the whole game: aluminum really is just electricity turned into metal.

flowchart LR
  BX["bauxite"] -->|crush| BXC["crushed"]
  BXC -->|digest + NaOH| LIQ["aluminate liquor"]
  LIQ -->|precipitate| GIB["gibbsite"]
  LIQ -.->|red mud waste| RM["red mud"]
  GIB -->|calcine| ALO["alumina"]
  GIB -.->|regenerated NaOH| LIQ
  COKE["coke"] -->|bake| AN["carbon anode"]
  ALO -->|Hall-Heroult cell| AL["aluminum"]
  CRY["cryolite bath"] --> AL
  AN --> AL
  AL -.-> AERO["aerospace alloy / rocket tanks"]

Why cryolite matters

Cryolite (made in its own chain from fluorite → hydrofluoric acid) finally has a job here — it is the solvent that makes electrolysis possible at a survivable temperature. Without it you would need to hold over 2000 °C; with it, ~960 °C. That single substitution is what turned aluminum from a curiosity into structural metal.

docs.title: Cells & canisters — what the wall can survive
docs.text: A “cell” is just a container, but in a chemistry game the container is a real engineering decision: the wall has to survive whatever you pour into it. Conduvia had cell_metal and cell_glass sitting in the item list with no recipe at all, which meant every filled canister downstream was unreachable. This chain gives them a proper forming route and turns four hand-waved fills into real, stationed recipes.

docs.title: Why stainless, and why it matters
docs.text:
One metal cell body has to survive both strong sulfuric acid and hot caustic soda:

  • Plain steel is eaten by acid.
  • Aluminium dissolves outright in lye: 2 Al + 2 NaOH + 6 H2O -> 2 Na[Al(OH)4] + 3 H2 — it doesn't just corrode, it generates flammable hydrogen.

Only stainless shrugs off both, so the metal cell is deep-drawn and seam-welded from stainless sheet. The glass cell is sealed heat-proof glass (the same stuff as lab beakers) — acids don't touch it and it shrugs off sudden temperature swings, but it's breakable, so you use it when you want to see what's inside.

docs.title: The route
docs.text:
flowchart LR
  ss["stainless sheet"] -->|"press + seam-weld"| cm["metal cell"]
  gt["borosilicate tube"] -->|"glass bench"| cg["glass cell"]
  cm -->|"fill"| w1["+ water"]
  cm -->|"fill"| a1["+ sulfuric acid"]
  cm -->|"fill"| n1["+ sodium hydroxide"]
  cg -->|"fill"| w2["+ water"]
Step Station In → Out Form metal cell Sheet-Metal Forming & Seaming Press 2 stainless sheet → 2 metal cell Form glass cell Glass Bench 2 borosilicate tube → 2 glass cell Fill (water) Reagent Filling & Sealing Line metal cell + water → filled Fill (acid) Reagent Filling & Sealing Line metal cell + H₂SO₄ → filled Fill (caustic) Reagent Filling & Sealing Line metal cell + NaOH → filled Fill (glass, water) Reagent Filling & Sealing Line glass cell + water → filled

This ties the steel, glass, sulfuric-acid and chlor-alkali chains together — the container branch every reagent eventually needs.

docs.title: Chlor-alkali — caustic soda, chlorine & hydrogen from brine
docs.text:
Here's a two-for-one deal: run electricity through plain salt water and you get two of the world's most useful chemicals at once — lye (sodium hydroxide, a strong cleaner and base) and chlorine gas. You even get hydrogen as a freebie. This is the chlor-alkali process, and it's the quiet workhorse behind half the chemistry in Conduvia: that lye dissolves aluminum ore, helps build the aluminum-smelting bath, makes soap, and neutralizes acid spills all over your base.

One cell, three products

2 NaCl + 2 H₂O → Cl₂ + H₂ + 2 NaOH Chlorine gas bubbles off one side, hydrogen gas off the other, and lye is left dissolved in the water. A special divider down the middle keeps the three apart — let them mix and you just get useless, even dangerous, gunk.

docs.title: The plant, step by step
docs.text:
The chemistry is one line, but a real plant is three unit operations bolted around the cell — and skipping any of them ruins the run.

# Step · station In → Out Why Tier · time · energy 1 Dissolve · brine unit 2 salt + 1 water → 2 brine saturate the feedstock T2 · 30s · 20 kJ 2 Purify · brine unit 2 brine → 2 purified + tailings strip Ca/Mg to ppb — else the membrane fouls T3 · 45s · 40 kJ 3 Electrolyse · membrane cell 2 purified → 1 Cl₂ + 2 dilute caustic + 1 H₂ the cell itself T3 · 120s · 350 kJ 4 Concentrate · evaporator 2 dilute caustic → 1 NaOH + 1 water cell makes only ~12–33%; boil to ~50% T3 · 60s · 110 kJ

The two details that make this honest, not hand-wavy:

  • Clean salt water matters more than you'd guess. The divider inside the cell gets wrecked by the bits of calcium and magnesium in ordinary salt, so the water is scrubbed almost perfectly clean before it ever goes in. That's why cleaning the brine is its own separate step.
  • The cell only makes weak lye. Straight out, it's too watery to use, so you boil it down to the strong stuff everything else needs. That boiling takes a lot of energy on its own.
flowchart LR
  SALT["rock salt"] -->|dissolve| BR["brine"]
  BR -->|soften| PB["purified brine"]
  PB -->|membrane cell| CL["chlorine (anode)"]
  PB -->|membrane cell| H2["hydrogen (cathode)"]
  PB -->|membrane cell| DC["dilute caustic"]
  DC -->|evaporate| NAOH["50% NaOH"]
  NAOH -.-> BAYER["Bayer digestion"]
  NAOH -.-> CRY["cryolite"]
  CL -.-> HCL["HCl / chlorides"]
  H2 -.-> RED["reduction / fuel"]

Two great electrolysis cells, side by side

Conduvia now runs the two defining industrial electrolyses back to back: the Hall-Héroult cell turns alumina into aluminium, and the chlor-alkali cell turns brine into caustic + chlorine. Both are enormous electricity sinks — together they are why an industrial base lives or dies by its power supply.

docs.title: Cobalt — refining cobaltite, and fixing the carbide binder
docs.text:
For a while cobalt just sat in the ground with no way to refine it, so the tungsten chain had to glue its hard cutting-tool tips together with nickel as a fill-in. This chain finally refines real cobalt and fixes that: the tool tip gets its proper cobalt glue — the same recipe real cutting tools use.

flowchart TD
  ore["cobaltite (CoAsS)"] -->|"roast"| ox["cobalt oxide"]
  ore -.->|"SO2"| acid["acid plant"]
  ore -.->|"As2O3 (toxic)"| waste["captured arsenic"]
  ox -->|"sulfuric leach"| liq["cobalt sulfate liquor"]
  liq -->|"solvent extraction"| pure["purified cobalt solution"]
  liq -.->|"co-recovered"| ni["nickel concentrate"]
  pure -->|"electrowin"| cath["cobalt cathode"]
  cath --> wc["WC-Co carbide insert"]

docs.title: Roast it
docs.text: The ore (cobaltite) is a nasty mix of cobalt, sulfur, and arsenic, so roasting it is messy on purpose. The sulfur leaves as a gas (off to the acid plant), and the poisonous arsenic fumes are captured instead of vented — a real, ugly headache of cobalt and gold ores. The cobalt is left behind as a roasted powder.

docs.title: Dissolve it and clean it up
docs.text: You dissolve that powder in sulfuric acid to get a pink cobalt liquid, then wash out the nickel and copper that always tag along with cobalt (you keep the nickel — no sense wasting it). Run electricity through the cleaned liquid and pure cobalt plates out, bright and shiny.

docs.title: The payoff: proper cutting-tool tips
docs.text: Now that you have real cobalt, you can rebuild the tungsten-carbide cutting tip the right way. Cobalt grips the hard carbide grains far better than the nickel fill-in did, which is why every real cutting tool uses a few percent cobalt as the glue. The nickel stand-in gets retired.

docs.title: Honest notes
docs.text:
• The arsenic byproduct is captured, not magicked away — it's a genuine hazard of this ore.
  • Cobalt's other real homes (superalloys, magnets, batteries) are noted but not yet built; the carbide binder is the live sink today.

docs.title: Copper Route C — oxide leach (SX-EW)
docs.text:
The main copper guide follows the sulfide route: crush, float, roast, smelt, and refine — lots of fire. But a huge share of the world's copper never goes near a furnace. The colorful, weathered cap of rock sitting on top of a copper deposit can be soaked in acid to pull the copper out, then won back with electricity. That's the SX-EW route. It only became possible in Conduvia once the sulfuric-acid chain existed, because the whole thing runs on acid… and then hands most of it back at the end.

Why two copper routes?

The fire way (smelting): rich ore goes through a furnace; the sulfur leaves as gas that feeds the acid plant. Lots of heat. The wet way (SX-EW, this page): poor ore gets soaked in acid, the copper is picked out of the liquid, then plated out with electricity. No furnace, no fumes, runs at room temperature. Both finish with the exact same pure copper, so from there they share the same melt-into-ingots tail.

docs.title: The acid loop
docs.text:
This is the satisfying part. The wet route isn't just an acid user — it's almost a closed loop:

  • Soaking spends strong acid to dissolve the copper out of the rock.
  • The pick-out step hands that acid right back (now weak and copper-free), and it gets pumped back onto the pile to soak more rock.
  • The plating step actually remakes strong acid as a side effect: 2 CuSO₄ + 2 H₂O → 2 Cu + O₂ + 2 H₂SO₄.

So the acid plant only has to top up the little bit of acid the waste rock eats — exactly like a real plant does.

docs.title: The chain, step by step
docs.text:
# Step · station In → Out Byproduct / note Tier · time · energy 0 Mine oxide cap · — → 3 oxidized copper ore world resource (gossan) T1 · 25s · — 1 Crush (coarse) · stamp mill 2 ore → 2 crushed + stone dust coarse on purpose — keep the heap permeable T1 · 25s · 12 kJ 2 Heap leach · leach pad 3 crushed + 2 H₂SO₄ → 3 PLS + tailings no oxidant needed; gangue eats acid T2 · 120s · 20 kJ 3 Solvent extraction · mixer-settler 3 PLS → 1 rich electrolyte + 1 dilute acid LIX oxime, pH<2.5, rejects iron; raffinate recycles T3 · 40s · 30 kJ 4 Electrowinning · EW cell 2 electrolyte → 2 cathode + 1 H₂SO₄ + 1 O₂ regenerates acid; Pb anode / SS cathode T3 · 90s · 400 kJ

From the cathode, you're back on the shared copper tail: remelt → refined ingot → plate / foil / rod / wire / dust.

docs.title: The chemistry (the real reactions)
docs.text:
• Dissolving the oxide — these copper minerals dissolve in plain weak acid with no extra helper needed (sulfides can't do this):
    • CuO + H₂SO₄ → CuSO₄ + H₂O
    • malachite: Cu₂CO₃(OH)₂ + 2 H₂SO₄ → 2 CuSO₄ + 3 H₂O + CO₂
    • The usual green/blue minerals: malachite, azurite, chrysocolla, cuprite, tenorite.
  • Picking the copper out — a special oil-based chemical grabs only the copper out of the dirty liquid and leaves the iron behind; then strong acid pulls the copper back off the oil. The oil is used over and over (the game treats it as part of the machine, not a consumed item).
  • Plating it out — electricity drives pure copper onto one plate (Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu⁰, about 99.99% pure) while oxygen bubbles off the other. The leftover liquid turns back into strong acid.

What it unlocks elsewhere

This plating step is the first place the game makes oxygen gas (gas_oxygen). The oxygen that used to just bubble away now becomes a real product, feeding the oxygen-cylinder and liquid-oxygen lines later on.

docs.title: Sulfide vs oxide at a glance
docs.text:
flowchart LR
  subgraph Oxide ["Route C · hydrometallurgy"]
    OX["oxide ore"] -->|crush| OXC["crushed"]
    OXC -->|leach + H2SO4| PLS["PLS"]
    PLS -->|SX / LIX| EL["rich electrolyte"]
    EL -->|electrowin| CAT["cathode"]
    EL -.raffinate.-> PLS
    CAT -.O2 + H2SO4.-> ACID["acid plant"]
  end
  subgraph Sulfide ["Main route · pyrometallurgy"]
    SUL["chalcopyrite"] -->|float→roast→smelt→convert→refine| CAT2["cathode"]
    SUL -.SO2.-> ACID
  end
  CAT --> ING["refined ingot → plate / foil / rod / wire"]
  CAT2 --> ING

When to use which

Use the fire route on rich ore when you want lots of copper fast and want the sulfur fumes to feed your acid plant. Use the wet route on poor ore when you've already got acid to spare and want copper without firing up a furnace — it trades heat and fuel for electricity and acid.

docs.title: Copper — full processing chain
docs.text:
Copper is the first metal in Conduvia we follow all the way, from raw rock to wire-grade metal. Along the road you'll froth it, roast it, melt it, blow air through it, and finally polish it up with electricity. Every step here is how it's really done.

Two real routes

Sulfide route (built): chalcopyrite → concentrate → matte → blister → refined copper. This is ~80% of world production and the one you climb in-game today. Oxide route (SX-EW, built): leach → solvent extraction → electrowinning. The sulfuric-acid chain it depends on is already built — see the Copper oxide / SX-EW field guide.

docs.title: The sulfide chain, step by step
docs.text: # Step · station In → Out Byproduct Tier · time · energy 1 Crush · stamp mill 6 chalcopyrite → 5 crushed stone dust T1 · 45s · — 2 Grind · stamp mill 5 crushed → 4 ground stone dust T1 · 60s · — 3 Froth flotation · flotation cell 4 ground → 3 concentrate tailings T1 · 120s · 25 kJ 4 Partial roast · roasting pit 3 concentrate → 2 calcine SO₂ gas T1 · 180s · 30 kJ 5 Matte smelt (+silica) · reverb furnace 2 calcine + silica + charcoal → 1 matte fayalite slag ×2 T2 · 240s · 140 kJ 6 Convert · Peirce-Smith converter 2 matte + silica → 1 blister SO₂ + slag T2 · 200s · 110 kJ 7a Fire refine (poling) · anode furnace 2 blister + charcoal → 2 tough-pitch ingot refining slag T2 · 150s · 90 kJ 7b Cast anode → electrorefine · tankhouse anode → 99.99% cathode anode slime (Au/Ag/Se/Te) T4 · 600s · 850 kJ 8 Forms · rolling mill / mill ingot → plate → foil; ingot → rod, dust; refined → wire — T1–T3

docs.title: What's actually happening (the curious-kid version)
docs.text:
You don't need any of this to play — but here's why each step is there.

Froth it. The crushed rock is stirred into soapy water while air bubbles up through it. The copper bits hate water, so they grab onto the bubbles and ride up as a froth you skim off the top. The worthless rock gets wet and sinks. You're literally using bubbles to pick out the good stuff. (Pros call it flotation.)

Roast it. A gentle bake burns off some of the sulfur — which leaves as a gas you pipe over to the acid plant — and starts driving the iron out:

\(2\,CuFeS_2 + O_2 \rightarrow Cu_2S + 2\,FeS + SO_2\)

Melt it. You melt the roasted ore with a little sand. The iron grabs the sand and floats off as glassy junk (slag), leaving a richer copper-and-sulfur layer called matte underneath:

\(2\,CuFeS_2 + 2\,SiO_2 + 4\,O_2 \rightarrow Cu_2S + 2\,FeSiO_3 + 3\,SO_2\)

Blow air through it. Blasting air through the molten matte burns away the last of the iron and sulfur, leaving rough blister copper — about 99% pure, but bubbly and ugly:

\(2\,FeS + 3\,O_2 \rightarrow 2\,FeO + 2\,SO_2 \qquad Cu_2S + O_2 \rightarrow 2\,Cu + SO_2\)

Clean it up. A quick fire-cleanup gets you everyday copper. For the really pure stuff you use electricity: the copper slowly dissolves off one slab and re-plates, spotless, onto another — 99.99% pure. Bonus: flecks of gold and silver settle out as sludge at the bottom, well worth collecting.

Byproduct loop

The SO₂ from roasting and converting is not waste — it is the feedstock for the Contact Process that makes sulfuric acid. That chain is built, so copper's off-gas feeds the acid plant that unlocks the oxide (SX-EW) route.

docs.title: Where to find these recipes
docs.text: The crush/grind/float/roast/smelt steps live under Recipes → Tier 1 (Stone & materials) and Tier 2 (Metal Ages); electrorefining sits in Tier 5 (Electric & chemistry). Every copper item links back to the recipe that makes it from the Items & Ores → Tier 2 — Metal Ages catalogue.

docs.title: Ferroalloys — ferrochrome & ferronickel
docs.text:
Plain steel turns into stainless steel at the AOD converter — but only if you can feed it chromium and nickel in a form that survives a bath of molten steel. You don't toss in pure chromium or nickel; they're too pricey to make and they'd just burn off into the slag. Instead you add ready-made iron-chromium and iron-nickel blends, smelted straight from ore. This page covers both.

Where these go

Both blends go into the AOD converter along with steel and lime to make stainless; the iron-chromium one also feeds the Inconel / superalloy line. Before this chain they popped out of thin-air one-step recipes — now they each have a real, furnace-deep backstory.

docs.title: Iron-chromium (ferrochrome)
docs.text:
Chromite ore is the only place chromium comes from, and it's tough stuff. There's no clever chemistry trick here — you just hit it with carbon and a huge jolt of electricity to tear the oxygen off and leave the metal behind.

# Step · station In → Out Note Tier · time · energy 1 Crush · stamp mill 2 chromite → 2 crushed + stone dust friable, sheds fines T2 · 30s · 16 kJ 2 Pelletize · disc + induration 3 crushed → 3 pellets keeps the burden permeable T3 · 45s · 40 kJ 3 SAF smelt · submerged-arc furnace 3 pellets + 3 coke + 1 lime → 2 ferrochrome + 2 slag + 2 CO₂ FeCr₂O₄ + 4C → Fe + 2Cr + 4CO T4 · 150s · 380 kJ

What pours out is about 50–70% chromium (plus a little carbon — the AOD burns that off later). The lime sweeps the leftover rock into slag, and the gas given off burns away cleanly. This furnace is one of the most power-hungry steps in the whole tech tree.

docs.title: Iron-nickel (ferronickel)
docs.text:
This nickel ore is poor stuff — only 1 to 2% nickel — and the bubble-flotation trick doesn't work on it, because the nickel is locked tight inside the rock. So you don't bother cleaning it up; you just dry, bake, and smelt the whole pile. (Industry calls this the rotary-kiln electric-furnace route.)

# Step · station In → Out Note Tier · time · energy 0 Strip-mine laterite · — → 3 saprolite shallow, no shaft T2 · 30s · — 1 Calcine · rotary kiln 3 laterite + 1 coke → 3 calcine + CO₂ drives off crystal water, pre-reduces T3 · 90s · 120 kJ 2 Electric-furnace smelt · SAF 3 calcine + 2 coke + 1 lime → 2 ferronickel + 2 slag ~99% Ni recovery T4 · 150s · 360 kJ

The baked ore is rushed straight from the kiln into the furnace while it's still red-hot, to save energy. What pours out is about 20 to 40% nickel.

docs.title: Both routes at a glance
docs.text:
flowchart LR
  CR["chromite"] -->|crush| CRC["crushed"] -->|pellet| CRP["pellets"]
  CRP -->|SAF + coke + lime| FECR["ferrochrome"]
  LAT["nickel laterite"] -->|rotary kiln calcine| CAL["reduced calcine"]
  CAL -->|electric furnace + coke| FENI["ferronickel"]
  FECR --> AOD["AOD converter"]
  FENI --> AOD
  STEEL["carbon steel"] --> AOD
  AOD --> SS["stainless steel"]
  FECR -.-> INC["inconel / superalloy"]

Two ways to reduce an ore

Iron-chromium cleans up the ore first, then blasts it with carbon and power. Iron-nickel can't be cleaned up at all, so it spends its energy drying and melting the whole pile of rock. Both end at a blend the AOD finishes off — a neat example of how the richness of an ore decides how you have to handle it.

docs.title: Flint — knapping, fire-steel & potters' flint
docs.text:
Tier 1–2 · Branch · Pack T1_Flint.luau

Flint is cryptocrystalline quartz (chert). It breaks by conchoidal fracture — a sharp blow shears off a razor flake — so it was the first tool stone there is. It started as a dead raw_ore_flint with nothing downstream. Two honest non- survival tie-ins keep the chain from being mere fluff: a flint-and-steel fire striker (which reaches back into the steel chain) and calcined potters' flint, a real ceramic-body silica.

docs.title: Flow
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    ore["raw_ore_flint"] -->|knap (hammerstone)| flake["flint flake"]
    ore --> deb["stone debitage (byproduct)"]
    flake -->|retouch| blade["knapped flint blade"]
    flake -->|shape| arrow["flint arrowhead"]
    ore -->|+ steel, struck| fs["flint-and-steel striker"]
    steel["steel_ingot"] --> fs
    ore -->|calcine ~600C + grind| silica["calcined potters' flint"]

docs.title: Steps
docs.text: # Recipe Station In Out 1 fl_knap_flint Knapping Station 2 raw flint 3 flake + 1 stone debitage 2 fl_knap_blade Knapping Station 2 flake 1 knapped blade 3 fl_make_arrowhead Knapping Station 1 flake 2 arrowhead 4 fl_fire_steel Workbench 1 flint + 1 steel ingot 2 fire-steel striker 5 fl_calcine_silica Rotary Calciner Kiln 2 flint 2 calcined potters' flint

docs.title: Why it's built this way
docs.text:
• Conchoidal fracture is the mechanic. Striking a flint core with a hammerstone shears off sharp flakes; the blunt shatter and trimming chips fall away as stone debitage — recovered as temper/abrasive grit, so the previously producer-less stone_flakes item finally has an honest source.
  • Flakes fork into tools. A flake retouched down one edge becomes a durable knife/scraper blade; bifacially worked, it becomes a barbed projectile point.
  • Flint-and-steel is real chemistry, not magic. The flint edge is harder than steel, so a hard scrape shaves off tiny white-hot iron particles — the sparks — which catch in char tinder. It ties the most primitive ore directly to the steel chain.
  • Calcined potters' flint is a genuine industrial use. Flint calcined near 600 °C turns friable and grinds to a fine silica powder that partly inverts to cristobalite. Added to clay bodies and glazes it stiffens the body and puts the cooled glaze film under compression, suppressing crazing.

docs.title: Byproducts & sinks
docs.text:
• stone debitage (stone_flakes) — temper, abrasive grit, kiln filler.
  • calcined potters' flint (flint_calcined_silica) — feeds ceramic/glaze bodies; a clean silica source parallel to raw_ore_silica.
  • fire-steel striker — survival/utility tool; consumes a steel ingot.

Verified 2026-06-05 against Digitalfire (Flint), the Cambridge Mineralogical Magazine note on raw vs calcined flint, and Wikipedia (Fire striker, Knapping, Flint). Calcination temperature, cristobalite inversion, and the flint-harder- than-steel spark mechanism are all confirmed.

docs.title: Geology Timeline
docs.text: Conduvia’s world isn’t scattered at random — it is laid down in layers like a real planet. Where you dig, how deep you go, and which biome you are standing in all decide which rock and which ore you hit. Learn to read the ground and you stop prospecting blind.

docs.title: Read the rock, not the map
docs.text:
Three rock families cover almost everything you will mine:

  • Sedimentary — layered, near the surface, laid down by water and time. Coal, limestone, clays, sands, and rock salt live here.
  • Igneous — hard rock born from heat, deeper or near old volcanism. Native metals, sulfide veins, and quartz come from here.
  • Metamorphic — rock cooked and squeezed at the boundaries between the other two. Often the richest or most altered ores sit in these zones.

docs.title: Depth bands
docs.text: Band What you find Surface & rivers Soils, clays, and placer gravels — rounded river stone you can pan for native copper, tin, and gold. Shallow beds Sedimentary seams: coal, limestone, sandstone, rock salt. Mid veins Sulfide vein systems: copper, iron, lead, and zinc ores, plus quartz. Deep igneous Richer sulfides and the rarer metals that feed late-game alloys.

docs.title: Biomes hint at ore
docs.text:
• Volcanic & mountain — igneous metals and sulfur.
  • Rivers & floodplains — placer deposits and good clay.
  • Sedimentary basins — coal, limestone, and salt.

docs.title: Why the order is forced
docs.text: You can only reach the deeper, hotter-formed, richer deposits after the tools and heat the earlier tiers give you. Geology is the physical reason the tech ladder cannot be skipped — no deep ore without good picks, and no good picks without the metals from the shallow ore.

docs.title: The in-game timeline
docs.text: The interactive era-by-era timeline is generated live from the world’s geology data when you open it in-game. This page is the field-manual companion that explains how to read it.

docs.title: Gypsum — plaster, drywall, sulfuric acid & cement
docs.text:
Tier 2–4 · Branch · Pack T4_Gypsum.luau

Gypsum (a soft, chalky rock) is the most humble mineral in the game and one of the most useful. From a dead raw_ore_gypsum it now branches into wall plaster, drywall, sulfuric acid, and cement — and it quietly closes a loop by eating up the waste gypsum that the hydrofluoric-acid chain throws off.

docs.title: Flow
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    ore["raw_ore_gypsum"] -->|calcine ~150 C| plaster["plaster of Paris"]
    hfwaste["waste_gypsum_refined (from HF chain)"] -->|recalcine| plaster
    plaster -->|+ water, set| board["gypsum board (drywall)"]
    ore -->|Mueller-Kuehne| clinker["cement clinker"]
    coke["coke_fuel"] --> clinker
    silica["raw_ore_silica"] --> clinker
    ore -->|Mueller-Kuehne| so2["gas_so2 -> acid plant"]
    clinker -->|grind + gypsum retarder| portland["Portland cement"]
    ore --> portland

docs.title: Steps
docs.text: # Recipe Station In Out 1 gyp_calcine_plaster Rotary Calciner Kiln 2 gypsum 2 plaster of Paris 2 gyp_recover_waste_plaster Rotary Calciner Kiln 2 HF waste gypsum 2 plaster of Paris 3 gyp_cast_board Clay Casting Pit 2 plaster + 1 water 2 gypsum board 4 gyp_muller_kuhne Rotary Calciner Kiln 2 gypsum + coke + silica 1 SO₂ + 2 clinker 5 gyp_grind_portland Cement Ball Mill 2 clinker + 1 gypsum 2 Portland cement

docs.title: Why it's built this way
docs.text:
• The baking trick runs both ways — that's the whole point. A gentle bake near 150 °C drives most of the water out of gypsum and leaves "plaster of Paris" behind. Add the water back and it sets rock-hard again. Drywall is literally that reaction run backwards between two sheets of paper. Bake it too hot, though, and it turns into a dead powder that won't set — so it's a low, careful bake.
  • One kiln, two products. Starve the gypsum of air, mix in some coke, and crank the heat way up: the sulfur leaves as a gas (piped to the acid plant) while what's left bakes into cement clinker. This is the real industrial answer to "how do you make sulfuric acid if you have no sulfur to mine?" — and you get cement out of the same kiln for free.
  • Gypsum is also cement's brake pedal. That clinker gets ground up with a pinch of raw gypsum to slow its setting; without it, cement would harden in the mixer before you could pour it. So gypsum shows up at both the start and the end of the cement story.

docs.title: Byproducts, loops & sinks
docs.text:
• waste_gypsum_refined — the HF chain's waste is now feedstock (loop closed).
  • gas_so2 — fed to the existing contact sulfuric-acid plant.
  • gypsum_board / portland_cement — terminal building materials (future hook: concrete = Portland + sand + gravel + water).

Verified against Wikipedia (Gypsum, Plaster of Paris, Müller-Kühne process / sulfuric acid from gypsum, Portland cement clinker) and process references.

docs.title: Iron & steel — the backbone chain
docs.text:
Iron is the metal everything else leans on. Plates and beams alone are the key to dozens of machines, rails, and buildings, so this is the chain you'll come back to over and over. In Conduvia you take iron the whole way: from a soft lump pulled out of a muddy swamp and baked in a clay oven, all the way up to giant modern furnaces that melt old scrap back into fresh steel. Every step here is how it's really done.

Two eras, two primary routes

Bloomery → wrought iron (Tier 1): solid-state reduction in a clay furnace; the iron never melts. Sponge bloom is forged out to wrought iron. Your earliest, charcoal-only iron. Blast furnace → pig iron → steel (Tier 2–4): coke + flux melt the ore to high-carbon pig iron, then a converter burns the carbon back down to steel. The modern mass-production spine — refined three honest ways: Bessemer, Basic Oxygen (BOF), and Electric Arc (EAF).

docs.title: Getting the rock furnace-ready
docs.text:
You can't just shovel raw crushed rock into a furnace. The dust clogs it up, and the worthless bits waste fuel. So first you clean the ore, then you clump it back into chunks the furnace likes. There are two kinds of iron rock, and each gets cleaned a different way:

  • Magnetite is naturally magnetic. So you run the crushed rock past magnets and they pull out a clean, iron-rich powder, leaving the junk behind. (The fancy name for this is magnetic separation.)
  • Hematite is barely magnetic, so the magnets can't grab it yet. You roast it in a little smoke first, which makes it magnetic, then run it past the magnets.

That clean powder is too fine and dusty to feed straight in, so you clump it into chunks one of two ways:

  • Sinter — bake the powder with a little fuel and crushed limestone until the bits fuse into a hard, crumbly cake. The baking burns off sulfur as a gas, which you pipe over to the acid factory.
  • Pellets — roll the damp powder with a pinch of clay into little balls (think meatballs), then bake them rock-hard.

docs.title: Ironmaking — three ways to win the metal
docs.text:
Route What happens Product Bloomery (T1) Solid-state reduction in a clay furnace; bloom never melts, forged to bar wrought iron (soft, tough, low-C) Blast furnace (T2) Coke → CO reduces oxide as it descends (Fe₂O₃/Fe₃O₄ + CO → Fe + CO₂); limestone fluxes gangue to slag pig iron (~4 % C, brittle) Direct reduction (DRI) (T3) Coal/CO reduce pellets below melting point sponge iron (porous, metallic — clean EAF charge)

The tall blast furnace likes those sinter chunks better than raw rock — air flows through them more easily. And the new direct-reduction shaft turns the baked pellets into spongy iron without ever fully melting them.

docs.title: Steelmaking — burning the carbon back out
docs.text:
Fresh-from-the-furnace iron (pig iron) has way too much carbon mixed in, which makes it brittle, like a dry cracker. To turn it into steel, you blow air or oxygen through the hot metal to burn most of that carbon back out. Conduvia models the three ways that actually matter:

Furnace · station Charge How Tier Bessemer (existing) pig iron air blast burns out C (historical) T2 Open hearth (existing) pig iron + coal slow, controllable bath T3 Basic Oxygen (BOF / LD) pig iron + ~25 % scrap + burnt lime pure-O₂ blow, autogenous heat, basic slag pulls P & S T3 Electric Arc (EAF) sponge iron + scrap + lime electric arc melts a cold charge — the recycling route T4

The BOF (basic oxygen furnace) makes most of the world's brand-new steel. The EAF (electric arc furnace) is the recycling one — it melts down old scrap metal with a giant electric spark. Both are in the game, and both turn steel scrap into a real ingredient you can gather out in the world: old rails, busted machines, and demolition junk, exactly like real recyclers do.

docs.title: Stainless — keeping the chromium
docs.text:
Stainless steel is just steel with chromium mixed in — that's the part that stops it from rusting. But there's a catch: if you blow oxygen through the hot metal to burn out the carbon, you burn away the pricey chromium too. The fix is a machine called the AOD converter. It mixes the oxygen with a do-nothing gas (argon) so the carbon burns off while the chromium stays put. Real factories make about three-quarters of all stainless steel this exact way.

2 steel + ferrochrome + ferronickel + burnt lime → 2 stainless + slag

Ferroalloys have their own chains

The AOD step consumes ferrochrome and ferronickel. Both now have honest, furnace-deep upstreams — proper chromite→ferrochrome and laterite/sulfide→ferronickel chains — documented in the Ferroalloys field guide.

docs.title: Byproduct & cross-chain loops
docs.text:
• Slag — every time you melt metal, a layer of glassy junk floats on top. That's slag. Don't toss it; it's handy as building filler.
  • Tailings — the leftover worthless rock from cleaning the ore.
  • Sulfur gas off the sinter line feeds the sulfuric-acid factory.
  • Roasted pyrite leftovers from the acid chain are basically ready-made iron ore — drop them straight back into ironmaking.
  • Sponge iron melts down into nearly pure iron, great for electromagnet cores and clean mixing stock.

docs.title: The chain at a glance
docs.text:
flowchart TD
  MAG["crushed magnetite"] -->|LIMS| CONC["iron concentrate"]
  HEM["crushed hematite"] -->|mag-roast + sep| CONC
  CONC -->|sinter strand| SINT["sinter"]
  CONC -->|pelletizer| PEL["pellets"]
  SINT -->|blast furnace| PIG["pig iron"]
  PEL -->|DRI shaft| DRI["sponge iron"]
  PIG -->|BOF + scrap + lime| STEEL["steel"]
  DRI -->|EAF + scrap + lime| STEEL
  SCRAP["salvaged scrap"] --> STEEL
  STEEL -->|hot rolling| PLATE["plate / beam / rail"]
  STEEL -->|AOD + ferroalloys| SS["stainless"]
  DRI -->|remelt| PURE["pure iron"]
  SINT -.SO2.-> ACID["→ sulfuric acid"]

Tier ladder

Bloomery (T1) → blast furnace + Bessemer + beneficiation/sinter (T2) → BOF + pellets + DRI + open hearth (T3) → EAF + AOD stainless (T4). Hot rolling rides on top to turn ingots into plate, beam, and rail — the parts the rest of the tree actually consumes.

docs.title: Lead — galena, and the silver that rides with it
docs.text:
Lead is the easy metal to melt out of its rock — and the fun one to clean up. The ore (galena) gives up its lead without much fuss, but there's a twist: that fresh lead always comes with a little silver hidden inside it. Getting the silver back out is the coolest trick in the chain — you stir in some zinc, and the silver ditches the lead to go hang out with the zinc instead. (Chemists call this the Parkes process.)

flowchart TD
  ore["galena (PbS)"] -->|"froth flotation"| conc["concentrate"]
  conc -->|"sinter-roast"| sint["lead oxide sinter"]
  conc -.->|"SO2"| acid["acid plant"]
  sint -->|"blast furnace + coke"| bull["crude bullion (Ag-bearing)"]
  zinc["zinc metal"] --> parkes
  bull -->|"Parkes desilver"| parkes{"skim"}
  parkes --> soft["softened lead"]
  parkes --> crust["silver-zinc crust"]
  crust -->|"cupellation"| dore["silver dore"]
  crust -.->|"litharge PbO"| lith["flux / recycle"]
  soft -->|"fire refine + cast"| ingot["lead ingot"]

docs.title: From rock to raw lead
docs.text: First you float the ore with bubbles to get a clean concentrate (the copper guide explains that bubble trick). Then you roast it, which burns off the sulfur as a gas for the acid plant. Finally you melt it down with coke in a tall furnace to get raw lead. The leftover rock floats off as glassy slag — and almost all the silver tags along inside the lead.

docs.title: Getting the silver back out (the clever bit)
docs.text: Silver would much rather stick to zinc than to lead. So you stir a little zinc into the molten lead and let it cool. The silver chases the zinc up to the top, where it freezes into a crust you scoop off. The lead left behind is now silver-free. To get the silver on its own, you heat that crust with a blast of air, which burns the zinc and lead away and leaves a shiny button of silver behind. That's why the lead refinery quietly eats zinc from your zinc chain.

docs.title: Honest notes
docs.text:
• The silver is a real byproduct, not a lucky bonus drop — it genuinely rides through the smelter inside the lead and comes out at the end.
  • The lead-oxide leftover (litharge) isn't trash: you can feed it back to the furnace, and it's also a real ingredient in glass and pottery glaze.
  • Where real lead ends up: radiation shielding, car batteries, and acid-plant pipes.

docs.title: Lithium — spodumene to battery salts
docs.text:
Tier 4 · Branch · Pack T4_Lithium.luau

Lithium is the lightest metal there is, and the one the whole world is scrambling for right now — it's what makes phone and car batteries work. Here it starts as raw spodumene rock and ends as the two lithium salts that actually go into battery factories: lithium hydroxide and lithium carbonate.

The whole chain hangs on one fact most games skip: you can't just dissolve raw spodumene. Fresh out of the ground the crystal is stubborn — acid won't touch it. You have to bake it first to rearrange the crystal into a form acid can get into.

docs.title: Flow
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    ore["raw_ore_spodumene (alpha)"] -->|decrepitate ~1100 C| beta["beta-spodumene"]
    beta -->|sulfuric acid bake| crude["crude lithium sulfate"]
    acid["acid_sulfuric"] --> crude
    crude -->|water leach + lime| liquor["lithium sulfate liquor"]
    lime["burned_lime_quicklime"] --> liquor
    liquor -->|causticize NaOH| lioh["lithium hydroxide"]
    naoh["sodium_hydroxide"] --> lioh
    lioh -->|carbonate CO2| li2co3["lithium carbonate"]
    co2["gas_co2"] --> li2co3
    lioh --> sulfate["salt_sodium_sulfate (byproduct)"]

docs.title: Steps
docs.text: # Recipe Station In Out 1 li_decrepitate Rotary Calciner Kiln 2 spodumene 2 beta-spodumene 2 li_acid_bake Sulfation Acid-Roast Kiln 2 beta + 1 H₂SO₄ 2 crude Li sulfate + tailings 3 li_leach_purify Heap Leach Pad 2 crude + 1 quicklime 2 Li sulfate liquor + tailings 4 li_causticize Caustic Digester Autoclave 2 liquor + 2 NaOH 2 LiOH + 1 Na₂SO₄ 5 li_carbonate Carbonation Reactor 2 LiOH + 1 CO₂ 1 Li₂CO₃

docs.title: Why it's built this way
docs.text:
• The bake is the secret gate. Fresh spodumene just shrugs off acid. Roasting it near 1100 °C rearranges the crystal into an open form that finally lets the lithium come loose. It looks like a pointless energy cost until you realize nothing downstream works without it — exactly the "why won't this react?!" puzzle the game loves.
  • You bake it with acid, you don't soak it. The rearranged rock is baked together with strong acid, not just dunked in watery acid. The lithium hops onto the acid and leaves the rest of the rock behind.
  • Two real products. Treat it with lye and you get lithium hydroxide (best for the newest batteries); bubble in carbon dioxide and you get lithium carbonate (the classic one). Real battery makers use both.
  • A dead item comes alive. One leftover here (sodium sulfate) used to appear out of nowhere in the game — now it has an honest source, and it's genuinely useful in glass and detergent.

docs.title: Byproducts & sinks
docs.text:
• salt_sodium_sulfate — recovered salt, not waste (glass/detergent).
  • lithium_hydroxide / lithium_carbonate — terminal battery feedstocks; the future hook is a lithium-ion cell chain that consumes them.

Verified against Wikipedia (Lithium extraction, Spodumene, Lithium hydroxide, Lithium carbonate) and standard lithium-process references.

docs.title: Magnesium — the lightest structural metal, two ways
docs.text:
Magnesium is all around us — there's tons of it dissolved in seawater and locked into common rock — but you can't just smelt it like iron. Its ore clings to oxygen too hard for carbon to pry loose, and any magnesium you do free will instantly catch fire in the furnace fumes and turn right back into ore. So the world makes magnesium two completely different ways, and Conduvia builds both. They both finish at the same magnesium ingot.

flowchart TD
  dol["dolomite"] -->|"calcine"| dolime["dolime MgO·CaO"]
  sil["silica + iron + coke"] -->|"SAF"| fesi["ferrosilicon"]
  dolime -->|"Pidgeon vacuum retort"| crown["Mg crown"]
  fesi --> crown
  crown -->|"flux remelt + cast"| ingA["magnesium ingot"]
  brine["brine"] -->|"+ lime, precipitate"| moh["Mg(OH)2"]
  moh -->|"+ Cl2 + C"| mgcl2["anhydrous MgCl2"]
  mgcl2 -->|"fused-salt electrolysis"| ingB["magnesium ingot"]
  ingB -.->|"Cl2 recycled"| mgcl2

docs.title: Route A — the Pidgeon process (squeeze it out with silicon)
docs.text:
The main modern way. You roast dolomite rock to drive off its carbon dioxide, leaving a chalky powder. Separately, you make ferrosilicon (an iron-and-silicon mix that doubles as a handy steel ingredient). Then, in a sealed, air-free tube heated to about 1200 °C, the silicon yanks the magnesium loose:

2 (MgO·CaO) + Si → 2 Mg↑ + Ca₂SiO₄

The freed magnesium drifts to the cold end of the tube and freezes into a chunky ring called a crown; the leftover rock stays behind as slag. Because magnesium burns in open air, you remelt the crown under a protective blanket of salt and cast it into an ingot.

docs.title: Route B — split it with electricity (straight from seawater)
docs.text: Stir lime into magnesium-rich salt water and a magnesium powder settles out. You turn that into a dry magnesium-and-chlorine salt, then melt it and run electricity through it: magnesium collects on one side, chlorine gas comes off the other — and you pipe that chlorine straight back to reuse it, just like the salt loop in the titanium chain.

docs.title: Honest notes
docs.text:
• There's no cheating shortcut here — both routes respect why magnesium is genuinely hard to make.
  • Ferrosilicon shows up here as a real new alloy; it's also used in steelmaking to clean up the steel.
  • The chlorine in the electric route gets recycled; the very first batch comes from your salt-water (chlor-alkali) plant.
  • Magnesium can itself be the metal that frees titanium and zirconium — so this gives those chains a magnesium-powered alternative to sodium.

docs.title: Natural gas & helium — the only place helium comes from
docs.text:
Natural gas looks like nothing more than a fuel, but the raw gas straight from the well is a little chemistry set all by itself. It's mostly methane, mixed with carbon dioxide and toxic, rotten-egg-smelling hydrogen sulfide — and, in a lucky few gas fields, a tiny trace of helium. There's nowhere else on Earth to get helium cheaply: each atom has been trapped underground since the planet formed, and if you don't catch it here it floats up into the air and off into space, gone for good.

flowchart LR
  well["sour natural gas"] -->|"amine sweetening"| ch4["pipeline methane"]
  well --> co2["CO2"]
  well --> h2s["hydrogen sulfide"]
  h2s -->|"Claus process"| s["recovered sulfur"]
  s -->|"burn in air"| so2["SO2 → sulfuric acid"]
  ch4 -->|"cryo N2 rejection"| che["crude helium"]
  che -->|"PSA purify"| he["helium 99.999%"]
  he -->|"compress"| cyl["helium cylinder"]
  ch4 -->|"steam reforming"| syn["syngas (CO + H2)"]
  syn -->|"water-gas shift"| h2["hydrogen"]

docs.title: Three things come out of one gas field
1. Sulfur (from the poison)
docs.text: The toxic hydrogen sulfide pulled out while cleaning the gas would kill you — but the Claus process turns it into harmless bright-yellow sulfur: 2 H2S + O2 -> 2 S + 2 H2O. This isn't a party trick: most of the world's sulfur is recovered this way from gas and oil, not dug out of the ground. Burn that sulfur (S + O2 -> SO2) and you've got a cleaner feed for the sulfuric-acid plant than roasting ore — so the gas field quietly feeds the acid chain.

docs.title: 2. Helium (the whole reason to bother)
docs.text: After cleaning, the gas is chilled down so cold that everything except helium turns to liquid. What's left over is rough helium, which then gets polished up to 99.999% pure and bottled. You have to run an enormous amount of gas to collect a little helium — an honest reflection of why helium is pricey and worth saving.

docs.title: 3. Hydrogen (the second road)
docs.text: The cleaned methane can also be cooked with steam to crack hydrogen out of it: CH4 + H2O -> CO + 3 H2, and a second step squeezes out even more: CO + H2O -> CO2 + H2. This is where most of the world's hydrogen — and therefore most of its ammonia — really comes from. It gives Conduvia a genuine alternative to getting hydrogen from the salt-water plant.

docs.title: Honest notes
docs.text:
• Helium is the one common industrial gas with no way to make it — this chain deliberately has just this single source, and the cylinder seals matter because helium leaks through almost anything.
  • Cooking hydrogen out of methane is cheap, but it makes carbon dioxide, so it isn't clean hydrogen. The salt-water route is cleaner but costs more — both are in the tree on purpose.
  • Sulfur recovered from gas and sulfur burned from ore both end up feeding the same acid plant, exactly as in the real world.

docs.title: Nickel — the sulfide route (matte smelting)
docs.text:
There are two ways to get nickel out of the ground. One of them (from a clay-like ore) is already covered in the ferroalloys guide. This is the other half: nickel from a sulfur-rich ore, cleaned up by melting it down — the deeper route, and the one that comes with an acid plant bolted onto its side.

flowchart TD
  ore["pentlandite ore"] -->|"froth flotation"| con["nickel concentrate"]
  con -->|"flash smelt (+SiO2, O2)"| matte["furnace matte"]
  matte -->|"Peirce-Smith converter"| bm["converter matte Ni3S2"]
  bm -->|"dead roast"| nio["nickel oxide sinter"]
  nio -->|"electrowin"| cath["nickel cathode"]
  matte -.->|"SO2"| acid["contact acid plant"]
  bm -.->|"SO2"| acid
  nio -.->|"SO2"| acid
  cath --> inc["Inconel"]

docs.title: Float it
docs.text: You grind the ore and float it with bubbles: the nickel-bearing grains stick to the air bubbles and ride up as a froth you skim off, while the worthless rock sinks. This cleans up the ore before you spend any fuel on it.

docs.title: Melt it (and let it burn itself)
docs.text: The cleaned ore is blasted with oxygen-rich air so hot that it basically burns itself — no extra fuel needed. The iron floats off as glassy slag, the sulfur leaves as a gas, and a molten nickel layer (called matte) collects at the bottom.

docs.title: Blow air through it
docs.text: Blasting air through that molten matte burns out the leftover iron, leaving a richer nickel matte behind — plus more sulfur gas.

docs.title: Roast and plate
docs.text: A final roast burns off the last of the sulfur to leave nickel oxide, and then electricity plates it out as pure nickel metal. That's the same nickel the other route is chasing, and it's exactly what you need for the heat-proof alloy Inconel.

docs.title: Why there's always an acid plant next door
docs.text: Three different steps here give off sulfur gas, and all of it gets piped to the sulfuric-acid plant. This isn't a bonus — it's just how it really works: you can't roast sulfur ores at any real scale without catching that sulfur, so a nickel (or copper) smelter always has an acid plant as its partner.

docs.title: Honest notes
docs.text:
• Real sulfide ores also carry cobalt, copper and platinum-group metals recovered alongside nickel; here we keep the SO₂ and slag byproducts and leave the minor metals as a documented simplification.
  • The laterite/RKEF route is untouched; both routes converge on the same nickel_cathode.

docs.title: Niobium & tantalum — the inseparable twins
docs.text:
Niobium and tantalum are just about the most alike pair of metals there is — a quirk of physics makes a tantalum atom almost exactly the same size as a niobium one — so they always turn up together in the same ore and flatly refuse to be told apart. Telling them apart is the whole story here.

flowchart TD
  ore["columbite ore"] -->|"HF digest"| liq["crude Nb/Ta fluoride"]
  liq -->|"MIBK solvent extraction"| nbf["niobium fluoride"]
  liq -->|"MIBK solvent extraction"| taf["tantalum fluoride"]
  nbf -->|"calcine (+HF recovered)"| nbo["Nb2O5"]
  nbo -->|"aluminothermic + Fe2O3"| fenb["ferroniobium"]
  taf -->|"calcine (+HF recovered)"| tao["Ta2O5"]
  tao -->|"aluminothermic reduce"| tap["tantalum powder"]
  tap -->|"press, sinter, anodise"| cap["tantalum capacitor"]

docs.title: Step 1: dissolve it
docs.text: The ore is dissolved in hot hydrofluoric acid — the one acid nasty enough to eat these stubborn minerals. Both metals dissolve together, and the worthless rock is left behind as waste.

docs.title: The key step: telling the twins apart
docs.text: The mixed liquid runs through a column where a special solvent grabs the tantalum and lets the niobium stay behind — finally splitting the two apart. This is one of the hardest routine separations in all of metalworking, and it's exactly why tantalum is so expensive.

docs.title: Making the niobium useful
docs.text: The niobium half is baked into an oxide (the acid is caught and reused), then reacted with aluminum and iron to pull out the metal: 3 Nb₂O₅ + 10 Al → 6 Nb + 5 Al₂O₃. Running it with iron means the product comes out already blended with iron — which is exactly how this alloy is made in real life. A pinch of it makes stronger steel; more of it helps high-temperature alloys like Inconel hold their shape under stress.

docs.title: Making the tantalum useful
docs.text: The tantalum half is baked into an oxide (again, the acid is recycled), pulled out as a fine powder, then pressed and treated into a tantalum capacitor — a tiny electronics part that stores charge. Tantalum makes the smallest, most reliable capacitors there are, and that's the payoff that makes the whole tricky separation worth the trouble.

docs.title: Honest notes
docs.text:
• The hydrofluoric acid is caught and reused at both baking steps, not thrown away.
  • The iron-niobium blend is made in a single step (oxide + aluminum + iron oxide), not by melting niobium metal — that's how it's really produced.
  • Niobium and tantalum share one ore and one separation column; giving tantalum a capacitor to become turns a former dead-end leftover into a reason to mine in the first place.

docs.title: Obsidian — volcanic glass to a surgical blade
docs.text:
Tier 1–3 · Branch · Pack T1_Obsidian.luau

Obsidian is natural glass — lava that cooled so fast it never grew any crystal grains. With no grain to get in the way, it snaps along perfectly smooth lines, so a freshly chipped edge is unbelievably sharp: only a few atoms wide at the very tip, finer than the best steel scalpel and getting close to a diamond. That's why a few surgeons still use obsidian blades for delicate cuts that heal with less scarring. It started life as a dead raw_ore_obsidian; now the payoff is a real precision tool, not survival filler.

docs.title: Flow
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    ore["raw_ore_obsidian"] -->|knap (hammerstone)| flake["obsidian flake"]
    ore --> deb["stone debitage (byproduct)"]
    flake -->|pressure-flake (wood billet)| blade["pressure-flaked blade"]
    flake -->|shape| arrow["obsidian dart point"]
    blade -->|haft to handle| scalpel["obsidian scalpel"]

docs.title: Steps
docs.text: # Recipe Station In Out 1 ob_knap_obsidian Knapping Station 2 raw obsidian 3 flake + 1 stone debitage 2 ob_pressure_blade Knapping Station 2 flake + 1 wood 1 pressure-flaked blade 3 ob_make_arrowhead Knapping Station 1 flake 2 dart point 4 ob_make_scalpel Carving Bench 1 blade + 1 wood 1 obsidian scalpel

docs.title: Why it's built this way
docs.text:
• No grain means the sharpest edge in the game. Because obsidian is glass with no grain, it shears along perfectly smooth lines. Straight off the hammerstone a flake is already sharper than knapped flint.
  • Pressure-flaking fine-tunes the edge. Pressing tiny chips off with a wood or antler tool brings the edge down to just a few atoms wide. The tool gets used up doing it.
  • The scalpel is the real-world payoff. Fitted to a carved handle, the tiny blade slips between cells instead of tearing them, so the cut is exceptionally clean and heals with little swelling — a genuine precision tool, and a high-tier use for an otherwise primitive rock.
  • Brilliant but brittle. Obsidian is sharper than steel but shatters on impact — great for fine cutting, useless for chopping.

docs.title: Byproducts & sinks
docs.text:
• stone debitage (stone_flakes) — shared knapping byproduct with flint.
  • dart point (obsidian_arrowhead) — hunting/ceremony projectile.
  • obsidian scalpel (obsidian_scalpel) — precision/surgical instrument; the chain's terminal high-value product.

Verified 2026-06-05 against CNN/NCBI on obsidian scalpel edge geometry (~30 Å vs 300–600 Å for steel), the PubMed obsidian-vs-steel wound-healing study, Fine Science Tools' wood-handled obsidian scalpel product, and Wikipedia (Obsidian, Pressure flaking, Knapping).

docs.title: Ore & Material Field Guide
docs.text:
Found something you don’t recognise? Pick the deposit type it came from, then match what you see in the world. Every entry shows what it looks like in-game, how to refine it, and what it leads to.

Salt Flat & Crystal Deposits 3

White crusts, salt pans, and layered crystal beds in dry basins. Halite, gypsum, sylvite.

Metal Ore Veins 4

Brassy, metallic, or dark minerals in quartz veins. Green staining = copper nearby. Rusty cap = look below. Chalcopyrite, galena, sphalerite, pyrite.

Iron Ores & Bulk Rock 5

The big feedstocks: iron for steel, limestone for cement, silica for glass, bauxite for aluminium. Usually massive, not small veins. Hematite, magnetite, bauxite, limestone, silica.

Surface & River Finds 5

Materials you can grab with no mining at all: riverbed pebbles, bog nodules, surface float. Flint, obsidian, native copper, bog iron, cassiterite.

Deep & Instrument Finds 3

Late-game materials you won't identify by eye alone. Spodumene (lithium), rare earth ore, uraninite.

docs.title: Halite (Rock Salt)
docs.text:
Real-world uses · Food seasoning; chemical feedstock (chlorine/caustic soda); de-icing.

Spot it · Clear to white cubic crystals; can be reddish or orange from mineral impurities. Soft; breaks into clean cube cleavage fragments.

Refine it

  • At Brine Treatment Unit → BSbrine solution
  • At Electric Arc Furnace → SMsodium metal CGchlorine gas
Geology & field notes

Geology · Evaporite deposits in arid basins -- precipitates from drying seawater or saline lakes; massive bedded layers or salt domes.

Typical Size / Scale · Huge stratified beds hundreds of meters thick; salt domes can be kilometers across. Often mined underground via room-and-pillar methods, or produced via evaporation ponds.

Landmark / Recognition · White crusts on dry lakebeds; polygon-cracked pans; little to no vegetation. Outcrops are rare in wet climates because salt dissolves readily -- an arid basin with white crust is the primary cue.

In Conduvia · Expect bright-white flats and subtle cube-crystal clusters in desert terrain. Cracked salt-pan plates; ground may behave differently underfoot.

docs.title: Gypsum (Calcium Sulfate)
docs.text:
Real-world uses · Plaster and drywall; cement additive; soil conditioner.

Spot it · White or gray chalky massive rock; can be clear (selenite) or fibrous (satin spar). Very soft -- scratches with a fingernail; can form bladed crystals or desert rose clusters.

Refine it

  • At Rotary Calciner Kiln → POplaster of Paris
  • At Rotary Calciner Kiln → CCcement clinker
  • At Cement Ball Mill → PCPortland cement

Leads to · GBgypsum board (drywall)

Geology & field notes

Geology · Evaporite layers interbedded with limestone, shale, and halite; forms during evaporation or hydration of anhydrite; also from saline groundwater and hot springs.

Typical Size / Scale · Thick beds meters to tens of meters; common open-pit quarries. Crystals can be exceptionally large in rare pockets -- meter scale in some localities.

Landmark / Recognition · White slopes and cliffs; crumbly scratchable outcrop; powdery crust soils; sparse vegetation from soil salinity.

In Conduvia · Light gray and white seams and layered bands in sedimentary terrain. Rare selenite pockets expose translucent blade-like crystal clusters.

docs.title: Sylvite (Potash Ore)
docs.text:
Real-world uses · Fertilizer potassium source; potassium chemical feedstock.

Spot it · Colorless to pale orange or red cubic crystals; often pinkish with mineral impurities.

Refine it

  • At Froth Flotation Cell → MOmuriate of potash (KCl) SLsalt lick
Geology & field notes

Geology · Late-stage evaporite in closed basins -- forms after extreme evaporation; interlayered with halite and anhydrite in ancient evaporite sequences.

Typical Size / Scale · Localized beds and lenses (meters) within thick salt sequences; mined underground or via solution methods.

Landmark / Recognition · Rarely exposed at surface; occurs in association with other evaporite minerals deeper in arid basin terrain.

In Conduvia · Deep in salt-bearing terrain, look for rare pinkish cube-crystal clusters nested within halite layers.

docs.title: Chalcopyrite (Copper Iron Sulfide)
docs.text:
Real-world uses · Copper ore -- primary source of copper for wiring, electronics, and alloys.

Spot it · Brassy yellow metallic mineral; tarnishes iridescent purple, blue, or green. Softer than pyrite; often massive and grainy with rare well-formed crystals.

Refine it

  • At Trip Hammer → CCcrushed chalcopyrite
  • At Electric Jaw Crusher → CCcrushed chalcopyrite
  • At Trip Hammer → CCchalcopyrite concentrate

Leads to · RCrefined copper ingot CCcopper cathode

Geology & field notes

Geology · Hydrothermal veins and disseminations in igneous intrusions; common in porphyry copper systems and massive sulfide settings.

Typical Size / Scale · Veins: cm to m thick. Porphyry systems: huge low-grade volumes (km-scale pit type). Often mined as large bulk-rock operations when found in porphyry style.

Landmark / Recognition · Green malachite and blue azurite stains in oxidized zones. Rusty gossan caps can indicate sulfides below; quartz veins with brassy specks are a reliable surface tell.

In Conduvia · Yellow-gold metallic chunks embedded in quartz or stone veins, with green staining around them. Green staining near a vein is a reliable indicator of a copper system -- learn to read it.

docs.title: Galena (Lead Sulfide)
docs.text:
Real-world uses · Lead ore (batteries, shielding); often carries significant silver value.

Spot it · Lead-gray metallic cubes with perfect cubic cleavage; very heavy for size. Soft; scratches easily; fresh faces are shiny, tarnished surfaces go dull.

Refine it

  • At Froth Flotation Cell → GCgalena concentrate
  • At Rocker Box → GCgalena concentrate
  • At Sluice Box → GCgalena concentrate
  • At Electric Ball Mill → GGground galena
Geology & field notes

Geology · Low to mid temperature hydrothermal veins, especially in limestone and dolostone (MVT style); often with sphalerite, fluorite, and calcite.

Typical Size / Scale · Veins usually under 1 m thick but laterally extensive; replacement bodies can be tens of meters. Common underground mining geometry.

Landmark / Recognition · White coatings in weathered zones (anglesite and cerussite). Often found in limestone caves and veins alongside calcite and fluorite.

In Conduvia · Heavy dark-gray cube chunks embedded in limestone veins. One of the densest ores you'll carry -- noticeably heavier than its size suggests.

docs.title: Sphalerite (Zinc Sulfide)
docs.text:
Real-world uses · Zinc ore -- galvanizing, alloys and brass, batteries; may carry minor cadmium and indium.

Spot it · Brown to honey-yellow resinous crystals; iron-rich varieties are black. Translucent when pure; freshly broken faces can carry a faint sulfur smell.

Refine it

  • At Electric Ball Mill → GSground sphalerite
  • At Froth Flotation Cell → SCsphalerite concentrate

Leads to · ZIzinc ingot ZSzinc sulfate liquor

Geology & field notes

Geology · Common with galena in limestone-hosted deposits (MVT) and in VMS systems with copper minerals.

Typical Size / Scale · Vein and replacement bodies similar to galena; the two minerals are often intimately mixed.

Landmark / Recognition · Oxidized zones may show pale green smithsonite coatings. Look for shiny brown-black chips in limestone vugs near galena cubes -- they commonly occur together.
