In Conduvia · Often occurs in the same vein district as galena -- if you found one, check the surrounding area. Resinous brown to black crystal chunks with a distinct glossy surface.

docs.title: Pyrite (Iron Sulfide -- Fool's Gold)
docs.text:
Real-world uses · Not a modern iron ore; historically used for sulfur and sparks; a major indicator mineral for nearby metal systems.

Spot it · Brassy yellow metallic cubes with characteristic striations; harder than chalcopyrite. Often forms nodules ("pyrite suns") or tiny glitter points disseminated in shale.

Refine it

  • At Sulfur Burner / Pyrite Roaster → RHroasted hematite

Leads to · IBiron bloom

Geology & field notes

Geology · Extremely common sulfide in hydrothermal, igneous, metamorphic, and sedimentary settings; forms cubes, pyritohedra, and massive granular zones.

Typical Size / Scale · Scattered grains to massive ore bodies; veins can be meters thick.

Landmark / Recognition · Weathering creates rusty gossans and orange-stained drainage. In outcrop: tiny gold-cube glitter; in weathered zones: red-brown iron staining.

In Conduvia · Pyrite alone isn't the prize -- but where it appears, more valuable sulfides are often nearby. Rusty orange drainage stains and gossan caps are your environmental cue when prospecting.

docs.title: Hematite (Iron Oxide)
docs.text:
Real-world uses · Primary iron ore for steel; also a red pigment (ochre).

Spot it · Specular hematite: steel-gray or black shiny flakes. Earthy hematite: brick-red dull masses; diagnostic reddish-brown streak when scratched.

Refine it

  • At Stone Mortar → ROred ochre pigment
  • At Roasting Pit → RHroasted hematite
  • At Trip Hammer → CHcrushed hematite
  • At Electric Jaw Crusher → CHcrushed hematite

Leads to · IBiron bloom PIpig iron ingot

Geology & field notes

Geology · Commonly from banded iron formations (BIFs) and enrichment and weathering zones; often associated with magnetite and goethite.

Typical Size / Scale · Can form enormous formation-scale deposits; open pits hundreds of meters deep. Also occurs as smaller ironstone beds or residual boulders.

Landmark / Recognition · Red soils; striped red and gray banding in BIF cliffs. Leaves rust-red powder when scratched -- a reliable hand test.

In Conduvia · Red-brown seams and banded layers on cliff faces; banded iron formations show alternating red and gray stripes. Mining raises red dust -- a visual tell distinct from other iron ores.

docs.title: Magnetite (Iron Oxide)
docs.text:
Real-world uses · Iron ore; also produces natural magnetism (the basis of historic lodestone compasses).

Spot it · Black metallic to dull mineral; granular to octahedral crystals. Magnetic -- the single clearest field identification test.

Refine it

  • At Trip Hammer → CMcrushed magnetite
  • At Electric Jaw Crusher → CMcrushed magnetite
Geology & field notes

Geology · Occurs in igneous and metamorphic rocks, layered intrusions, skarns, and iron formations; also concentrates as black sand placers.

Typical Size / Scale · Seams in intrusions (meters thick) or massive iron formation and taconite systems. Black sands can blanket beaches and river bars.

Landmark / Recognition · A magnet sticks to it; black sand streaks on beaches and river bars are a surface indicator. Dense concentrations can interfere with compass readings.

In Conduvia · Black ore lumps or octahedral crystal clusters. A compass near a magnetite deposit will behave unreliably -- a useful prospecting cue.

docs.title: Bauxite (Aluminum Ore)
docs.text:
Real-world uses · Primary aluminum ore (alumina to aluminum); can carry gallium as a byproduct.

Spot it · Red-brown earthy mass with pisolites (pea-sized pellets). Clay-like texture; mottled whites, greys, and browns; no large crystals.

Refine it

  • At Trip Hammer → CBcrushed bauxite
  • At Electric Jaw Crusher → CBcrushed bauxite
Geology & field notes

Geology · Tropical laterite weathering concentrate of aluminum hydroxides; forms blanket-like soil profiles over suitable parent rock.

Typical Size / Scale · A few to tens of meters thick but spread over enormous plateaus; typically strip-mined.

Landmark / Recognition · Distinct red lateritic soils; soft and diggable; the pea-sized pisolite pellets are the diagnostic feature.

In Conduvia · Found as surface patches in tropical and humid biomes -- thin coverage but broad extent. Breaks into distinctive pea-sized pellets, visually unlike any other ore you'll encounter at surface.

docs.title: Limestone (Calcium Carbonate Rock)
docs.text:
Real-world uses · Steelmaking flux; cement and concrete backbone; soil pH neutralizer; lime source.

Spot it · Gray to cream massive rock, often with fossils visible on weathered surfaces. Fresh break looks sugary-crystalline; reacts with acid if present.

Refine it

  • At Trip Hammer → LFlimestone flux BLburned lime (quicklime)
  • At Hand Crafting → LNlimestone nodule
  • At Trip Hammer → LFlimestone flux
Geology & field notes

Geology · Sedimentary carbonate rock from marine shelves and reefs; often fossil-bearing; can form karst caves and host replacement ores.

Typical Size / Scale · Formation-scale layers spanning many kilometers; cliffs and plateaus; enormous quarries.

Landmark / Recognition · Karst terrain: sinkholes, caves, and springs. Sheer light-colored cliffs; thin soils on outcrop.

In Conduvia · Large continuous light-gray layers forming cliffs and cave systems. Fossil imprints on exposed faces are a clear identifier -- look for them on freshly broken surfaces.

docs.title: Quartz / Silica (Sand or Quartzite)
docs.text:
Real-world uses · Glass and foundry sand; ceramics; silicon pipeline starter; quartzite as a high-grade silica source.

Spot it · Sand: pale cream or golden gritty grains; often carries a glassy sparkle. Quartzite: hard, sugary crystalline rock; quartz crystals: clear or milky hexagonal prisms.

Refine it

  • At Reverberatory Furnace → CMcopper matte
  • At Copper Converter (Peirce-Smith) → BCblister copper
  • At Rotary Calciner Kiln → CCcement clinker
  • At Submerged Arc Furnace → F(ferrosilicon (FeSi) CDcarbon dioxide gas
  • At Flash Smelting Furnace → NFnickel furnace matte
  • At Submerged Arc Furnace → MGmetallurgical-grade silicon CDcarbon dioxide gas

Leads to · BGborosilicate glass tube FGflat glass sheet GFglass-fiber roving PCPortland cement CIcopper ingot

Geology & field notes

Geology · Quartz-rich sands from beaches and dunes and clean sandstones; quartzite is metamorphosed sandstone; quartz veins also common.

Typical Size / Scale · Dune fields can be vast; sandstones hundreds of meters thick; quartzite ridges run for kilometers.

Landmark / Recognition · Light-colored beaches and river sands; quartzite stands as resistant ridges; quartz veins cut across rock as white ribbons.

In Conduvia · Appears in two main forms: loose sand patches along riverbeds and desert flats, and quartz crystal veins cutting through rock. River-gathered sand is easier to access; vein quartz yields higher purity material.

docs.title: Flint (Chert)
docs.text:
Real-world uses · Stone Age toolmaking material; fire-starting sparks; sharp edges from conchoidal fracture.

Spot it · Dark gray, brown, or black waxy luster; conchoidal fracture produces razor-sharp edges. Often has a white chalky outer rind (cortex) if derived from nodules.

Refine it

  • At Knapping Station → FFflint flake
  • At Workbench → FAflint-and-steel striker
  • At Rotary Calciner Kiln → CPcalcined potters’ flint
Geology & field notes

Geology · Microcrystalline quartz in chalk and limestone nodules and chert beds; often of biological silica origin in marine sediments.

Typical Size / Scale · Nodules: golf ball to football size; bedded chert forms thin but laterally persistent layers.

Landmark / Recognition · Black nodules in white chalk; sharp flakes scattered on ground near chalk and gravel exposures. Resistant chert ribs outcrop where surrounding limestone has eroded away.

In Conduvia · Small black and brown nodules embedded in limestone and chalk layers. Striking flint against another hard stone produces sharp shards -- useful for early tools and firestarting.

docs.title: Obsidian (Volcanic Glass)
docs.text:
Real-world uses · Ultra-sharp blade material; volcanic glass formed by rapid cooling of silica-rich lava.

Spot it · Jet black glassy rock; conchoidal fracture; extremely sharp edges; brittle. Can be banded or bubble-rich; reflective in direct light.

Refine it

  • At Knapping Station → OFobsidian flake
Geology & field notes

Geology · Forms at rhyolite domes and flow margins where lava cools too fast to crystallize; often near pumice and light rhyolite.

Typical Size / Scale · Flows and domes can be hill-sized; outcrops meters to hundreds of meters thick and kilometers long.

Landmark / Recognition · Black glossy blocky flow surfaces; minimal vegetation on fresh flows; abundant sharp shards on the ground.

In Conduvia · Found as jet-black glassy boulders and flow surfaces in volcanic terrain. The edges of freshly broken obsidian are exceptionally sharp -- a prized early cutting material. Rare but unmistakable once you've seen it.

docs.title: Native Copper (Elemental Copper)
docs.text:
Real-world uses · The earliest copper route -- cold-workable metal nuggets and masses; later smelted.

Spot it · Reddish metallic masses or wire forms; tarnish to green or black with age; malachite and azurite coatings common.

Refine it

  • At Clay Crucible → CIcopper ingot
  • At Clay Crucible → BIbronze ingot
  • At Stone Anvil → HChammered copper nugget
Geology & field notes

Geology · Occurs as native metal in basalt flows and sediment-hosted beds, and in oxidized zones of copper systems; often coated with green and blue copper minerals.

Typical Size / Scale · Nuggets from gram weight to fist size in gravels; rare giant masses have been found historically in certain districts. Can also occur as disseminations or plates within host rock.

Landmark / Recognition · Green malachite staining with a metallic red glint; heavy and malleable if hammered.

In Conduvia · Found near basalt cliffs or in river gravels as reddish metallic masses. Green malachite and blue azurite staining marks copper-bearing ground -- learn to read these weathering colors.

docs.title: Bog Iron (Limonite)
docs.text:
Real-world uses · Early iron source from wetlands; bloomery-friendly; renewable on long timescales.

Spot it · Rust-brown earthy nodules and crusts; often porous and mixed with organic material.

Refine it

  • At Roasting Pit → RBroasted bog iron

Leads to · IBiron bloom

Geology & field notes

Geology · Forms by precipitation of iron oxyhydroxides in bogs where iron-rich groundwater meets oxygenated, acidic surface conditions.

Typical Size / Scale · Surficial deposit in peat and sand near the water table; nodules range from pea to fist size. Deposit regenerates over decades -- a slow renewable resource.

Landmark / Recognition · Orange sediment visible in bog water; rusty soils; an oily-looking surface film from iron bacteria is a reliable tell.

In Conduvia · Gathered from bogs and wetland areas -- not mined from seams but scooped from iron-rich ground. A reliable early iron source before deeper deposits become accessible.

docs.title: Cassiterite (Tin Oxide)
docs.text:
Real-world uses · The primary tin ore; essential for bronze alloy. Found as heavy dark pebbles in stream gravels.

Spot it · Heavy, dark brown to black; sub-metallic lustre; short stubby prisms or rounded stream pebbles. Very dense -- sinks fast and settles ahead of lighter material in moving water; often associated with quartz and tourmaline.

Refine it

  • At Clay Crucible → TItin ingot
  • At Clay Crucible → BIbronze ingot
  • At Trip Hammer → CCcrushed cassiterite
  • At Electric Induction Furnace → TItin ingot
  • At Electric Induction Furnace → BIbronze ingot
Geology & field notes

Geology · Cassiterite (SnO₂) forms in high-temperature granite-related veins and pegmatites, then concentrates in placer gravels downstream -- the reason Bronze Age traders followed rivers to find it.

Typical Size / Scale · Hard-rock lodes (veins, pegmatites): narrow high-grade shoots. Alluvial placers: thin gravel lenses in stream beds and terrace gravels.

Landmark / Recognition · Dark heavy pebbles in clear-water stream gravels; black sand association with ilmenite and magnetite. Upstream hard-rock source: quartz-tourmaline veins in granite terrain.

In Conduvia · Shows up first as heavy dark pebbles in stream gravels -- the easier early find. Hard-rock vein deposits go deeper underground and offer a more consistent long-term source.

docs.title: Spodumene (Lithium Ore)
docs.text:
Real-world uses · Lithium for advanced batteries; also used in ceramics and glass inputs.

Spot it · Large prismatic crystals; pale green, pink, or white; distinctive blade-like cleavage. Pegmatite host is visibly coarse: oversized feldspar and mica.

Refine it

  • At Rotary Calciner Kiln → BSbeta-spodumene

Leads to · LSlithium sulfate liquor

Geology & field notes

Geology · Forms in granitic pegmatites (very coarse-grained dikes) with quartz, feldspar, and mica; crystals can be enormous.

Typical Size / Scale · Pegmatite dikes 1–20 m thick, hundreds of meters long; ore shoots tens of meters. Crystal size can be extreme in rare cases -- meter-scale specimens exist.

Landmark / Recognition · Coarse "chunky granite" outcrops; occasional greenish blades visible on exposed surfaces.

In Conduvia · Found in mountain pegmatite veins, identified by unusually coarse, oversized crystal structure. If the granite-like rock around you has abnormally large mineral grains, you may be standing on a pegmatite.

docs.title: Rare Earth Ore (e.g. Bastnäsite)
docs.text:
Real-world uses · Rare earth elements for magnets, lasers, and electronics; a critical late-tech supply chain.

Spot it · Often subtle: brown or tan host rock with disseminated minerals; heavy mineral sands can include brownish grains. Not reliably distinctive-looking -- identification often requires instruments.

Refine it

  • At Froth Flotation Cell → RErare earth concentrate

Leads to · COcerium oxide NOneodymium oxide

Geology & field notes

Geology · Commonly from carbonatite intrusions and some hydrothermal systems; also from ionic adsorption clays and heavy mineral concentrates.

Typical Size / Scale · Carbonatite pits can be large; clay deposits blanket hillsides (meters thick over kilometers).

Landmark / Recognition · Carbonate igneous rock context; barite and fluorite associations; slight radioactivity in some deposits.

In Conduvia · Rare earth ore won't announce itself visually -- this is anomaly-hunting territory. The host rock and subtle mineral associations are the tell, not the ore itself.

docs.title: Uraninite (Pitchblende)
docs.text:
Real-world uses · Uranium ore for nuclear fuel and late-game power chains; requires careful handling.

Spot it · Black pitchy massive material; can appear as a black smear or coating on surrounding rock. Bright secondary minerals often appear nearby: yellow-green crusts and stains.

Refine it

  • At Heap Leach Pad → UPuranium pregnant liquor
  • At Electric Arc Furnace → YEyellowcake

Leads to · UDuranium dioxide (UO2) UHuranium hexafluoride

Geology & field notes

Geology · Occurs in hydrothermal veins, sandstone roll-front deposits, and unconformity settings; commonly associated with alteration, sulfides, and redox boundaries.

Typical Size / Scale · Veins: cm to tens of cm thick; roll-front bodies can be broad and at depth; unconformity pods can be small but very high grade.

Landmark / Recognition · Yellow staining on sandstone fractures. Often not visually obvious until you know what to look for -- instruments are the reliable method.

In Conduvia · Instruments are your primary tool here -- visual cues alone are unreliable. Yellow-green crust clusters around a dark core are what you're looking for on exposed faces.

docs.title: Potash — sylvite to three potassium products
docs.text:
Tier 3–4 · Branch · Pack T3_Potash.luau

Potash means potassium for crops — the "K" in the N-P-K you see on a bag of fertilizer. It started as a dead raw_ore_sylvite and now forks into the three products a real potash mine actually sells: muriate of potash, sulfate of potash, and caustic potash (three different potassium chemicals).

The ore is potassium salt and ordinary table salt grown together in the same rock. The whole chain is really just two ideas: first split those two salts apart, then pick which potassium chemical you want to make.

docs.title: Flow
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    ore["raw_ore_sylvite"] -->|froth flotation| mop["muriate of potash (KCl)"]
    ore --> salt["salt (NaCl byproduct)"]
    mop -->|Mannheim +H2SO4| sop["sulfate of potash (K2SO4)"]
    acid["acid_sulfuric"] --> sop
    sop --> hcl["gas_hcl (co-product)"]
    mop -->|membrane electrolysis| koh["caustic potash (KOH)"]
    water["water_raw"] --> koh
    dc["dc_charge"] --> koh
    koh --> cl2["gas_chlorine"]
    koh --> h2["gas_hydrogen"]

docs.title: Steps
docs.text: # Recipe Station In Out 1 k_froth_flotation Froth Flotation Cell 3 sylvite 2 MOP + 1 salt 2 k_mannheim_sop Mannheim Muffle Furnace 2 MOP + 1 H₂SO₄ 2 SOP + 1 HCl gas 3 k_membrane_koh Membrane Electrolysis Cell 2 MOP + 2 water + 2 DC 2 KOH + Cl₂ + H₂

docs.title: Why it's built this way
docs.text:
• You float it apart, you don't just dissolve it. A soapy chemical grabs the potassium salt and floats it off on bubbles, leaving the plain table salt behind. That leftover salt is saved and sold — which finally gives the game's salt item an honest source instead of it appearing from nowhere.
  • The "Mannheim" step makes the premium grade. Treating the potassium salt with sulfuric acid makes a chloride-free fertilizer that's gentler on touchy crops, so it sells for more — and the gas it gives off feeds the game's existing hydrochloric-acid supply. Nothing wasted.
  • Caustic potash is just the salt-water trick, with potassium. That last cell is the exact potassium-flavored twin of the chlor-alkali plant: caustic on one side, chlorine on the other, hydrogen alongside.

docs.title: Byproducts & sinks
docs.text:
• salt — NaCl reject, now a real product (food/industrial/feedstock).
  • gas_hcl — Mannheim co-product; adds a second source to the HCl economy.
  • gas_chlorine / gas_hydrogen — membrane-cell co-products.
  • MOP / SOP / KOH — terminal fertilizer and chemical products.

Verified against Wikipedia (Potash, Potassium chloride, Mannheim process, Potassium sulfate, Potassium hydroxide) and standard fertilizer references.

docs.title: Rare earths — the brutal separation to an NdFeB magnet
docs.text:
Tier 5–6 · Branch · Pack T5_RareEarths.luau

Rare earths are the hardest things in all of metalworking to pull apart: a family of fifteen elements that are almost chemically identical, so they have to be separated one painstaking step at a time. The game had a raw ore, a dead "concentrate" item, and nothing else. This chain does the real, ugly job honestly — including the radioactive thorium that tags along in every rare-earth ore — and lands on the thing people actually dig these up for: a powerful neodymium magnet (the kind in headphones, hard drives, and electric-motor parts).

docs.title: Flow
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    ore["raw_ore_ree"] -->|flotation| conc["concentrate_ree"]
    conc -->|sulfuric acid bake| mixed["mixed REE sulfate"]
    acid["acid_sulfuric"] --> mixed
    mixed --> thorium["thorium residue (radioactive)"]
    mixed -->|solvent extraction cascade| nd["neodymium oxide"]
    mixed --> ce["cerium oxide (byproduct)"]
    mixed --> raff["mixed raffinate (byproduct)"]
    nd -->|molten-salt electrolysis| ndm["neodymium metal"]
    dc["dc_charge"] --> ndm
    borax["raw_ore_borax"] -->|calcine| b2o3["boric oxide"]
    ndm --> magnet["sintered NdFeB magnet"]
    fe["metal_iron_ingot"] --> magnet
    b2o3 --> magnet

docs.title: Steps
docs.text: # Recipe Station In Out 1 ree_flotation Froth Flotation Cell 3 REE ore 2 concentrate + tailings 2 ree_acid_bake Sulfation Acid-Roast Kiln 2 concentrate + 2 H₂SO₄ 2 mixed REE sulfate + 1 thorium residue 3 ree_sx_split SX Mixer-Settler 2 mixed sulfate Nd oxide + Ce oxide + raffinate 4 ree_reduce_neodymium Molten-Salt Electrolysis Cell 2 Nd oxide + 3 DC 2 Nd metal + O₂ 5 reb_calcine_boric_oxide Rotary Calciner Kiln 2 borax 2 boric oxide 6 nd_make_ndfeb_magnet Powder Press / Sinter Furnace 2 Nd + 1 Fe + 1 B₂O₃ 2 NdFeB magnet + slag

docs.title: Why it's built this way
docs.text:
• The acid bake traps the radioactive bit. Rare-earth ores come with radioactive thorium mixed in. Instead of pretending it isn't there, the early bake drops it out as a captured residue — a real disposal headache (and a possible future fuel), not vanished matter.
  • Splitting them apart is the whole battle. One tidy step here stands in for hundreds of real repeated stages that tease the look-alike elements apart by exploiting tiny differences between them. It pulls off the cheap, common one (cerium) first, then the prized neodymium, and banks the rest for later. This is the single most expensive step in the whole game.
  • Pulling neodymium metal out of its salt eats a huge amount of power — most of the reason rare-earth metals cost what they do.
  • A real neodymium magnet needs boron. You can't fake the recipe, so a tiny side-chain supplies the boron. The magnet is genuinely neodymium-iron-boron, not a hand-waved "rare-earth magnet."

docs.title: Byproducts & sinks
docs.text:
• cerium_oxide — abundant byproduct: glass polish and catalyst, not waste.
  • ree_raffinate_mixed — the heavier lanthanides, banked for future splits.
  • thorium_residue — radioactive leaf; future tie-in to a thorium breeder.
  • nd_magnet_sintered — the payoff: premium permanent magnet for compact motors and generators (a tier above the wrought-iron magnet core).

Verified against Wikipedia (Rare-earth element processing, Monazite, Solvent extraction, Neodymium magnet, Borax) and standard REE-process references.

docs.title: Tokens & Rewards
docs.text:
As you finish quests in a chapter you earn category tokens — think of them as proof you’ve mastered that era’s skills. Spend them here: upgrade three lower tokens into one higher token, or cash tokens in for useful bundles. The exchange is always open.

docs.title: Upgrade Tokens
docs.text: Exchange 3 tokens of one tier for 1 token of the next. All four categories can be upgraded independently.

docs.title: Survival Rewards
docs.text: Spend Survival tokens for material bundles that give you a head-start on staying alive. Costs are in Survival tokens of the appropriate tier.

docs.title: Tech Rewards
docs.text: Spend Tech tokens for tool and equipment bundles. Saves you some of the more tedious crafting steps.

docs.title: Industry Rewards
docs.text: Spend Industry tokens for fuel, materials, and processing bundles. Helps keep the production line moving when a bottleneck hits.

docs.title: Survival I → II
docs.text:
Exchange 3× Survival Token I for 1× Survival Token II.

Useful when you have a surplus of early-era tokens.

docs.title: Survival II → III
docs.text: Exchange 3× Survival Token II for 1× Survival Token III.

docs.title: Survival III → IV
docs.text: Exchange 3× Survival Token III for 1× Survival Token IV.

docs.title: Survival IV → V
docs.text: Exchange 3× Survival Token IV for 1× Survival Token V.

docs.text: Exchange 3× Industry Token IV for 1× Industry Token V.

docs.title: Survivor's Basics
docs.text: Spend 2× Survival Token I for a bundle of core gathering materials. Good when you need to restock without grinding another run.

docs.title: Shelter Starter
docs.text: Spend 3× Survival Token I for thatch and cordage to build or reinforce a shelter fast.

docs.title: Field Kit
docs.text: Spend 3× Survival Token II for a portable field kit: a fire-starting kit, cutting tool, and water container.

docs.title: Stone Tools Pack
docs.text: Spend 2× Tech Token I for a set of stone-age tools. Saves the full knapping + hafting chain when you need to replace a worn set.

docs.title: Forger's Assist
docs.text: Spend 4× Tech Token II for a bundle of refined metal components. Cuts some of the repetitive ore-to-ingot grind.

docs.title: Steam Engineer's Pack
docs.text: Spend 5× Tech Token III for a set of steam-era engineering components. Helps when a critical machine part is the bottleneck.

docs.title: Charcoal & Clay Kit
docs.text: Spend 2× Industry Token I for a bulk supply of charcoal and clay. Keeps the kiln and forge fed without another gather run.

docs.title: Ironworker's Cache
docs.text: Spend 4× Industry Token II for wrought iron stock, fuel, and cord. Unblocks a forge session when raw materials run short.

docs.title: Steam Works Supply
docs.text: Spend 5× Industry Token III for a batch of steam-plant consumables and spares. Helps when the boiler house is eating through pipe and packing.

docs.title: Silicon — from beach sand to the wafer that runs the world
docs.text:
Silicon is everywhere — it's the second most common stuff in the ground, and the sand on a beach is basically free. So why are computer chips expensive? One word: cleanliness. A finished chip wafer is silicon scrubbed until it's about 99.9999999% pure (that's nine 9s in a row) and frozen into one perfect crystal. Getting that clean is one of the longest cleanup ladders in all of industry, and every rung trades a pile of electricity for a little more purity.

The trick: make it a liquid

You can't boil a solid clean. So the whole plan is: turn the dirty silicon into a liquid you can boil, boil that liquid over and over until it's spotless, then turn it back into a solid. Dirty solid in, super-clean solid out.

docs.title: The ladder
docs.text:
# Step · station In → Out Tier · time · energy 1 Carbothermic reduction · submerged-arc furnace 2 quartz + 2 coke → 1 MG-silicon + 2 CO₂ T3 · 120s · 260 kJ 2 Hydrochlorination · fluidised-bed reactor 1 MG-Si + 3 HCl → 1 trichlorosilane + H₂ T4 · 70s · 90 kJ 2b SiCl₄ redistribution · fluidised-bed reactor 3 SiCl₄ + 1 MG-Si + 2 H₂ → 4 trichlorosilane T4 · 80s · 110 kJ 3 Siemens deposition · CVD reactor 3 trichlorosilane + 2 H₂ → 1 polysilicon (9N) + 3 HCl + 1 SiCl₄ T4 · 200s · 380 kJ 4 Czochralski pull · crystal puller 4 polysilicon → 1 monocrystal ingot T4 · 180s · 300 kJ 5 Slice · wafer fab line 1 ingot → 8 raw wafers T4 · 60s · 80 kJ 6 Polish (CMP) · wafer fab line 1 raw wafer → 1 polished wafer T4 · 50s · 70 kJ \[ SiO_2 + 2\,C \rightarrow Si + 2\,CO \qquad Si + 3\,HCl \rightarrow SiHCl_3 + H_2 $$ $$ SiHCl_3 + H_2 \rightarrow Si + 3\,HCl \] 
flowchart LR
  SAND["quartz sand (SiO2)"] -->|carbothermic| MG["MG-silicon ~98%"]
  MG -->|+ HCl| TCS["trichlorosilane"]
  SICL4["silicon tetrachloride<br>(from zirconium plant)"] -->|redistribute + MG-Si| TCS
  TCS -->|Siemens CVD + H2| POLY["9N polysilicon"]
  POLY -->|Czochralski| INGOT["monocrystal ingot"]
  INGOT -->|wire saw| RAW["raw wafer"]
  RAW -->|CMP polish| POL["polished wafer"]
  POLY -.->|byproduct| SICL4
  RAW --> DEV["diodes · transistors · PV cells"]
  POL --> CHIP["logic chips"]

docs.title: Why each rung matters
docs.text:
• Roast sand with carbon and you get cheap, ~98%-pure silicon — fine for mixing into metals, but hopelessly dirty for electronics. It also eats a ton of power, which is why this furnace is a mid-game gate. (The proper name is carbothermic reduction: carbon plus heat pulling the oxygen off.)
  • Turn it into a liquid. You react that dirty silicon with a gas to make a liquid called trichlorosilane. This is the single cleverest move in the chain, because now you can boil it clean.
  • Reuse the leftovers. A related gas comes off both the zirconium plant and the silicon reactor. Instead of dumping it, you feed it right back in to make more of the liquid. Nothing wasted.
  • Boil it spotless, then rebuild it. In the Siemens reactor the clean liquid is laid back down as solid silicon, atom by atom, on a glowing rod — now those famous nine 9s pure. This is the most power-hungry step in all of electronics.
  • Grow one perfect crystal. The Czochralski puller dips a seed into melted silicon and slowly draws up one flawless crystal rod. Slice it into thin discs and polish them mirror-smooth, and you've got the wafers every chip and solar cell is printed on.

No more wafers-from-sand shortcut

The old recipe let you make finished wafers straight from sand and charcoal, skipping the whole cleanup ladder. Now every diode, transistor, and solar cell sits properly behind the real chain — you have to climb all the way to a grown crystal before you can slice a single wafer.

docs.title: Sulfuric acid — the keystone reagent
docs.text:
Sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) is the most-made industrial chemical on the planet, and in Conduvia it's your first chain that's pure chemistry — it has no ore of its own. You build the whole thing from a gas you're already making elsewhere. It mops up the smelly sulfur fumes from your copper smelter and unlocks a pile of later recipes: leaching metals out of rock, battery fluid, other acids, and uranium work. Every step here is how it's really done.

Two real routes

Contact Process / DCDA (built, Tier 4): SO₂ → SO₃ over a vanadium catalyst → oleum → ~98% acid. The modern, high-purity route — what ~all the world's acid uses today. Lead Chamber Process (built, Tier 3): SO₂ + NOₓ + moist air in lead chambers → ~65–78% "chamber acid" → boil up. The historical route (Roebuck, 1746). Lower tech, lower grade — your early path to acid before a contact plant exists.

docs.title: Where the sulfur comes from
docs.text:
The acid never starts from an "acid ore" — it starts from SO₂ gas, which you get two honest ways:

  • Dead-roast pyrite — 4 FeS₂ + 11 O₂ → 2 Fe₂O₃ + 8 SO₂. Pyrite is a common, minable sulfide. The roast drives off SO₂ and leaves an iron-oxide cinder that is literally the roasted hematite the iron chain smelts — a real cross-chain feed, not a throwaway.
  • Capture copper-smelter off-gas — the copper roast and converter already emit gas_so2. Real smelters pipe that 3–13% SO₂ straight into an acid plant instead of venting it. Same item, so your copper line feeds your acid line for free.

docs.title: The chain, step by step
docs.text: # Step · station In → Out Byproduct / note Tier · time · energy 0 Gather water · — → 4 raw water world resource T0 · 10s · — 1 Dead-roast pyrite · sulfur burner 4 pyrite → 6 SO₂ +2 roasted hematite → iron chain T2 · 50s · 40 kJ — (or reuse copper-smelter SO₂) copper line → SO₂ loop closed — Contact route 2 Oxidise · contact converter (V₂O₅) 2 SO₂ → 2 SO₃ 450 °C, 1–2 atm, ~98% T4 · 30s · 60 kJ 3 Absorb to oleum · absorption tower 3 SO₃ + 1 water → 2 oleum never SO₃ + water direct! T4 · 20s · 18 kJ 4 Dilute oleum · absorption tower 2 oleum + 1 water → 3 H₂SO₄ ~98% product T4 · 20s · 12 kJ Lead-chamber route 5 Chamber acid · lead chamber 3 SO₂ + 1 water → 2 dilute H₂SO₄ NOₓ catalysed, ~78% cap T3 · 40s · 15 kJ 6 Concentrate · Glover tower 2 dilute → 1 H₂SO₄ boil down T3 · 35s · 25 kJ

docs.title: Want the real chemistry? (totally optional)
docs.text:
You don't need any of this to play — the recipe table above is enough. But if you're the kind of person who likes to know why, here's the honest version, the way a good chemistry teacher would walk you through it.

The main move: fattening up the sulfur gas. You take a sulfur gas and add oxygen to it, which turns it into a hungrier gas that can grab water and become acid. The fussy part: this step is lazy. Heat it too much and it runs backward; heat it too little and it barely runs at all. So real plants pick a warm-but-not- roasting sweet spot (around 450 °C) and toss in a catalyst — a helper that speeds the reaction along without getting used up itself. To squeeze out the last bit, the gas gets run through more than once.

Why you must never just add water. That hungry gas wants water so badly that if you mix the two straight together, you get a fine acid fog that drifts off instead of settling into liquid — you'd literally lose your product up the chimney. The sneaky fix: dissolve the gas into acid you already have, making a thick, fuming acid called oleum. Then you trickle water into the oleum slowly and under control to finish the job. This little detour is the single biggest "gotcha" of the whole process — get it wrong and your acid floats away.

The old-school way (your early route). Long before catalysts existed, people made acid in big lead-lined rooms, using a puff of nitrogen gases as the helper instead. It's cheap and tough, but it only reaches a weakish acid — fine for fertiliser and cleaning metal, too weak for the strong stuff. That's your Tier-3 stepping stone until you can build the modern plant.

docs.title: The byproduct loop
docs.text:
copper roast/convert ─┐
                      ├─► SO₂ ──(V₂O₅)──► SO₃ ──► oleum ──► H₂SO₄ ──► leaching / HF / electrolyte / yellowcake
pyrite dead-roast  ───┘        │
                               └─► roasted hematite ──► iron chain (bloomery / blast furnace)

Nothing is wasted: the sulfur that copper smelting would have vented becomes acid, and the iron in the pyrite becomes feed for the iron chain.

docs.title: Where to find these recipes
docs.text:
• In-game / NEI: search the T3_SulfuricAcid pack, or any of the stations — Sulfur Burner / Pyrite Roaster, Contact Converter (V₂O₅ beds, DCDA), Oleum Absorption Tower, Lead Chamber (NOₓ).
  • In these docs: the recipe index lists every step by id (acid_roast_pyrite, acid_so2_to_so3, acid_form_oleum, acid_dilute_oleum, acid_lead_chamber, acid_concentrate_chamber).
  • Unlocks next: with acid in hand, the deferred copper oxide / SX-EW route (leach → solvent extraction → electrowinning) becomes buildable — see the Missing-chains roadmap.

docs.title: Titanium — the chloride route & the Hunter reduction
docs.text:
Titanium is everywhere in the ground, yet it's one of the priciest metals you can buy — and that contradiction is the whole lesson here. Titanium is greedy: it grabs onto oxygen, nitrogen, and carbon so hard that you can't just smelt it like iron. Throw it in a furnace with carbon and you get a useless crumbly compound, not metal. The workaround is a long, roundabout path that turns titanium into a liquid first — and that detour is why titanium stayed a lab curiosity until the 1940s.

The trick: turn it into a liquid

You can't clean up the stubborn ore directly. So you turn it into a liquid (TiCl₄, which chemists nickname "tickle") that boils at just 136 °C and is dead easy to boil clean. Then you pull the metal out of that using a second, eager metal — all sealed away from the air. Turning it into a liquid is the whole trick.

docs.title: The plant, step by step
docs.text:
# Step · station In → Out Chemistry Tier · time · energy 1 Upgrade · rotary kiln 2 ilmenite + coke → 2 synthetic rutile + slag strip iron from FeTiO₃ T3 · 90s · 130 kJ 2 Chlorinate · fluidised bed 2 rutile + coke + 2 Cl₂ → 2 crude TiCl₄ + CO₂ TiO₂ + C + 2Cl₂ → TiCl₄ + CO₂ T4 · 120s · 200 kJ 3 Distil · distillation column 2 crude → 2 TiCl₄ + tailings fractional distillation to purity T4 · 80s · 90 kJ 4 Reduce · Hunter retort TiCl₄ + 4 Na → sponge + 4 NaCl TiCl₄ + 4Na → Ti + 4NaCl T4 · 200s · 300 kJ 5 Remelt · VAR furnace 3 sponge → 1 ingot vacuum arc remelting T4 · 180s · 400 kJ

Downstream the existing steps continue: ingot + aluminium → Ti-6Al-4V, the workhorse aerospace alloy → rolled into titanium plate.

Three details that make this honest:

  • Clean the ore first. The raw ore (ilmenite) is about a third iron. You strip that iron out before the chlorine step, so the chlorine is spent grabbing titanium instead of being wasted on iron.
  • The salt goes home. Pulling the metal free leaves a pile of ordinary table salt behind, which loops right back to the salt-water plant to become chlorine and sodium again. Real titanium plants close this exact loop.
  • Everything stays sealed and slow. Fresh titanium is so oxygen-hungry it can catch fire in open air, so it's made in sealed, air-free chambers and melted under vacuum. That slow, careful, power-hungry process is why titanium costs so much.
flowchart LR
  ILM["ilmenite (FeTiO3)"] -->|upgrade| RUT["synthetic rutile"]
  RUT -->|chlorinate +Cl2 +coke| CR["crude TiCl4"]
  CR -->|distil| TI4["pure TiCl4"]
  CL["chlorine (chlor-alkali)"] --> CR
  TI4 -->|Hunter +Na| SP["Ti sponge"]
  NA["sodium (Downs cell)"] --> SP
  SP -.->|4 NaCl back to brine| BRINE["brine plant"]
  SP -->|vacuum arc remelt| ING["Ti ingot"]
  ING -->|+ aluminium| T64["Ti-6Al-4V"]
  T64 -->|roll| PL["Ti plate"]

Hunter vs Kroll

The modern world reduces TiCl₄ with magnesium (the Kroll process); the older Hunter process uses sodium. Conduvia runs Hunter because sodium is the reactive metal the plant already makes — from the Downs cell and chlor-alkali line. Same idea, different reducing metal: TiCl₄ + 2Mg → Ti + 2MgCl₂ (Kroll) versus TiCl₄ + 4Na → Ti + 4NaCl (Hunter).

docs.title: Tungsten — the metal you cannot melt
docs.text: Tungsten melts at a ridiculous 3422 °C — hotter than any other metal, and hotter than anything you could build a pot to melt it in. So the whole industry is built around never melting it. Instead you grind it into a powder and press that powder into shape — and you can turn the same powder into some of the hardest cutting tools on Earth.

docs.title: Why it's different
docs.text: Every other metal in Conduvia ends by being cast — melted and poured into a mold. Tungsten can't be, because nothing can hold a puddle of molten tungsten. So the trick is to work with it as a powder: you strip the ore down to a fine metal powder, then press it and heat it until the grains weld together solid — all without ever melting it. Feed that same powder some carbon and it becomes tungsten carbide, which gets glued together into the tips that cut hardened steel and drill through rock.

docs.title: The chain
docs.text:
flowchart TD
  ore["wolframite (Fe,Mn)WO₄"] -->|caustic digest, + NaOH| nat["sodium tungstate soln"]
  nat -->|+ NH₃, crystallise| apt["ammonium paratungstate (APT)"]
  apt -->|calcine| wo3["tungsten oxide WO₃"]
  apt -.->|recovers| nh3["NH₃ recycled"]
  wo3 -->|+ H₂ reduce| pow["tungsten powder"]
  pow -->|press + sinter ~2500°C| rod["sintered rod"]
  pow -->|+ C, carburise| wc["tungsten carbide WC powder"]
  wc -->|+ Ni binder, liquid-phase sinter| insert["cemented carbide insert"]

docs.title: 1 · Caustic digestion — digester autoclave, T4
docs.text: Boil wolframite in hot caustic soda under pressure: (Fe,Mn)WO₄ + 2 NaOH → Na₂WO₄ + Fe/Mn hydroxide slag. Tungsten dissolves as sodium tungstate; the iron and manganese drop out. The first and biggest purity gain.

docs.title: 2 · APT crystallisation — APT crystalliser, T4
docs.text: Precipitate ammonium paratungstate, (NH₄)₁₀(W₁₂O₄₁), with ammonia. Essentially all the world's tungsten passes through APT — recrystallising it rejects the last impurities, so it's the clean, tradeable form. Some caustic is regenerated back to the digester.

docs.title: 3 · Calcination — rotary calciner kiln, T4
docs.text: APT → WO₃ + NH₃ + H₂O. Drive off ammonia (captured and piped back to the crystalliser) and water, leaving canary-yellow tungsten trioxide.

docs.title: 4 · Hydrogen reduction — pusher furnace, T4
docs.text: WO₃ + 3 H₂ → W + 3 H₂O. Reduce the oxide to metal in flowing hydrogen. The product is a fine grey powder — and powder is the only practical form, because you cannot cast a metal that melts at 3422 °C.

docs.title: 5 · Press & sinter — powder press + sinter furnace, T5
docs.text: Press the powder into a bar and sinter it near 2500 °C until the grains weld into a dense rod — without ever melting the metal. Powder metallurgy is the only route. Used for rocket nozzles, electrodes and filaments.

docs.title: 6 · Carburising — electric arc furnace, T5
docs.text: W + C → WC. Heat tungsten powder with carbon until it takes up carbon as tungsten carbide — near diamond-hard, but still just a loose powder.

docs.title: 7 · Cemented carbide — powder press + sinter furnace, T5
docs.text: Liquid-phase sinter WC powder with a nickel binder: the metal melts and wicks between the carbide grains, gluing them into a dense, almost diamond-hard yet shatter-resistant solid — the cutting insert that machines hardened steel.

docs.title: Honest notes
docs.text:
• Why nickel, not cobalt? Cobalt is the textbook binder, but Conduvia only has cobaltite ore — no refined cobalt yet. Nickel-bonded grades are entirely real: the corrosion-resistant, non-magnetic hard-metals. When a cobalt refining chain lands, a true WC-Co grade can join it.
  • Ammonia is recycled. The NH₃ used to crystallise APT comes back out at calcination and loops — honest reagent accounting, fed by the Haber-Bosch plant.
  • Feeds: wolframite (gatherable), NaOH + H₂ (chlor-alkali), NH₃ (gases/Haber-Bosch), nickel cathode (sulfide chain), carbon (charcoal).

Verified against Wikipedia (Tungsten, Ammonium paratungstate, Tungsten carbide, Cemented carbide, Powder metallurgy), the International Tungsten Industry Association, and ScienceDirect Topics.

docs.title: Uranium — the full nuclear fuel cycle
docs.text:
Uranium is the single longest road in the game: a faintly radioactive yellow rock becomes, eleven careful steps later, a working nuclear reactor. Every step here is real, and two of them finally pay off chains we already built — the Zircaloy tubing from the zirconium chain, and the hafnium control rods (hafnium is zirconium's separated-out twin).

flowchart TD
  ore["uraninite ore"] -->|"acid leach"| liq["pregnant liquor"]
  liq -->|"precipitate + calcine (NH3)"| yc["yellowcake U3O8"]
  yc -->|"H2 reduce"| uo2["UO2"]
  uo2 -->|"+ HF"| uf4["UF4 green salt"]
  hf["HF"] -->|"electrolysis"| f2["F2"]
  uf4 -->|"+ F2"| uf6["UF6"]
  uf6 -->|"gas centrifuge"| leu["enriched LEU"]
  uf6 -.->|"tails"| dep["depleted UF6"]
  leu -->|"reconvert (HF recovered)"| pwd["enriched UO2 powder"]
  pwd -->|"press + sinter"| pel["fuel pellet"]
  pel -->|"+ Zircaloy clad"| rod["fuel rod"]
  hfcl["HfCl4 (from Zr refining)"] -->|"Na reduce"| hfs["Hf sponge"]
  hfs -->|"clad in steel"| cr["control rod"]
  rod --> rx["light-water reactor"]
  cr --> rx

docs.title: Milling — from rock to yellowcake
docs.text: You crush the ore and dissolve the uranium out with acid, leaving the radioactive leftover rock behind. Then you concentrate that liquid, drop the uranium back out as a solid, and bake it into yellowcake — the yellow powder uranium is actually shipped around the world as.

docs.title: Conversion — yellowcake to UF₆
docs.text:
To enrich uranium you first have to turn it into a gas, and the only uranium gas that works is one built with fluorine, so:

  1. U3O8 + 2 H2 -> 3 UO2 + 2 H2O (hydrogen reduction)
  2. UO2 + 4 HF -> UF4 + 2 H2O (hydrofluorination — the green salt)
  3. UF4 + F2 -> UF6 (fluorination)

You have to make that fluorine right there on-site (2 HF -> H2 + F2) using electricity. Fluorine is so aggressive that nothing else can pry it loose — electricity is the only way.

docs.title: Enrichment — the expensive middle
docs.text: Fresh uranium is only 0.7% of the good, splittable kind (called U-235); a reactor needs about 3 to 5%. To bump it up, you spin the uranium gas in a long chain of super-fast centrifuges that fling the slightly heavier atoms outward and let the lighter, good ones drift inward. The catch: you feed in a lot and get out only a little enriched uranium, leaving a big pile of leftover "depleted" uranium. That slow, hard sorting is the whole reason nuclear fuel is expensive — and, in the real world, the whole reason it's so tightly controlled.

docs.title: Fabrication — back to a solid
docs.text: Now you turn the enriched uranium gas back into a powder (and the fluorine that comes off is caught and recycled — nothing wasted). You press that powder into hard little ceramic pellets, stack them inside Zircaloy tubes — the see-through-to-neutrons tubing the whole zirconium chain exists to make — and seal them up into a fuel rod.

docs.title: Control rods — the twin returns
docs.text: Back in the zirconium chain, we had to carefully separate out hafnium because it soaks up neutrons. That's a problem for tubing — but it's exactly what you want in a control rod, the part you slide in and out to turn the reactor up or down. So the leftover hafnium, once an orphan, gets turned into metal and sealed into control rods. Zirconium and hafnium, split apart with so much effort, end up back together inside the same reactor doing opposite jobs.

docs.title: Reactor
docs.text: Bolt together your fuel rods, the hafnium control rods, a thick steel vessel, steam valves, a coolant pump, and a generator, and you've got a reactor: splitting atoms heats water, the steam spins the generator, and that's your power. The crown jewel at the very top of the tech tree.

docs.title: Honest notes
docs.text:
• The leftover "depleted" uranium is kept as a real item, not deleted — it's a genuine stockpile, and later it's useful as heavy shielding or ballast.
  • The fluorine is caught and reused at the fabrication step; it's never treated as free.
  • Hafnium control rods are a real design (there are others), and we use hafnium here because the world already makes it as a leftover from refining zirconium.

docs.title: Zinc — sphalerite two ways, from one calcine
docs.text:
Zinc always starts the same way — float the ore, then roast it — and then the path forks. You can pull the zinc out with electricity (the modern way) or boil it out with heat (the old way). Both roads end at the same zinc ingot.

flowchart TD
  ore["sphalerite (ZnS)"] -->|"froth flotation"| conc["concentrate"]
  conc -->|"roast"| cal["zinc oxide calcine"]
  conc -.->|"SO2"| acid["acid plant"]
  cal -->|"sulfuric leach"| liq["zinc sulfate liquor"]
  liq -->|"electrowin"| ingA["zinc ingot"]
  ingA -.->|"H2SO4 regenerated"| liq
  cal -->|"coke + distil"| ingB["zinc ingot"]

docs.title: The shared start: float, then roast
docs.text: You float the ore with bubbles to clean it up, then roast it. The roasting burns off the sulfur as a gas, which you send to the acid plant. (Any time you roast a sulfur ore like this, an acid plant is its natural partner — you'll see that pairing everywhere.)

docs.title: Route A — pull it out with electricity (the modern way)
docs.text: You dissolve the roasted ore in sulfuric acid to make a zinc "soup," then run electricity through it to plate pure zinc onto metal sheets. Handy bonus: the cell makes fresh acid as it runs, and that loops right back to dissolve more ore. Melt the plated zinc and cast it into ingots.

docs.title: Route B — boil it out with heat (the old way)
docs.text: You heat the roasted ore with coke until the zinc literally boils off as a vapor (zinc boils at a low 907 °C), then catch that vapor and let it cool back into metal. No electricity needed, but it burns a lot of fuel and the zinc comes out less pure.

docs.title: Honest notes
docs.text:
• The acid loop in the electric route is real — plating the zinc out also makes fresh acid that goes right back to the start.
  • Where real zinc goes: rust-proofing steel (galvanizing), brass, and die-cast parts — and, in this game, feeding the lead chain's silver-removal step, which is why these two ores (which show up together in nature) are wired together here.

docs.title: Zirconium — the nuclear metal & the hardest separation in the table
docs.text:
Zirconium really matters in one place above all: inside a nuclear reactor. Neutrons fly straight through it as if it's barely there, which makes it the perfect skin to wrap around fuel rods. But getting zirconium pure enough for a reactor runs straight into the single nastiest sorting problem in everyday metalworking — and that problem is the whole chain.

The hafnium problem

Zircon ore always carries a little hafnium mixed in, and hafnium is a near- perfect chemical twin of zirconium — the two atoms are almost the same size, so they behave almost identically and flat-out refuse to separate by normal chemistry. The trouble: hafnium soaks up neutrons like a sponge, the exact opposite of what you want wrapped around fuel. So reactor-grade zirconium means driving the hafnium down to almost nothing. The upside: the hafnium you pull out is exactly what you want for reactor control rods.

docs.title: The route
docs.text:
Zircon is a silicon-based mineral, so the very first step actually frees up two useful chemicals at once — a zirconium one and a silicon one — and the silicon one is a valuable product, not trash.

# Step · station In → Out Tier · time · energy 1 Carbochlorinate · fluidised-bed chlorinator 2 zircon + 2 coke + 4 Cl₂ → 2 crude ZrCl₄ + 1 SiCl₄ + 2 CO₂ T4 · 130s · 210 kJ 2 Separate hafnium · Hf/Zr column 3 crude ZrCl₄ → 3 nuclear-grade ZrCl₄ + 1 HfCl₄ T4 · 110s · 150 kJ 3 Reduce · Hunter retort 1 ZrCl₄ + 4 Na → 1 Zr sponge + 4 NaCl T4 · 200s · 300 kJ 4 VAR ingot · vacuum-arc furnace 3 sponge → 1 zirconium ingot T4 · 180s · 400 kJ 5 Alloy · electric arc furnace 4 ingot + 1 tin + 1 steel → 4 Zircaloy T4 · 120s · 220 kJ \[ ZrSiO_4 + 2\,C + 4\,Cl_2 \rightarrow ZrCl_4 + SiCl_4 + 2\,CO_2 $$ $$ ZrCl_4 + 4\,Na \rightarrow Zr + 4\,NaCl \] 
flowchart LR
  ZIRCON["zircon sand (ZrSiO4)"] -->|carbochlorinate| CRUDE["crude ZrCl4 (Hf-bearing)"]
  ZIRCON --> SICL4["silicon tetrachloride"]
  CRUDE -->|Hf/Zr separation| PURE["nuclear-grade ZrCl4"]
  CRUDE --> HFCL4["hafnium tetrachloride"]
  HFCL4 -.->|control rods, superalloys| HF["hafnium"]
  PURE -->|Na reduction| SPONGE["Zr sponge"]
  SPONGE -->|VAR| INGOT["zirconium ingot"]
  INGOT -->|+ tin + steel| ZIRCALOY["Zircaloy cladding"]
  SPONGE -->|+ Na| NACL["rock salt → brine plant"]

docs.title: Why each step matters
docs.text:
• Splitting the ore frees silicon too. Because zircon is a silicon mineral, cracking it open with chlorine gives off both a zirconium gas and a silicon gas. The silicon one is collected and sold (it makes ultra-pure silica and fiber-optic glass), not vented.
  • Separating out the hafnium is the keystone. Carefully splitting the twins apart is the only way to get reactor-grade metal. Skip it and your "zirconium" gobbles neutrons and is useless in a reactor. This is the step that makes zirconium expensive and strategic.
  • Sodium reduction with a closed salt loop. Like titanium, this step makes ordinary salt as a leftover, and that salt loops straight back to the salt-water plant — nothing wasted. (Real industry usually uses magnesium here, ZrCl₄ + 2 Mg → Zr + 2 MgCl₂; we use sodium because that's the reactive metal our plant already makes.)
  • Melting under vacuum. Hot zirconium grabs air greedily, so the final melt is done in a vacuum to keep it clean.
  • Zircaloy. Mostly zirconium with a little tin and a trace of steel — strong, rust-proof in hot water, and nearly invisible to neutrons.

Shares tooling with titanium

The fluidised-bed chlorinator, Hunter reduction retort and vacuum-arc furnace are the very same machines built for the titanium chain — the chloride route is a family of processes, and the game models them that way. Only the Hf/Zr separation column is new.

docs.title: Controls
docs.text:
Conduvia is played on keyboard and mouse. The defaults:

Key Action Tab Open / close your Inventory J Open / close the Quest Log F Interact — pick up items, use a station, open a container R Recipes — how to make the hovered item U Uses — what the hovered item is used in A Favourite the hovered item K Bookmark the hovered item Q Drop one of the held item G Sort your inventory M Open the Map (fullscreen) B Drop a map waypoint at your position N Open the waypoint list P Open / close the Menu V Push-to-talk (voice chat) Shift Sprint Ctrl Crouch C Slide (while sprinting) Print Screen Screenshot

docs.title: Two keys you’ll live in
docs.text:
• R (Recipes) and U (Uses) are your in-game version of this site. Hover anything, press R to see how it’s made, U to see what it feeds into. Lost? These two keys are the fastest way to orient.
  • J (Quest Log) always shows your current objectives and the next step on the win-path.

Note on R

R shows the Recipes for whatever you're hovering. Sorting your inventory is a separate key, G — they don't conflict.

docs.title: Dying
docs.text: Death isn’t the end of a run. You drop a gravestone where you fell and a waypoint marks it on your map — go back to recover your things. Set up a bedroll early: it claims your spawn point so you respawn nearby instead of starting over far away. The Survival chapter walks you through both.

docs.title: Hand-crafting vs stations
docs.text: Simple things you can make anywhere from your inventory. Anything that needs heat, force, or process needs a station — a placed machine modeled on a real piece of equipment. You craft at a station by putting inputs in and collecting outputs.

docs.title: Reading a recipe
docs.text:
Hover any item and press R (Recipes) to see exactly how it’s made: what goes in, what comes out, which station does the work, and how long it takes. Press U (Uses) to go the other way — what an item feeds into. Between those two keys you can trace the whole tree without leaving the game.

In this manual, recipes and tasks show items as chips like ROrope×2 (an item plus a count). On the item pages, each entry lists what it’s for and which station builds it.

docs.title: How a station works
docs.text:
Most stations have up to three kinds of slots:

  • input — raw materials go here
  • fuel — if the process needs heat (charcoal, coke, etc.)
  • output — finished goods land here

Some stations have no panel at all — they do their job just by being placed (markers, info posts, sleeping spots). The Stations reference lists every station with its slots and what it’s for.

docs.title: The pattern that repeats forever
docs.text:
Every tier is the same shape at a higher temperature:

get a heat/energy source → build the station that uses it → process raw material into a refined material → use that material to build the next, hotter station.

Campfire → kiln → bloomery → blast furnace → arc furnace → crystal puller. Once you see the pattern, you can predict what the game wants next.

docs.title: What is Conduvia?
docs.text:
Conduvia is a survival game with one big idea: you rebuild the whole modern world starting from a pile of sticks — and the recipes are the real ones.

Not "made-up game logic." Real. Want steel? You'll bake coal into a hotter fuel, then run a giant furnace, just like real factories do. Want aluminum? You'll pull it out of rock using electricity. Want a computer chip? You'll grow a perfect crystal and slice it into thin discs. The fun part isn't memorizing some crafting tree a game designer invented. It's realizing you already live in a world built this exact way — and now you get to build it with your own hands.

docs.title: How a normal session goes
docs.text:
1. Grab what's around you — plants, stone, wood, water.
  2. Build a machine and craft on it. Each machine is a copy of a real one: a kiln, a furnace, a crystal-growing rig. You feed stuff in, you get stuff out.
  3. Reach the next step, which hands you a new power: first heat you can control, then metal, then electricity, then chemistry, then electronics.
  4. Keep climbing until you can build a rocket and launch it into space. That's the win.

docs.title: Why you can't skip ahead
docs.text:
The steps aren't locked behind "reach level 10" rules. They're locked behind real life. You can't melt aluminum over a campfire — it needs a wall of electricity a campfire can't give. You can't make that electricity without steel machines. You can't make steel without a super-hot fuel. Every step literally stands on the one below it, like rungs on a ladder. That's why finally reaching the top feels earned — because it is.

We're honest about the shortcuts

To keep a play session fun, Conduvia speeds up time and rounds off some amounts. But the steps themselves are the real ones. Anywhere we simplify something, the guide tells you straight up.

docs.title: What this guide is
docs.text:
Think of this as your "okay, what do I do now?" handbook. The main path is the quest log: follow The Climb one step at a time and it will always point you at the next thing to make. Everything else — the list of every item, the machines, and the deep-dive guides — is there for when you want to know more about how something works.

Start with your first hour →

docs.title: Win-path vs branches
docs.text: Conduvia has one main path to the win, plus a whole lot of optional stuff around it. Knowing which is which keeps you from getting lost.

docs.title: Win-path
docs.text:
The win-path is the one straight line of steps that ends with a rocket in space. Every quest on it is marked Win-path, and the steps that finish a chapter are marked Checkpoint. Do only these, skip everything else, and you'll still reach orbit.

sticks + fiber -> cordage -> stone knife -> fire -> kiln + charcoal
  -> copper -> bronze -> bloomery -> wrought iron
  -> coke -> steam power -> blast furnace -> steel
  -> DC dynamo -> AC grid
  -> electrolysis (aluminium, chlor-alkali, ferroalloys, MG-silicon)
  -> polysilicon -> wafer -> chips / automation
  -> cryogenics (LOX) + titanium + superalloys
  -> rocket engine -> tanks + fuel -> stage -> vehicle -> ORBITAL LAUNCH

docs.title: Branches
docs.text: Everything marked Branch is optional: fishing, trapping, cooking, farming, decorative building, and the deeper side-systems. Branches make your life easier or richer — better food, more storage, comfort, automation conveniences — but the win doesn’t require them.

docs.title: Briefings & exchanges
docs.text:
• Briefing — a read-only intro or explainer. No crafting required.
  • Exchange — the token economy: trade category tokens for upgrades and bundles.

docs.title: How to use this
docs.text: In a hurry or feeling lost? Just follow the Win-path and Checkpoint quests down The Climb. Want to slow down and enjoy the world? Take the branches too. Both are totally fine ways to play — this guide just makes the main line easy to spot.

docs.title: Your first hour
docs.text: You wake up with nothing. Here’s how to not die, in order. Every step here is the opening of the Survival chapter — open your Quest Log (J) and it’ll track each one for you.

docs.title: 1. Make some rope
docs.text: In the early game, rope holds everything together. Grab plant fibers off the ground and out of bushes, then craft ROrope. Rope was one of the very first things people ever made — without it you can't tie a stone to a stick or lash together a shelter.

docs.title: 2. Make a cutting edge
docs.text: Find some stone and chip it into a sharp edge — a SKstone knife flake or a hand axe. Now you can cut, scrape, and break down everything else.

docs.title: 3. Find water
docs.text: Getting thirsty is a countdown. Find clean water early and drink up. Keep half an eye on your needs bar while you work.

docs.title: 4. Light a fire
docs.text: Fire is heat, safety, and cooking. Build a campfire and get it lit. This is also your first taste of controlled heat — the thing the entire tech tree is secretly about.

docs.title: 5. Set up to sleep
docs.text:
Place a bedroll to claim your spawn point, so a bad night doesn’t send you back to square one. Then survive until morning.

You’ve hit the first checkpoint

Water, fire, a blade, and a safe night — that’s Survival I complete. From here the game opens up: trapping, fishing, your first cooked meal, simple storage, and the first farm plots are all optional branches you can take in any order before you start the Stone climb.

Continue to the full Survival chapter →

docs.title: Items & Ores
docs.text:
Every craftable, mineable, and placeable thing in Conduvia — 1150 entries — grouped by the tier it belongs to. In-game, hover any item and press R to see how to make it, or U to see what it’s used in.

Tier 0 — Survival161 items Tier 0 — Wildlife & Hunting149 items Tier 1 — Stone79 items Tier 2 — Metal Ages26 items Tier 3 — Steam35 items Tier 4 — Steel & DC46 items Tier 5 — Electric & Chemistry94 items Tier 6 — Solid State13 items Tier 7 — Space47 items Ores & Raw Materials49 items

docs.title: Loot & Rewards
docs.text:
Loot bags are Conduvia’s reward drops — earned from quests, milestones, and world caches. Right-click a bag to open it and it rolls a handful of item stacks from its pool. Everything below is pulled straight from the game’s loot tables, so this is exactly what each bag can contain.

10 bag types · 27 shared resources in the common pool

How a bag rolls

  • Each bag yields a fixed number of stacks — its roll count.
  • Tiered bags blend a bag-specific pool with the shared Common resource pool (below). The common fraction is the chance each roll comes from that shared pool.
  • Curated milestone bags ignore the common pool entirely — they only ever drop their hand-picked contents.
  • The Chance column is the relative odds within that pool — a higher number means that item shows up more often.
