docs.title: Field notes
docs.text:
The real process behind these items — what they are, how they were used, and why they matter. Tap to expand.

ACattitude control array

Reaction wheels or CMGs for precise spacecraft orientation without propellant expenditure.

A set of spinning flywheels (reaction wheels) or control moment gyroscopes (CMGs) that exchange angular momentum with the spacecraft to rotate it without firing thrusters. Enables precise pointing of instruments, solar panels, and antennas.

Real-world use · The ISS uses four control moment gyroscopes to maintain attitude, saving significant propellant. The Hubble Space Telescope uses reaction wheels for arcsecond-level pointing precision. CMGs are more power-efficient than thrusters for continuous attitude maintenance and are standard on all large spacecraft.

  • When · First use on OSO-1 (1962); widespread use throughout the space age.
  • Where · On all spacecraft requiring controlled attitude -- from CubeSats to space stations.

Why it matters · Attitude control without propellant expenditure is an elegant engineering solution: use stored angular momentum to rotate the spacecraft, then periodically desaturate using thrusters when the wheels are spinning near maximum speed. This is the space equivalent of the flywheel in a steam engine -- a mechanical store of rotational energy that smooths out variations. The flywheel appeared in T2; the attitude control array is its T6 descendant. In Conduvia, proper attitude control is required for rendezvous, solar panel orientation, and instrument operation.

BCbasic comms satellite

First-generation communications relay satellite -- enables over-the-horizon contact.

A basic geostationary or low-Earth-orbit communications relay satellite carrying S-band and X-band transponders. Receives signals from one ground station and retransmits them to another, enabling contact between surface locations with no line-of-sight.

Real-world use · Telstar 1 (1962) was the first active communications satellite; Syncom 3 (1964) the first geostationary comms satellite. Today's GEO comms satellites carry hundreds of transponders covering vast geographic areas. LEO constellations (Starlink, OneWeb) use many smaller satellites to provide global broadband.

  • When · 1962 (Telstar) onward; mass deployment of LEO constellations 2019–present.
  • Where · LEO (200–2000 km altitude) for low-latency coverage; GEO (35 786 km) for hemispheric coverage with three spacecraft.

Why it matters · A single comms satellite enables reliable contact between any two points that can see it -- a capability that took surface relay towers, cables, or ship messengers previously. In network terms: one satellite links every surface node with clear sky access to every other such node, replacing N² direct connections with N connections to one hub. This is the T6 version of the same connectivity principle introduced in T0 by waypoints and camp markers -- but now the reach spans a planet.

BLbasic launch pad complex

Ground support infrastructure for a small orbital launcher.

A launch pad complex including a launch mount, propellant loading lines, flame trench and deflector, umbilical tower, and basic blast-resistant service structures. Designed to support one launch configuration at a time.

Real-world use · Launch pads are expensive, specialised infrastructure. The flame trench redirects combustion gases to prevent structural damage on ignition. The flame deluge system quenches engine exhaust and suppresses acoustic damage. Umbilical lines supply propellant, pressurisation, and power to the vehicle until milliseconds before launch.

  • When · 1950s onward with modern launch complexes.
  • Where · Coastal or inland sites with clear airspace, preferably at low latitudes to maximise rotational velocity contribution to orbital energy.

Why it matters · The launch pad is infrastructure, not a product -- it does not go to orbit. It is the prerequisite for everything that does. This is a pattern repeated throughout Conduvia: kilns make ceramics but stay on the ground; bloomeries make iron but don't move; launch pads support spacecraft but are not spacecraft. Infrastructure cost is real. The launch pad requires significant resources to build, and its capabilities directly constrain what vehicles you can fly from it.

BLbasic life-support loop

Closed-loop atmospheric control -- recycles CO₂ to O₂ and manages humidity.

A basic environmental control and life support sub-system providing atmospheric revitalisation: CO₂ removal, O₂ generation via water electrolysis, trace contaminant control, and humidity management. Designed for a small crew in a pressurised module.

Real-world use · The International Space Station uses the Environmental Control and Life Support System (ECLSS), which includes the Carbon Dioxide Removal Assembly (CDRA), the Oxygen Generation Assembly (OGA) using electrolysis, and the Water Recovery System (WRS). These systems recycle ~90% of water from humidity, urine, and other sources. Without them, resupply would be prohibitively expensive.

  • When · 1960s (basic Gemini systems) through present (ISS ECLSS).
  • Where · Any crewed spacecraft or habitat. Scaling up to closed ecological systems (biospheres) is an active research area.

Why it matters · Life support forces you to think in closed loops rather than linear flows. On Earth, you exhale CO₂ and it is absorbed by plants. On a space station, you must actively remove it or you die within hours. The same logic applies to water, waste, and atmosphere composition. The closed-loop life support system is the physical implementation of circular economy thinking: waste output of one process is input to another. CO₂ → electrolysis → O₂; condensed water → filtration → drinking water. In Conduvia, this module enables sustained lunar or orbital base operations.

DIdemo ice ISRU extractor

In-situ resource utilisation demo unit -- extracts water ice from lunar regolith.

A demonstration-scale ISRU (In-Situ Resource Utilisation) system designed to excavate and thermally process permanently-shadowed lunar regolith to extract water ice, then electrolyse it into hydrogen and oxygen for propellant or life support.

Real-world use · NASA's MOXIE experiment on Perseverance demonstrated oxygen production from Martian CO₂. Lunar ISRU for water extraction targets permanently shadowed regions (PSRs) at the poles where ice deposits have been confirmed by orbital spectroscopy and the LCROSS impact. Producing propellant in-situ is the key to economically viable lunar surface operations -- launching all propellant from Earth is extremely expensive.

  • When · 2020s (demonstration missions); operational systems projected for 2030s.
  • Where · Lunar south pole permanently shadowed craters (Shackleton, Haworth, Nobile) where water ice concentrations are highest.

Why it matters · ISRU is the concept that breaks the fundamental limitation of space exploration: you cannot carry everything you need from Earth. If you can extract water from lunar ice, you have propellant (H₂/O₂) for rockets, water for life support, and oxygen for breathing -- all from local resources. This is the T6 version of the same idea introduced in T0 by foraging: use what's already where you are rather than carrying everything. The difference is scale and technology. In Conduvia, the ISRU extractor is a prerequisite for self-sustaining off-world operations.

DLdistilled LOX propellant

Liquid oxygen (LOX) -- the oxidizer half of kerolox and hydrolox rocket engines.

Cryogenic liquid oxygen (-183 °C) produced by fractional distillation of air and used as the oxidizer in liquid-propellant rocket engines. Provides the oxygen for combustion without carrying an air supply; LOX has a much higher density than gaseous oxygen and allows compact propellant tanks.

Real-world use · Liquid oxygen as rocket oxidizer was proposed by Robert Goddard and Konstantin Tsiolkovsky independently. The first LOX/gasoline rocket flight was Goddard's 1926 launch. LOX is produced in bulk by the Linde-Frankl process -- air is compressed, cooled, and fractionated. It is pale blue, highly reactive, and must be handled with care to avoid contact with organic materials.

  • When · 1926 (first flight) onward; industrial LOX since the 1890s.
  • Where · Air separation units co-located with launch facilities to minimise cryogen boil-off during transport.

Why it matters · LOX is the oxidizer that makes chemical rocketry possible -- in space, there is no atmospheric oxygen, so rockets must carry their own. The cryogenic nature of LOX (-183 °C) imposes major engineering constraints: insulated tanks, cryogenic-rated seals, controlled venting to prevent overpressure, and rapid topping-off before launch as boil-off removes propellant continuously. In Conduvia, the LOX production chain (air separation → distillation → cryogenic storage) represents industrial gas processing at scale.

EUenriched uranium fuel rod

Slightly enriched UO₂ ceramic pellets in zircaloy cladding -- the reactor fuel element.

A fuel assembly containing uranium dioxide pellets enriched to 3–5% U-235 (natural uranium is 0.7%) pressed into ceramic form and clad in zirconium alloy tubes. The ceramic form is chosen for stability at extreme temperatures; zircaloy cladding provides a structural and containment barrier while being nearly transparent to neutrons.

Real-world use · Uranium enrichment is the most technically demanding step in the nuclear fuel cycle. Natural uranium must be converted to UF₆ gas, then processed through gaseous diffusion or centrifuge cascades to increase the U-235 fraction. The fuel fabrication step then converts enriched uranium back to ceramic UO₂, pressed into pellets, sintered, and clad in tubes.

  • When · 1940s (enrichment development for weapons); commercial fuel fabrication 1950s onward.
  • Where · Enrichment facilities (centrifuge cascades); fuel fabrication plants; delivered to reactor sites under strict safeguards.

Why it matters · The fuel rod embodies the nuclear fuel cycle -- one of the most complex industrial supply chains humans operate. Mine → mill → convert → enrich → fabricate → operate → cool → reprocess or store. Each step requires specialised infrastructure and strict material accounting. The ceramic fuel form is a deliberate engineering choice: UO₂ melts at 2865 °C, far above operating temperatures, and if cladding fails the ceramic holds fission products rather than releasing them. In Conduvia, fuel rods are the input to the nuclear reactor and represent the peak of the T6 material processing chain.

KTkerolox test rocket engine

RP-1/LOX first-stage engine -- the workhorse of early orbital access.

A regeneratively-cooled liquid-propellant rocket engine burning refined kerosene (RP-1) with liquid oxygen (LOX). The kerolox combination offers high density and good specific impulse for first-stage use, with decades of flight heritage. This unit is a development engine validated on the test stand before flight.

Real-world use · Kerolox engines power some of the most prolific launchers in history: the R-7 family (Soyuz), the Falcon 9/Falcon Heavy, and the Saturn V first stage (F-1). The kerosene/LOX combination was chosen over hydrogen because RP-1 has a higher energy density by volume, is easier to store, and turbopumps handle it more reliably than cryogenic hydrogen at the engineering maturity of the 1960s.

  • When · 1950s onward; the R-7 flew in 1957, Falcon 9 first flew 2010.
  • Where · Engine test facilities first; then launch pads once qualification is complete.

Why it matters · Rocket engines are the single most thermodynamically demanding machine humanity builds routinely. Chamber pressures of 200 bar, combustion temperatures of 3500 K, and structural mass fractions of 1–2% of thrust force -- the engineering margins are extremely tight. Regenerative cooling (routing propellant through channels in the nozzle wall before it enters the chamber) is what makes the structure survive combustion temperatures that would melt any known material. In Conduvia, this engine is the proof-of-concept for the orbital stack. You test it on the stand, validate performance data, and then integrate it into the launch vehicle.

LWlight-water nuclear reactor

Pressurised or boiling water fission reactor -- high power density, no combustion.

A thermal fission reactor using slightly enriched uranium fuel, ordinary water (H₂O) as both moderator and coolant, and a reactor pressure vessel to contain the primary circuit. Produces steam to drive turbines for electricity generation at power densities no chemical fuel can approach.

Real-world use · Light-water reactors (PWR and BWR) produce roughly 70% of all nuclear electricity worldwide. The design emerged from the US Navy's submarine propulsion programme in the 1950s under Admiral Rickover. The pressurised water concept was chosen for its inherent negative temperature coefficient of reactivity -- as the core heats up, the reaction rate naturally decreases, providing passive safety.

  • When · First submarine (USS Nautilus) 1955; first commercial PWR (Shippingport) 1958.
  • Where · Coastal or riverine locations for cooling water supply; remote from population centres for safety radius.

Why it matters · The light-water reactor is the step where energy production breaks free of combustion entirely. Every previous tier burns something: wood, charcoal, coke, coal gas, oil. The reactor fissions uranium -- a physical process that releases orders of magnitude more energy per kilogram than any chemical reaction. The challenge is not producing the energy but controlling it and converting it to usable forms (electricity). The containment vessel, control rods, and emergency core cooling systems are all answers to the same question: how do you safely handle a process that runs at millions of degrees Celsius inside the fuel pellets?

LSlunar solar power plant

Large-area photovoltaic array for sustained lunar surface power -- no atmosphere to scatter light.

A deployed photovoltaic array designed for the lunar surface. Without an atmosphere, solar irradiance is the full 1361 W/m² at all times the sun is visible. Combined with high-efficiency multi-junction cells, a lunar solar plant produces more power per square metre than an equivalent terrestrial array.

Real-world use · Solar panels on the ISS generate about 90 kW from 2500 m² of arrays. On the lunar surface, Artemis base camp planning calls for kilopower nuclear reactors for the polar base (where the sun is never high) and solar arrays for equatorial surface ops. SpaceX Starship's lunar variant and the ESA lunar surface power concepts both use solar as the primary power source where applicable.

  • When · ISS arrays operational 2000; lunar surface solar concepts in active development 2020s.
  • Where · Equatorial and mid-latitude lunar sites with continuous sun access. Polar sites need nuclear because of permanent shadow periods.

Why it matters · Lunar solar is more powerful per area than Earth solar because there is no atmosphere to scatter or absorb light. But it has a critical vulnerability: the lunar night lasts 14 Earth days. Any base that relies on solar alone needs either enormous battery storage or secondary power for the night cycle. This is the same intermittency problem as terrestrial renewables, scaled to 336-hour night cycles. In Conduvia, combining lunar solar with nuclear provides the redundant power that sustained operations require.

OTorbital three-stage rocket stack

Three-stage orbital launch vehicle -- the result of the full Tier 6 build chain.

A complete orbital launch vehicle assembled from a first stage, second stage, and payload fairing. Three-stage architecture allows each stage to shed mass as propellant is consumed, enabling payloads to reach orbital velocity that a single-stage vehicle cannot achieve with chemical propellants.

Real-world use · Multi-stage rocketry was proposed by Konstantin Tsiolkovsky in the 1890s and enabled by the Rocket Equation. Staging is necessary because no practical propellant gives enough specific impulse for a single-stage vehicle to reach orbit with a useful payload fraction. The Saturn V (3-stage), Soyuz (2-stage with strap-ons), and Falcon 9 (2-stage) all use staging for this reason.

  • When · 1940s (V-2 precursors) through present.
  • Where · Launch complexes with vertical assembly facilities. The stack is assembled vertically, transported to the pad, and fuelled for launch.

Why it matters · The three-stage rocket is the culmination of every tier in Conduvia. Its mass breakdown illustrates the challenge: at liftoff, propellant is ~90% of the vehicle's mass. The payload may be 2–3%. Everything in between -- structure, engines, avionics, plumbing -- must be as light as possible. The rocket equation (Tsiolkovsky's) states this precisely: delta-v = Isp × g₀ × ln(m_wet / m_dry). Higher specific impulse, or higher mass ratio, equals more delta-v -- the only thing that matters for changing orbits.

RKrefined kerosene propellant

Highly refined kerosene (RP-1) for rocket engine use -- low-sulfur, narrow cut.

A highly refined grade of kerosene with tightly controlled chemical composition, extremely low sulfur and olefin content, and a narrow distillation range. Standard fuel for kerolox rocket engines; chosen for its volumetric energy density, stability, and compatibility with metal turbopump hardware.

Real-world use · RP-1 (Rocket Propellant-1) was developed in the 1950s as a standardised rocket fuel meeting MIL-P-25576 specification. It is refined from crude oil through fractional distillation, hydrotreatment, and strict quality control. Standard kerosene cannot substitute -- contaminants cause coking in the regenerative cooling channels.

  • When · 1957 onward (Sputnik R-7 used RP-1 precursor); RP-1 specification formalised by 1959.
  • Where · Produced at petroleum refineries with specialised processing units; stored cryogenically if sub-cooled.

Why it matters · RP-1 illustrates that 'fuel' is not a single material -- specification matters. The difference between road diesel and RP-1 is a long list of impurity limits and a controlled distillation cut. That specificity is what makes it work in a turbopump at 100 000 RPM without fouling the lines. In Conduvia, the refining chain from raw petroleum to RP-1 represents T6 chemical processing.

RRrobotic regolith drill

Autonomous drill head for lunar or planetary surface excavation.

A remotely-operated or autonomous rotary-percussive drill designed to excavate compacted regolith or rock and feed it to an ISRU hopper. Hardened for abrasive regolith conditions and radiatively cooled for use in vacuum.

Real-world use · Regolith is highly abrasive due to the lack of weathering -- particles are sharp and angular. Drill bits used in vacuum must be hardened beyond terrestrial standards. The ESA/NASA PILOT project and several commercial ventures are developing regolith excavation systems for near-term lunar ISRU missions.

  • When · 2020s development; operational systems projected for 2030s lunar surface.
  • Where · Lunar surface, particularly in permanently shadowed regions for ice access.

Why it matters · The regolith drill is mining technology adapted to an environment without water, atmosphere, or heavy machinery infrastructure. Compare it to T0's hammerstone: both are percussion-based excavation tools. The principles are identical; the engineering constraints are completely different. In space, every design choice must account for vacuum, extreme thermal cycling (-170 °C to +120 °C on the lunar surface), abrasive unweathered particles, and the impossibility of easy maintenance.

SDstandard docking port

Hard-dock interface for joining spacecraft and station modules.

A mechanical and electrical interface assembly allowing two spacecraft to attach firmly, share power and data, and transfer crew and cargo between sealed volumes. Uses capture latches, alignment guides, and sealing gaskets for vacuum-tight connection.

Real-world use · The first docking in space occurred with Gemini 8 (1966). The International Docking System Standard (IDSS) was developed to allow spacecraft from different nations to dock to a common interface -- the ISS uses both Russian APAS docking systems and the US NDS (NASA Docking System). SpaceX Dragon, Boeing Starliner, and future commercial vehicles all use a standard docking interface.

  • When · 1966 (first docking) onward; IDSS standardised 2011.
  • Where · On all spacecraft and station modules designed for rendezvous-and-docking operations.

Why it matters · The docking port is an interface standard -- its entire value comes from standardisation. A non-standard docking port is useless; a standard one connects any conforming spacecraft to any conforming station. Interface standards are how complex systems scale. In Conduvia, standard ports enable modular station assembly -- just as Conduvia's crafting uses standardised item IDs to connect recipes across tiers without hardcoding every combination.

VEvertical engine test stand

Vertical hot-fire stand -- validate engine performance before flight integration.

A structural test facility that holds a rocket engine in the firing position, with propellant feed, ignition, and data-acquisition systems. Used to measure thrust, specific impulse, and combustion stability before the engine is qualified for flight.

Real-world use · Every rocket engine is test-fired extensively before flight. NASA Stennis Space Center and SpaceX's test facilities in Texas have fired engines for hundreds of thousands of cumulative seconds before any given design reaches a launch vehicle. Testing catches manufacturing defects, design anomalies, and material fatigue before they cause mission loss.

  • When · 1940s onward as a standard practice.
  • Where · Remote sites with propellant infrastructure and acceptable blast radius. Water is often piped in for cooling and acoustic suppression.

Why it matters · The test stand embodies the principle of progressive validation: test the component, then the sub-system, then the system, then the vehicle. This 'test before fly' approach is what separates modern engineered systems from early trial-and-error. In Conduvia it gates engine integration: you can't put an unvalidated engine into the orbital stack.

docs.title: Recipes
docs.text:
Every recipe in Conduvia — 1334 in total — pulled straight from the game’s recipe database. Each card shows what goes in, what comes out, where you make it, and how long it takes. Byproducts (a second or third output) are flagged, because real processing usually throws something useful — or annoying — off to the side.

1334 recipes · 278 gathered from the world · 193 with byproducts

Tier 0 — Survival & Gathering435 recipes Tier 1 — Stone & Early Materials88 recipes Tier 2 — Metal Ages: smelting & alloys79 recipes Tier 3–4 — Steam, Coke & Steel94 recipes Tier 5 — Electric & Chemistry99 recipes Fabrication & Components539 recipes

While it runs it puts AC onto the line and vents carbon dioxide; the gas in the input slot is the fuel it burns, and the electricity leaves through the power network rather than as an item. Stop feeding gas and the set winds down and stops supplying the grid. e6_genset_gas_engine_run Gathered from the world 4 CBclay bucket×1 obtain=craft_or_find • ★ FLOOR PICKUP ITEM ★ -- craft_or_find world_bucket_clay IBiron bucket×1 obtain=craft_or_find • ★ FLOOR PICKUP ITEM ★ -- craft_or_find world_bucket_iron WBwooden bucket×1 obtain=craft_or_find • ★ FLOOR PICKUP ITEM ★ -- craft_or_find world_bucket_wood HPhydrogen peroxide×1 chemName=HydrogenPeroxide,formula=H2O2,elements=H:2|O:2,obtain=anthraquinone_process • ★ FLOOR PICKUP ITEM ★ -- anthraquinone_process world_peroxide_hydrogen Glass Bench 4 SSsmall silicon diode×4 RSraw silicon wafer×1 DCdrawn copper wire×1 TLtin-lead solder×1 → SSsmall silicon diode×4 Glass Bench Station: glass bench itemdb_diode_silicon_small_glass_bench SNsignal NPN transistor×4 RSraw silicon wafer×1 DCdrawn copper wire×1 TLtin-lead solder×1 → SNsignal NPN transistor×4 Glass Bench Station: glass bench itemdb_transistor_npn_signal_glass_bench GDglass diode tube×1 CFcarbon filament preform×1 ICinsulated copper wire×1 CHcharcoal×1 → GDglass diode tube×1 Glass Bench Station: glass bench itemdb_tube_diode_glass_glass_bench GTglass triode tube×1 CFcarbon filament preform×1 CFcarbon filament preform×1 ICinsulated copper wire×1 CHcharcoal×1 → GTglass triode tube×1 Glass Bench Station: glass bench itemdb_tube_triode_glass_glass_bench Iron Anvil 1 SIsoldering iron×1 CRcopper rod×1 WIwrought iron bar×1 WHwood haft×1 → SIsoldering iron×1 Iron Anvil Station: iron anvil block itemdb_soldering_iron_iron_anvil_block Mercury Arc Rectifier 1 DPDC power charge×2 APAC power charge×3 → DPDC power charge×2 Mercury Arc Rectifier 10s T3 Mercury-arc rectifier: the AC current jumps as a glowing arc off a pool of mercury, and only the 'forward' half of each wave gets through -- turning AC into DC. The arc and mercury vapor waste about a third of the power, so you get two DC charges out for every three AC charges in. e6_rectify_ac_to_dc Overshot Waterwheel 1 SPshaft-power charge×2 RWraw water×1 → SPshaft-power charge×2 Overshot Waterwheel 10s T3 Water is led over the top of the wheel so its weight, not just its push, drives the rotation. Far more efficient than the undershot wheel -- one unit of water yields two charges of shaft work -- but it needs a head of water (a mill race or small dam) to feed the top. e6_waterwheel_overshot_mech Post Mill (Windmill) 1 SPshaft-power charge×4 ROrope×1 → SPshaft-power charge×4 Post Mill (Windmill) 60s T3 Canvas sails on a lattice frame catch the wind and turn the windshaft -- power with no stream and no fuel, for flat country away from rivers. It is slow and gusty, and the rigging weathers, so you re-rope the sails now and then; in return a single re-rig yields a good batch of shaft work. e6_windmill_mech Simple Steam Engine 1 SPshaft-power charge×2 SCsteam charge×1 → SPshaft-power charge×2 Simple Steam Engine 8s T3 A reciprocating steam engine turns a puff of boiler steam into steady shaft rotation through its piston, connecting rod and flywheel. Unlike water or wind it runs anywhere, day or night -- the prime mover that frees the workshop from the riverbank. One steam charge makes two charges of shaft work. e6_steam_engine_mech Small AC Generator 2 APAC power charge×1 SCsteam charge×1 → APAC power charge×1 Small AC Generator 25s T3 This one uses slip rings instead of a commutator, so it lets the back-and-forth AC current pass through unchanged. One steam charge makes one output, at single-phase (basic workshop) voltage. e6_generator_ac_small_charge APAC power charge×1 SPshaft-power charge×1 → APAC power charge×1 Small AC Generator 25s T3 A small AC generator turned by any shaft -- water, wind, or steam. Slip rings pass the coil's alternating current straight through. One charge of shaft work makes one charge of single-phase AC. e6_generator_ac_small_mech_charge Steam Machine Shop 5 SCsteam control valve×1 RSrolled steel plate×1 TSturned steel shaft×1 BIbronze ingot×1 → SCsteam control valve×1 Steam Machine Shop Station: machine shop (steam) itemdb_control_valve_steam_machine_shop_steam LFlamp filament DC supply×1 ICinsulated copper wire×1 LAlaminated armature core×1 WIwrought-iron magnet core×1 → LFlamp filament DC supply×1 Steam Machine Shop Station: machine shop (steam) itemdb_filament_supply_dc_machine_shop_steam FHfluid handler station×1 WIwrought-iron steam pipe×3 SCsteam control valve×1 TLtank level sensor×1 RSrolled steel plate×2 → FHfluid handler station×1 Steam Machine Shop placeModel=station_fluid_handler • Station: machine shop (steam) itemdb_station_fluid_handler_machine_shop_steam VSvent stack×1 RSrolled steel plate×5 SRsteel rivet×8 WIwrought-iron steam pipe×1 → VSvent stack×1 Steam Machine Shop placeModel=station_vent_stack • Station: machine shop (steam) itemdb_station_vent_stack_machine_shop_steam LVlaboratory vacuum pump×1 RSrolled steel plate×1 WIwrought-iron steam pipe×1 ICinsulated copper wire×1 LFleather flat belt×1 → LVlaboratory vacuum pump×1 Steam Machine Shop placeModel=vacuum_pump_lab • Station: machine shop (steam) itemdb_vacuum_pump_lab_machine_shop_steam Step-Down Transformer 1 APAC power charge×1 APAC power charge×2 → APAC power charge×1 Step-Down Transformer 8s T3 Lowers the high transmission voltage back down to a safe, usable level. Pair it with a step-up transformer at the generator end. e6_transformer_step_down Step-Up Transformer 1 APAC power charge×2 APAC power charge×1 → APAC power charge×2 Step-Up Transformer 8s T3 An iron-core transformer raises the voltage to send power long distances, doubling how much charge moves per cycle -- but you have to step it back down at the far end. Works on AC only; DC can't use a transformer. e6_transformer_step_up Three-Phase AC Generator 2 APAC power charge×3 SCsteam charge×1 → APAC power charge×3 Three-Phase AC Generator 25s T3 Three sets of coils spaced a third of a turn apart (120°) make three times the power from the same engine. This three-phase power runs big AC motors and feeds a whole district. e6_generator_three_phase_charge APAC power charge×3 SPshaft-power charge×1 → APAC power charge×3 Three-Phase AC Generator 25s T3 Three coil sets spaced a third of a turn apart make three times the power from the same shaft. Run it off a big overshot wheel or a steam engine to feed a whole district with three-phase AC. e6_generator_three_phase_mech_charge Undershot Waterwheel 1 SPshaft-power charge×1 RWraw water×2 → SPshaft-power charge×1 Undershot Waterwheel 14s T3 A wheel dipped into a passing stream; the current pushes the bottom paddles round. This is the crude, low-head water mill -- easy to drop on any flowing water, but wasteful, so it takes two units of stream flow to turn out a single charge of shaft work. e6_waterwheel_undershot_mech Vent Stack 1 EGempty gas cylinder×1 HChydrogen cylinder×1 → EGempty gas cylinder×1 Vent Stack Station: vent stack itemdb_cylinder_gas_basic_empty_station_vent_stack Workbench 2 AFA-frame gin pole×1 RTrough timber beam×3 ROrope×4 SHsheave×1 ISiron strap×2 → AFA-frame gin pole×1 Workbench 75s T1 A guyed lifting mast -- two legs lashed at the head, stayed with rope, a sheave at the top. Swing heavy timber and cut stone up onto a wall with it. It is the crane of the pre-machine age: nothing but leverage, rope, and a steady hand on the tackle. a_frame_gin_pole_build_manual ISindustrial safety cage×1 RSrolled steel beam×4 RSrolled steel plate×2 HKheavy knife switch×1 → ISindustrial safety cage×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_safety_cage_industrial_workbench

top of the main condenser of the air-separation column, where it concentrates. re_neon NGnitrogen gas×7 OGoxygen gas×2 AGargon gas×1 LAliquid air×10 → NGnitrogen gas×7 OGoxygen gas×2 AGargon gas×1 Air Separation Unit 90s T3 120 kJ +2 byproducts Rectify liquid air in a double column: counter-current distillation pulls volatile nitrogen (-196C) up and out the top, oxygen (-183C) collects at the bottom, and argon (-186C) is drawn from a side column. One feed, three pure products -- the cleanest multi-output in the plant. asu_rectify_air LAliquid air×10 CAcompressed air×10 → LAliquid air×10 Air Separation Unit 60s T3 220 kJ Compress filtered air, scrub out CO2 and moisture (they freeze and plug the column), then chill it against the cold product streams until it condenses. Liquefying the air is the energy-hungry step; the separation that follows is nearly free. asu_liquefy_air Aluminothermic Crucible 3 FEferroniobium×1 SLslag×1 NPniobium pentoxide (Nb2O5)×1 AIaluminum ingot×2 IOiron ore pellets×1 → FEferroniobium×1 SLslag×1 Aluminothermic Crucible 100s T5 280 kJ +1 byproduct Aluminothermic reduction: ignite Nb2O5 with aluminium and iron oxide. The thermite-like reaction (3 Nb2O5 + 10 Al -> 6 Nb + 5 Al2O3) runs white-hot and yields ferroniobium directly, with alumina slag floating off -- exactly how FeNb is made for steel and superalloys. nb_aluminothermic NMniobium metal×1 SLslag×1 NPniobium pentoxide (Nb2O5)×1 AIaluminum ingot×2 → NMniobium metal×1 SLslag×1 Aluminothermic Crucible 100s T6 160 kJ +1 byproduct Aluminothermic reduction of white Nb2O5 with aluminium to pure niobium metal, alumina slag floating off. Unlike the ferroniobium route this skips the iron oxide, yielding nozzle-grade metal. re_niobium_metal CGcapacitor-grade tantalum powder×1 SLslag×1 TPtantalum pentoxide (Ta2O5)×1 AIaluminum ingot×3 → CGcapacitor-grade tantalum powder×1 SLslag×1 Aluminothermic Crucible 110s T5 310 kJ +1 byproduct Reduce Ta2O5 with aluminium to a high-purity tantalum metal powder. Its huge surface area and self-healing oxide are exactly what a capacitor anode needs. ta_reduce_powder Amine Gas Treater 1 MGmethane gas×2 CDcarbon dioxide gas×1 HShydrogen sulfide gas×1 RNraw natural gas×3 → MGmethane gas×2 CDcarbon dioxide gas×1 HShydrogen sulfide gas×1 Amine Gas Treater 70s T4 90 kJ +2 byproducts Wash the raw gas with an amine solution to strip the acid gases: out comes sweet pipeline methane, plus the CO2 and hydrogen sulfide that each have to be dealt with separately. ng_amine_sweeten Anode Baking Furnace 1 PCprebaked carbon anode×1 CFcoke fuel×2 → PCprebaked carbon anode×1 Anode Baking Furnace 75s T3 420 kJ Bind petroleum coke with pitch and bake it into a dense, conductive block. These prebaked anodes are fed into the pots and slowly eaten as the cell runs. al_bake_anode AOD Converter 1 SSstainless steel ingot×2 SLslag×1 SIsteel ingot×2 FEferrochrome×1 FEferronickel×1 BLburned lime (quicklime)×1 → SSstainless steel ingot×2 SLslag×1 AOD Converter 140s T4 390 kJ +1 byproduct Argon-oxygen decarburization: blow an O2/Ar mix through a chromium-bearing melt. Diluting the oxygen with argon lets carbon burn out while chromium does NOT oxidise away -- so cheap high-carbon ferrochrome can be used. Makes ~75% of the worlds stainless steel. iron_aod_stainless APT Crystallizer 1 APammonium paratungstate (APT)×2 SHsodium hydroxide×1 STsodium tungstate solution×2 AGammonia gas×2 → APammonium paratungstate (APT)×2 SHsodium hydroxide×1 APT Crystallizer 100s T4 120 kJ +1 byproduct Convert the tungstate solution to ammonium paratungstate and crystallise it out with ammonia. No flotation here -- bauxite is refined chemically, not concentrated mechanically. al_crush_bauxite CCcrushed chalcopyrite×5 SDstone dust×1 RCraw chalcopyrite ore×6 → CCcrushed chalcopyrite×5 SDstone dust×1 Trip Hammer 45s T1 +1 byproduct Coarse stamp-crush to liberate chalcopyrite from gangue. ~1 in 6 reports to fines as stone dust. Muscle/trip-hammer -- pay in time, not energy. cu_crush_chalcopyrite CCcrushed chromite ore×2 SDstone dust×1 RCraw chromite ore×2 → CCcrushed chromite ore×2 SDstone dust×1 Trip Hammer 30s T2 16 kJ +1 byproduct Crush friable lump chromite to an even grain. Chromite is brittle and sheds fines, so it is crushed deliberately and then re-agglomerated rather than charged as-mined. fcr_crush_chromite COcrushed oxide copper ore×2 SDstone dust×1 OCoxidized copper ore×2 → COcrushed oxide copper ore×2 SDstone dust×1 Trip Hammer 25s T1 12 kJ +1 byproduct Crush oxidized ore to a coarse, permeable gravel (12-50 mm). Oxide heaps want a COARSE crush -- fine grinding would only choke acid percolation and burn extra acid on freshly exposed gangue. cu_crush_oxide GCground chalcopyrite×4 SDstone dust×1 CCcrushed chalcopyrite×5 → GCground chalcopyrite×4 SDstone dust×1 Trip Hammer 60s T1 +1 byproduct Fine grind to ~75 micron flotation size. Over-grind sheds a little more stone dust. cu_grind_chalcopyrite IOiron ore concentrate×1 IOiron ore rock×1 → IOiron ore concentrate×1 Trip Hammer Station: stamp_mill_manual_trip_hammer • Byproduct: tailings itemdb_ore_iron_concentrate_stamp_mill_manual_trip_hammer PPpaper pulp×1 PFplant fibers×2 RWraw water×1 WAwood ash×1 → PPpaper pulp×1 Trip Hammer 70s T1 180 kJ Soak plant fibre in water with a little wood-ash lye and beat it under the trip hammer into a pulp. ppr_pulp_fiber PPpaper pulp×2 LClinen cloth×1 RWraw water×1 → PPpaper pulp×2 Trip Hammer 60s T1 180 kJ Beat linen rag in water under the trip hammer into a fine, high-yield rag pulp. ppr_pulp_rag PPpaper pulp×2 SLstripped log×1 RWraw water×1 SLslaked lime×1 → PPpaper pulp×2 Trip Hammer 110s T1 200 kJ Cook wood with slaked lime and beat it into a coarse wood pulp at the trip hammer. ppr_pulp_wood Uranium Conversion Reactor 3 UHuranium hexafluoride×1 UTuranium tetrafluoride (UF4)×1 FGfluorine gas×1 → UHuranium hexafluoride×1 Uranium Conversion Reactor 90s T6 150 kJ Fluorinate the green salt with elemental fluorine: UF4 + F2 -> UF6. The product sublimes to a gas at gentle heat -- the one uranium compound you can pump through a centrifuge. u_fluorinate_uf6 EUenriched UO2 powder×1 HAhydrofluoric acid×2 EUenriched UF6 (LEU)×1 RWraw water×2 HGhydrogen gas×1 → EUenriched UO2 powder×1 HAhydrofluoric acid×2 Uranium Conversion Reactor 110s T7 160 kJ +1 byproduct Hydrolyse and hydrogen-reduce enriched UF6 back to UO2 powder, recovering the fluorine as HF (fed straight back to conversion). Closing the fluorine loop is what makes conversion economic. u_reconvert_uo2_powder UTuranium tetrafluoride (UF4)×1 RWraw water×2 UDuranium dioxide (UO2)×1 HAhydrofluoric acid×4 → UTuranium tetrafluoride (UF4)×1 RWraw water×2 Uranium Conversion Reactor 100s T6 140 kJ +1 byproduct React UO2 with hydrogen fluoride: UO2 + 4 HF -> UF4 + 2 H2O. The oxide becomes the green salt, halfway to the volatile hexafluoride. u_hydrofluorinate_uf4 Vacuum Arc Remelt Furnace 3 TItitanium ingot×1 TStitanium sponge×3 → TItitanium ingot×1 Vacuum Arc Remelt Furnace 180s T4 1385 kJ Crush and compact the sponge into an electrode, then vacuum-arc-remelt it into a dense, homogeneous ingot -- often twice for aerospace grades. The vacuum keeps reactive titanium away from the oxygen and nitrogen it loves. ti_var_ingot ITInconel turbine rotor×1 ISInconel superalloy ingot×2 → ITInconel turbine rotor×1 Vacuum Arc Remelt Furnace 90s T6 220 kJ Cast and finish a bladed Inconel turbine wheel that survives the gas-generator exhaust. re_turbine_rotor ZIzirconium ingot×1 ZSzirconium sponge×3 → ZIzirconium ingot×1 Vacuum Arc Remelt Furnace 180s T4 1385 kJ Crush and compact the sponge into an electrode, then vacuum-arc-remelt it into a dense, homogeneous ingot. The vacuum keeps reactive zirconium clear of the oxygen and nitrogen it would otherwise gulp down. zr_var_ingot Wafer Fab Line 2 PSpolished silicon wafer×1 RSraw silicon wafer×1 → PSpolished silicon wafer×1 Wafer Fab Line 50s T4 70 kJ Chemically-mechanically polish (CMP) an as-cut wafer to an atomically smooth, defect-free mirror -- the pristine substrate fine logic circuits demand. si_polish_wafer RSraw silicon wafer×8 CSCzochralski silicon ingot×1 → RSraw silicon wafer×8 Wafer Fab Line 60s T4 80 kJ Slice the monocrystal ingot into thin wafers with a wire saw and lap them flat. As-cut wafers are matte and saw-damaged -- ready for rugged devices or for polishing. si_slice_wafer Workbench 24 SBsteel beam×1 RSrolled steel beam×1 SRsteel rivet×4 → SBsteel beam×1 Workbench 70s T4 Rivet up a steel span to carry heavy loads across wide openings no timber could manage. bld_build_beam_steel BBblank book×1 PApaper×5 LTlight tanned leather×1 PTplant thread×2 AGanimal glue×1 → BBblank book×1 Workbench 90s T1 70 kJ Fold and stitch paper sheets into a block, glue the spine, and bind it into leather covers to make a blank book. book_bind_blank LClinen cloth×1 PTplant thread×3 → LClinen cloth×1 Workbench 120s T1 200 kJ Weave spun plant thread into a bolt of light, breathable linen-type cloth. tex_weave_linen_cloth WCwool cloth×1 WYwool yarn×3 → WCwool cloth×1 Workbench 120s T1 200 kJ Warp the loom and weave wool yarn into a bolt of warm, hard-wearing cloth. tex_weave_wool_cloth COcopperas×2 IIiron ingot×1 CAchamber acid (dilute H2SO4)×1 → COcopperas×2 Workbench 80s T3 110 kJ Dissolve iron in dilute sulfuric acid and evaporate to pale green copperas crystals (iron sulfate). ink_make_copperas WFwool felt×1 WWwashed wool×2 RWraw water×1 → WFwool felt×1 Workbench 90s T1 200 kJ Agitate washed wool in hot water until the fibres lock together into a dense felt -- no spinning needed. tex_felt_wool FAflint-and-steel striker×2 RFraw flint×1 SIsteel ingot×1 → FAflint-and-steel striker×2 Workbench 60s T2 25 kJ Forge/fit a high-carbon steel striker to a flint nodule. Real flint-and-steel: the harder flint shaves heated steel particles that ignite -- the most primitive ore meets the steel chain. fl_fire_steel CSconcrete slab×1 WCwet concrete×4 SRsteel rebar×2 → CSconcrete slab×1 Workbench 120s T4 Cast a rebar slab to span wider and carry far more than timber. Heavy, so it needs solid supports beneath. bld_build_floor_concrete CFconcrete footing×1 WCwet concrete×4 SRsteel rebar×2 → CFconcrete footing×1 Workbench 120s T4 Pour a rebar-tied concrete footing, the strongest anchor and the best base for tall or heavy builds. bld_build_foundation_concrete SFstone footing×1 COcobblestone×6 LMlime mortar×2 → SFstone footing×1 Workbench 90s T2 Bed heavy stone in mortar to spread the load into the ground: a strong, low anchor for everything above. bld_build_foundation_stone HLHeap Leach Pad×1 PPPVC pipe×4 RRrounded river stone×4 RBrough board plank×2 → HLHeap Leach Pad×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_heap_leach_pad_workbench CIcarbon ink×2 BPblack pigment×1 AGanimal glue×1 RWraw water×1 → CIcarbon ink×2 Workbench 45s T1 60 kJ Suspend black carbon pigment in glue and water to make a writing ink. ink_make_carbon IGiron gall ink×2 CBcurled bark strip×2 COcopperas×1 RWraw water×1 → IGiron gall ink×2 Workbench 70s T1 70 kJ Steep bark tannin in water and react it with copperas to make dark, permanent iron-gall ink. ink_make_iron_gall KSKnapping Station×1 RRrounded river stone×2 RTrough timber beam×1 RCrawhide cord×1 → KSKnapping Station×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_knapping_station_workbench LYlye×1 WAwood ash×2 RWraw water×1 → LYlye×1 Workbench 50s T1 40 kJ Trickle water through wood ash to leach out a caustic lye solution. soap_leach_lye BPbrick pier×1 FCfired clay brick×8 CMcement mortar×2 → BPbrick pier×1 Workbench 90s T3 Lay fired brick in mortar into a stout pier: heavy, fireproof, and stiff against both load and sway. bld_build_pillar_brick CCconcrete column×1 RCreinforced concrete×3 → CCconcrete column×1 Workbench 90s T4 Cast a rebar-cored column, the workhorse vertical support for tall builds. bld_build_pillar_concrete TRtiled roof×1 RTrough timber beam×2 RBrough board plank×2 CRclay roof tile×10 → TRtiled roof×1 Workbench 100s T2 Lap fired clay tiles over a timber frame. Heavy and slower to build, but fireproof and long-lasting. bld_build_roof_tiled SSstone stair×1 COcobblestone×8 LMlime mortar×2 → SSstone stair×1 Workbench 90s T2 Set stone steps in mortar: heavy and slow, but fireproof and effectively permanent. bld_build_stair_stone PTplant thread×1 PFplant fibers×2 → PTplant thread×1 Workbench 60s T1 170 kJ Spin retted plant fibres into a fine, strong thread. tex_spin_plant_thread BWbrick wall×1 FCfired clay brick×10 CMcement mortar×3 → BWbrick wall×1 Workbench 90s T3 Lay fired brick in mortar into a load-bearing wall that can carry a floor or roof directly. bld_build_wall_brick CWconcrete wall×1 WCwet concrete×4 SRsteel rebar×1 → CWconcrete wall×1 Workbench 90s T4 Pour a concrete wall around light rebar: a dense, weatherproof shell that doubles as structure. bld_build_wall_concrete WRwool roving×1 WWwashed wool×1 → WRwool roving×1 Workbench 45s T1 180 kJ Comb washed wool so the fibres run parallel, drawing it into a soft roving sliver. tex_card_wool WYwool yarn×1 WRwool roving×1 → WYwool yarn×1 Workbench 75s T1 180 kJ Draw and twist the roving into a strong continuous wool yarn on the spindle. tex_spin_wool_yarn Yellowcake Precipitator 1 YEyellowcake×1 TAtailings×1 UPuranium pregnant liquor×3 AGammonia gas×1 → YEyellowcake×1 TAtailings×1 Yellowcake Precipitator 120s T6 110 kJ +1 byproduct Concentrate the liquor (solvent extraction / ion exchange) and precipitate ammonium diuranate with ammonia, then calcine it to bright-yellow U3O8 -- yellowcake, the form uranium is actually shipped in. u_precipitate_yellowcake

docs.title: Tier 2 — Metal Ages: smelting & alloys
docs.text:
79 recipes

Beehive Coke Oven 1 Camp Marker 5 Charcoal Clamp 1 Charcoal Drum Kiln 1 Charcoal Retort Kiln 1 Clay Bloomery 2 Clay Casting Pit 3 Clay Crucible 6 Earthen Kiln 1 Electric Arc Furnace 2 Electric Ball Mill 6 Electric Induction Furnace 3 Electric Jaw Crusher 6 Electrowinning Cell 1 Fire-Refining Furnace 2 Forge Hearth 1 Froth Flotation Cell 7 Gathered from the world 2 Iron Anvil 4 Roasting Pit 2 Rocker Box 2 Sluice Box 2 Steam Rolling Mill 1 Stone Anvil 2 Stone Mortar 1 Tailings Re-Wash Box 1 Trip Hammer 6 Wet High-Intensity Magnetic Separator (WHIMS) 2 Workbench 4 Zinc Distillation Retort 1 Beehive Coke Oven 1 CFcoke fuel×2 BCbituminous coal×3 → CFcoke fuel×2 Beehive Coke Oven 600s T1 Coking bakes bituminous coal at 900-1100°C without oxygen, driving off sulfur, tar, and volatile compounds. The resulting coke burns at blast-furnace temperatures that charcoal cannot sustain. t1_coke_production Camp Marker 5 SAstone anvil block×1 RRrounded river stone×4 MDmedium dry log×1 ROrope×1 → SAstone anvil block×1 Camp Marker station=true,station_input=3,station_output=1 • Station: camp_marker_basic itemdb_anvil_stone_camp_marker_basic BAbronze axe×1 BAbronze axe head×1 WHwood haft×1 ROrope×1 → BAbronze axe×1 Camp Marker 240s T1 Fit the axe head into the hole in the handle and tie it tight; a cast (poured-metal) axe keeps its edge far longer than a chipped-stone one. e2_haft_bronze_axe BPbronze pickaxe×1 BPbronze pick head×1 WHwood haft×1 ROrope×1 → BPbronze pickaxe×1 Camp Marker 240s T1 Wedge the pick head onto the handle and bind it tight; lets you mine oxide and sulfide ores (the two main kinds of metal ore). e2_haft_bronze_pickaxe BSbronze shovel×1 BSbronze shovel blade×1 WHwood haft×1 ROrope×1 → BSbronze shovel×1 Camp Marker 240s T1 Pin or tie the blade to the handle; it moves far more soil per swing than the crude wooden shovel. e2_haft_bronze_shovel ORore roasting pit×1 RRrounded river stone×6 LDloose dirt×4 MDmedium dry log×2 → ORore roasting pit×1 Camp Marker station=true,station_input=2,station_output=1,transport=chute • Station: camp_marker_basic itemdb_roasting_pit_camp_marker_basic Charcoal Clamp 1 CHcharcoal×3 MDmedium dry log×10 → CHcharcoal×3 Charcoal Clamp 600s T1 Earth-covered mound smoulders with restricted air. Most of the wood volatiles and much of its carbon burn off, so yield is poor and the burn needs many hours of tending. t1_charcoal_clamp Charcoal Drum Kiln 1 CHcharcoal×7 MDmedium dry log×10 → CHcharcoal×7 Charcoal Drum Kiln 300s T2 A closed steel drum controls airflow far better than an open mound, so less of the charge is lost to combustion. Faster and roughly double the yield of the earth clamp. t2_charcoal_drum Charcoal Retort Kiln 1 CHcharcoal×11 MDmedium dry log×10 → CHcharcoal×11 Charcoal Retort Kiln 180s T3 A sealed retort heats the wood from outside and admits no air, so almost none of the charge combusts. The wood-gas driven off is burned back under the retort to sustain heat. Highest yield and fastest of the three. t3_charcoal_retort Clay Bloomery 2 IBiron bloom×1 BSbloomery slag×4 CIcrushed iron ore×5 CHcharcoal×10 → IBiron bloom×1 BSbloomery slag×4 Clay Bloomery 360s T1 +1 byproduct Carbon monoxide from charcoal reduces FeO·OH to spongy metalite iron at ~1100-1200°C. The resulting bloom contains trapped slag and must be hammered to consolidate. t1_bloomery_bog_iron IBiron bloom×1 BSbloomery slag×4 RHroasted hematite×5 CHcharcoal×10 → IBiron bloom×1 BSbloomery slag×4 Clay Bloomery 420s T1 +1 byproduct Fe₂O₃ + 3CO → 2Fe + 3CO₂. Hematite is harder and needs longer reduction time than bog iron, but produces a cleaner bloom with less sulfur. t1_bloomery_hematite Clay Casting Pit 3 BAbronze axe head×1 SIstone ingot mold×1 BIbronze ingot×2 CHcharcoal×2 SIstone ingot mold×1 → BAbronze axe head×1 SIstone ingot mold×1 Clay Casting Pit 480s T1 +1 byproduct Melt the bronze over charcoal and pour it into the stone axe-head mold; knock it out once cool and grind the edge. e2_cast_bronze_axe_head BPbronze pick head×1 SIstone ingot mold×1 BIbronze ingot×3 CHcharcoal×2 SIstone ingot mold×1 → BPbronze pick head×1 SIstone ingot mold×1 Clay Casting Pit 540s T1 +1 byproduct Pour the heavier two-pointed pick head; it uses more metal than an axe head, so it needs a longer, hotter melt. e2_cast_bronze_pick_head BSbronze shovel blade×1 SIstone ingot mold×1 BIbronze ingot×2 CHcharcoal×2 SIstone ingot mold×1 → BSbronze shovel blade×1 SIstone ingot mold×1 Clay Casting Pit 480s T1 +1 byproduct Pour a wide, slightly curved blade; hammer the face smooth once cool so soil slides off instead of sticking. e2_cast_bronze_shovel_blade Clay Crucible 6 BIbronze ingot×9 CIcopper ingot×9 TItin ingot×1 → BIbronze ingot×9 Clay Crucible 150s T1 Controlled 9:1 Cu:Sn melt gives ~10% tin bronze -- the historically documented sweet spot. Below ~6% Sn gives little hardness gain; above ~17% the alloy becomes brittle. t1_alloy_bronze BIbronze ingot×3 RNraw native copper×5 RCraw cassiterite ore×2 CHcharcoal×8 → BIbronze ingot×3 Clay Crucible 240s T1 Smelting copper ore and cassiterite together without pre-measuring ingots produces an unpredictable tin fraction. Yield is lower than controlled alloying: 5 native copper + 2 cassiterite + 8 charcoal → 3 bronze here. The controlled route (same ore mass) smelts separately → 1 copper_ingot + 0.5 tin_ingot → alloy → 3 bronze AND you know the exact ~10% Sn. The Bronze Age switched to controlled alloying for consistency, not just yield. t1_cosmelt_crude_bronze CIcopper ingot×1 RNraw native copper×5 CHcharcoal×4 → CIcopper ingot×1 Clay Crucible 120s T1 35 kJ Era 3 (~20min). Reheat and hammer the billet with overlapping blows, rotating to draw it down to an even, measurable cross-section. Surface scale here is incidental -- no byproduct; collect pigment from the consolidation step instead. t1_smelt_native_copper CIcopper ingot×1 RNraw native copper×5 CHcharcoal×4 → CIcopper ingot×1 Clay Crucible 180s T1 Native copper is elemental metal -- no chemical reduction needed. Heat to ~1085°C in a clay crucible to melt and pour into a stone mold. t1_smelt_native_copper__1 TItin ingot×1 RCraw cassiterite ore×4 CHcharcoal×6 → TItin ingot×1 Clay Crucible 210s T1 SnO₂ + 2C → Sn + 2CO₂. Carbon reduction at ~1100°C is required before the metallic tin can be tapped -- its low melting point of 232°C only applies after reduction. t1_smelt_cassiterite TItin ingot×2 TCtin concentrate (black tin)×2 CHcharcoal×4 → TItin ingot×2 Clay Crucible 150s T1 Smelt washed black tin (the gravity concentrate) instead of raw lump ore. With most of the gangue already washed away, the same crucible recovers far more metal per load -- 2 concentrate -> 2 tin, against 4 raw ore -> 1 tin -- and on less charcoal. This is why tinners always dressed the ore before the blowing-house. t1_smelt_cassiterite_concentrate Earthen Kiln 1 CBclay bloomery furnace×1 EBearthenware brick×8 RCraw clay×4 CTclay tuyere×2 CHcharcoal×10 → CBclay bloomery furnace×1 Earthen Kiln transport=chute • Station: earthen kiln (updraft) itemdb_clay_bloomery_furnace_earthen_kiln_updraft Electric Arc Furnace 2 AAaerospace aluminum alloy×1 AIaluminum ingot×3 RCrefined copper ingot×1 → AAaerospace aluminum alloy×1 Electric Arc Furnace chemName=Aerospace aluminum alloy • Station: arc_furnace_electric itemdb_alloy_aluminum_aerospace_arc_furnace_electric CCcopper cathode×1 ASanode slime×1 BCblister copper anode×1 SAsulfuric acid×1 DPDC power charge×2 → CCcopper cathode×1 ASanode slime×1 Electric Arc Furnace 120s T4 +1 byproduct Hang the cast blister slab as an anode in a copper-sulfate and sulfuric-acid bath and pass direct current. Copper leaves the dirty anode and plates pure onto the cathode, while silver, gold, and selenium fall to the floor of the tank as anode slime. Slow and power-hungry, but it is the only way to reach the purity wire and electronics need. copper_electrorefine_cathode Electric Ball Mill 6 GCground chalcopyrite×4 CCcrushed chalcopyrite×4 APAC power charge×1 → GCground chalcopyrite×4 Electric Ball Mill 26s T4 135 kJ Tumbling steel balls grind jaw-crushed sulfide down to fine flotation feed. This is the correct fine-grinding stage -- cleaner and more uniform than grinding on the stamp mill, and it feeds the flotation cell directly. (electric ball mill) t4_ballmill_chalcopyrite GGground galena×4 RGraw galena ore×4 APAC power charge×1 → GGground galena×4 Electric Ball Mill 26s T4 130 kJ Grind lead ore to a fine, liberated powder so froth flotation can actually float the galena. Raw lump ore cannot be floated -- this grind is the step that unlocks the high-recovery flotation route. (electric ball mill) t4_ballmill_galena GHground hematite×4 CHcrushed hematite×4 APAC power charge×1 → GHground hematite×4 Electric Ball Mill 26s T4 135 kJ Tumble-grind crushed hematite to fine separation feed on AC power so a high-intensity separator can catch the weakly magnetic iron oxide. (ball mill) t4_ballmill_hematite GMground magnetite×4 CMcrushed magnetite×4 APAC power charge×1 → GMground magnetite×4 Electric Ball Mill 26s T4 135 kJ Tumble-grind crushed magnetite to fine separation feed on AC power, liberating the magnetic iron oxide for the magnetic separator that follows. (ball mill) t4_ballmill_magnetite GPground pentlandite×4 RPraw pentlandite ore×4 APAC power charge×1 → GPground pentlandite×4 Electric Ball Mill 28s T4 140 kJ Grind nickel sulfide ore to fine flotation feed. Pentlandite is tougher, so it takes a little longer and more power on the mill. (electric ball mill) t4_ballmill_pentlandite GSground sphalerite×4 RSraw sphalerite ore×4 APAC power charge×1 → GSground sphalerite×4 Electric Ball Mill 26s T4 130 kJ Grind zinc ore fine enough that the sphalerite liberates for flotation. The ball mill is what turns raw sphalerite rock into a floatable feed. (electric ball mill) t4_ballmill_sphalerite Electric Induction Furnace 3 BIbronze ingot×9 CIcopper ingot×8 TItin ingot×1 APAC power charge×1 → BIbronze ingot×9 Electric Induction Furnace 55s T4 80 kJ Alloy proper tin bronze -- about nine parts copper to one part tin -- in a controlled induction melt. This is the real bronze the crude copper-only crucible heat only approximates. (electric induction furnace) t4_induction_bronze BIbronze ingot×9 CIcopper ingot×8 RCraw cassiterite ore×2 APAC power charge×1 → BIbronze ingot×9 Electric Induction Furnace 95s T4 110 kJ Make tin bronze straight from copper and cassiterite ore in one heat, reducing the tin oxide and alloying it in a single induction melt -- handy when you have ore but no tin ingots on hand. (electric induction furnace) t4_induction_cosmelt_bronze TItin ingot×2 RCraw cassiterite ore×4 APAC power charge×1 → TItin ingot×2 Electric Induction Furnace 70s T4 90 kJ Smelt cassiterite to tin in a clean, hot induction melt. Far faster than the charcoal clay crucible and it recovers twice the tin, since the tighter heat means less metal lost to the slag. (electric induction furnace) t4_induction_tin Electric Jaw Crusher 6 CBcrushed bauxite×4 RBraw bauxite ore×4 APAC power charge×1 → CBcrushed bauxite×4 Electric Jaw Crusher 18s T4 90 kJ A fast, controlled crush that still leaves bauxite coarse enough for caustic digestion, without the dust losses of the stamp mill. (jaw crusher) t4_jawcrush_bauxite CCcrushed chalcopyrite×4 RCraw chalcopyrite ore×4 APAC power charge×1 → CCcrushed chalcopyrite×4 Electric Jaw Crusher 20s T4 100 kJ Quick primary crush of sulfide ore on motor power, sized for the ball mill and flotation cell that follow, with minimal dust loss. (single-toggle jaw crusher) t4_jawcrush_chalcopyrite CCcrushed chromite ore×4 RCraw chromite ore×4 APAC power charge×1 → CCcrushed chromite ore×4 Electric Jaw Crusher 22s T4 110 kJ Tough chromite yields to the eccentric-driven jaws far faster than hand stamping, and the clean gap keeps the chrome grains out of the dust pile. (primary jaw crushing) t4_jawcrush_chromite COcrushed oxide copper ore×4 OCoxidized copper ore×4 APAC power charge×1 → COcrushed oxide copper ore×4 Electric Jaw Crusher 18s T4 95 kJ A jaw crusher is the right tool for oxide heap ore -- it makes a coarse, permeable gravel the acid can percolate through, instead of the leach-choking fines a stamp battery throws. (coarse primary crushing) t4_jawcrush_copper_oxide CHcrushed hematite×4 RHraw hematite ore×4 APAC power charge×1 → CHcrushed hematite×4 Electric Jaw Crusher 18s T4 95 kJ The motor-driven jaws nip iron ore into a uniform coarse crush in one pass, keeping the fines a stamp battery would scatter as dust. Faster feed for the bloomery and blast furnace. (single-toggle jaw crusher) t4_jawcrush_hematite CMcrushed magnetite×4 RMraw magnetite ore×4 APAC power charge×1 → CMcrushed magnetite×4 Electric Jaw Crusher 20s T4 105 kJ Crushes hard magnetite cleanly so the grains free up for the magnetic separator, with almost no ore lost to dust. (primary jaw crushing) t4_jawcrush_magnetite Electrowinning Cell 1 ZIzinc ingot×2 SAsulfuric acid×1 ZSzinc sulfate liquor×2 DPDC power charge×2 → ZIzinc ingot×2 SAsulfuric acid×1 Electrowinning Cell 150s T4 380 kJ +1 byproduct Electrowin zinc onto aluminium cathodes; the cell regenerates sulfuric acid that is piped straight back to the leach, closing the acid loop. Strip, melt and cast to ingot. zn_electrowin Fire-Refining Furnace 2 CIcopper ingot×2 RSrefining slag×1 BCblister copper×2 CHcharcoal×1 → CIcopper ingot×2 RSrefining slag×1 Fire-Refining Furnace 150s T2 735 kJ +1 byproduct Anode-furnace fire refining: oxidize off S/Fe/Pb/Zn, then "pole" with green wood/charcoal to reduce Cu2O back to metal -- tough-pitch copper (~99%). Drops a copper-rich refining slag (recycled). cu_fire_refine LIlead ingot×2 SLsoftened lead×2 → LIlead ingot×2 Fire-Refining Furnace 90s T3 440 kJ Fire-refine and cast the softened lead into clean ingots -- corrosion-proof, dense, and ready for shielding, batteries, and chamber-acid plumbing. pb_refine_cast Forge Hearth 1 WIwrought iron billet×2 HShammer scale×2 BSbloomery slag×2 IBiron bloom×1 CHcharcoal×4 → WIwrought iron billet×2 HShammer scale×2 BSbloomery slag×2 Forge Hearth 240s T1 +2 byproducts Era 3 (~45min active, 3-5 reheats). Hammer the hot bloom at welding heat from the centre outward -- welds the iron crystals and squeezes slag to the surface, leaving a dense but unsized billet. ~10-15% comes off as hammer scale (iron oxide); squeezed slag joins the heap. A cold bloom shatters -- reheat between passes. Draw the billet to bar next. t1_forge_bloom_to_wrought Froth Flotation Cell 7 CCchalcopyrite concentrate×3 TAtailings×1 GCground chalcopyrite×4 → CCchalcopyrite concentrate×3 TAtailings×1 Froth Flotation Cell 120s T1 25 kJ +1 byproduct Condition with lime (depress pyrite, pH ~9-12), add a xanthate collector + pine-oil frother; copper sulfide floats off, silicate gangue sinks as tailings. 4 ground -> 3 concentrate. cu_float_chalcopyrite GCgalena concentrate×2 TAtailings×1 RGraw galena ore×3 → GCgalena concentrate×2 TAtailings×1 Froth Flotation Cell 60s T2 40 kJ +1 byproduct Froth-float crushed galena: the heavy lead sulfide rides the bubbles to a concentrate, the silica gangue sinks as tailings. pb_froth_flotation GCgalena concentrate×3 TAtailings×1 GGground galena×4 → GCgalena concentrate×3 TAtailings×1 Froth Flotation Cell 70s T4 45 kJ +1 byproduct Float finely ground galena into a high-grade lead concentrate. Proper grinding liberates more of the sulfide, so this recovers noticeably more concentrate per load than floating raw lump ore directly. (froth flotation) t4_float_galena_ground RErare earth concentrate×2 TAtailings×1 RRraw rare earth ore×3 → RErare earth concentrate×2 TAtailings×1 Froth Flotation Cell 70s T4 50 kJ +1 byproduct Froth-float the rare-earth ore to a mixed-lanthanide concentrate, rejecting the bulk gangue. ree_flotation SCsphalerite concentrate×3 TAtailings×1 GSground sphalerite×4 → SCsphalerite concentrate×3 TAtailings×1 Froth Flotation Cell 68s T4 45 kJ +1 byproduct Float ground sphalerite into a high-grade zinc concentrate. The fine grind from the ball mill is what lets the reagents separate the zinc sulfide from the gangue at high recovery. (froth flotation) t4_float_sphalerite_ground SCsphalerite concentrate×2 TAtailings×1 RSraw sphalerite ore×3 → SCsphalerite concentrate×2 TAtailings×1 Froth Flotation Cell 60s T2 40 kJ +1 byproduct Froth-float sphalerite to a zinc-sulfide concentrate, rejecting the silica gangue as tailings. zn_froth_flotation NCnickel concentrate×3 TAtailings×1 GPground pentlandite×4 → NCnickel concentrate×3 TAtailings×1 Froth Flotation Cell 72s T4 50 kJ +1 byproduct Float ground pentlandite into a high-grade nickel concentrate. Grinding ahead of flotation recovers more nickel than running raw ore straight into the cell. (froth flotation) t4_float_pentlandite_ground Gathered from the world 2 ORore rock×1 obtain=rock_outcrop • ★ FLOOR PICKUP ITEM ★ -- rock_outcrop world_ore TOtin ore rock×1 obtain=rock_outcrop • ★ FLOOR PICKUP ITEM ★ -- rock_outcrop world_ore_tin_rock__1 Iron Anvil 4 IHiron hammer head×1 WIwrought iron bar×2 → IHiron hammer head×1 Iron Anvil Station: iron anvil block itemdb_iron_hammer_head_iron_anvil_block GHgeology hammer×1 WIwrought iron bar×1 ROrope×1 FFfine forest twigs×1 → GHgeology hammer×1 Iron Anvil Station: iron anvil block itemdb_tool_geology_hammer_iron_anvil_block SWsimple wrench×1 WIwrought iron bar×2 WHwood haft×1 → SWsimple wrench×1 Iron Anvil Station: iron anvil block itemdb_wrench_simple_iron_anvil_block WIwrought iron bar×1 WIwrought iron billet×1 CHcharcoal×1 → WIwrought iron bar×1 Iron Anvil 120s T1 Era 3 (~20min). Reheat and hammer the billet with overlapping blows, rotating to draw it down to an even, measurable cross-section. Surface scale here is incidental -- no byproduct; collect pigment from the consolidation step instead. t1_draw_billet_to_bar Roasting Pit 2 RBroasted bog iron×4 RBraw bog iron×5 → RBroasted bog iron×4 Roasting Pit 90s T1 250 kJ Bog iron (limonite, FeO·OH) is roasted to dehydrate it to hematite/magnetite before bloomery reduction. Loses ~10% mass as water (2FeO·OH → Fe₂O₃ + H₂O). Low hardness means it roasts quickly. t1_roast_bog_iron RHroasted hematite×4 RHraw hematite ore×4 → RHroasted hematite×4 Roasting Pit 120s T1 335 kJ Unlike sulfide ores, hematite (Fe₂O₃) is already fully oxidized -- almost no mass is lost on roasting. The purpose is to drive off moisture and crack the ore structure for better reduction in the bloomery. Yield is effectively 100%. t1_roast_hematite Rocker Box 2 TCtin concentrate (black tin)×2 TAtailings×2 CCcrushed cassiterite×4 → TCtin concentrate (black tin)×2 TAtailings×2 Rocker Box 70s T1 +1 byproduct A hand-rocked cradle washes the crushed ore: the heavy cassiterite settles behind the riffles while the light gangue rinses away. Crude tin streaming -- slow, poor recovery, and a lot of tailings. t1_rocker_concentrate_cassiterite GCgalena concentrate×2 TAtailings×4 RGraw galena ore×6 → GCgalena concentrate×2 TAtailings×4 Rocker Box 150s T1 +1 byproduct Hand-rocked cradle washes crushed galena; the dense lead sulfide settles behind the riffles while light gangue washes out. Crude and slow, with poor recovery and a lot of tailings. t1_rocker_concentrate_galena Sluice Box 2 TCtin concentrate (black tin)×3 TAtailings×1 CCcrushed cassiterite×4 → TCtin concentrate (black tin)×3 TAtailings×1 Sluice Box 45s T1 +1 byproduct A continuous riffled sluice runs water over the crushed ore so the heavy tin drops out steadily. Better recovery and faster than the rocker box -- the workhorse of the old tin streams. t2_sluice_concentrate_cassiterite GCgalena concentrate×3 TAtailings×3 RGraw galena ore×6 → GCgalena concentrate×3 TAtailings×3 Sluice Box 90s T2 +1 byproduct A continuous riffled sluice runs water over the charge so the heavy galena drops out steadily. Better recovery and faster than the rocker box, but still loses the fines that only flotation can catch. t2_sluice_concentrate_galena Steam Rolling Mill 1 ASaluminum sheet×1 AIaluminum ingot×2 → ASaluminum sheet×1 Steam Rolling Mill chemName=Aluminum sheet,formula=Al • Station: rolling mill (steam) itemdb_metal_aluminum_sheet_rolling_mill_steam Stone Anvil 2 BSbronze shovel blade×1 BIbronze ingot×1 → BSbronze shovel blade×1 Stone Anvil 60s T1 Bronze is too hard to cold-hammer like copper, so it is melted and poured into a stone mould rested on the anvil. Once it sets, the cast blade is knocked out and the edges are trued up. The stone anvil doubles as the casting bench here. anvil_bronze_shovel_blade_cast HChammered copper nugget×1 RNraw native copper×3 → HChammered copper nugget×1 Stone Anvil formula=Cu,chemName=Copper,elements=Cu:1 • Station: stone anvil itemdb_copper_nugget_hammered_anvil_stone Stone Mortar 1 CIcrushed iron ore×4 RBroasted bog iron×4 → CIcrushed iron ore×4 Stone Mortar 60s T1 Era 3 (~30min hand work). Break roasted ore to walnut-fist size -- too large makes cold spots, too fine chokes airflow. Reject white silica veins. Crushing dust scatters; no byproduct worth a slot. t1_crush_roasted_bog_iron Tailings Re-Wash Box 1 GCgalena concentrate×1 TAtailings×8 → GCgalena concentrate×1 Tailings Re-Wash Box 200s T1 Re-washing settled tailings scavenges the little galena the first pass missed. Low yield and slow, but nothing goes to waste. t1_tailings_retreat_galena Trip Hammer 6 CCchalcopyrite concentrate×2 RCraw chalcopyrite ore×3 → CCchalcopyrite concentrate×2 Trip Hammer formula=CuFeS2,chemName=Chalcopyrite,elements=Cu:1|Fe:1|S:2 • Station: stamp mill (manual trip hammer) itemdb_concentrate_chalcopyrite_stamp_mill_manual_trip_hammer CCcrushed cassiterite×3 TAtailings×1 RCraw cassiterite ore×4 → CCcrushed cassiterite×3 TAtailings×1 Trip Hammer 60s T1 +1 byproduct Stamp the lump cassiterite to a coarse sand so the dense tin oxide can be washed loose from the lighter rock. About a quarter is lost as fines to tailings, the same as the other stamp-mill feeds. t1_crush_cassiterite CHcrushed hematite×3 TAtailings×1 RHraw hematite ore×4 → CHcrushed hematite×3 TAtailings×1 Trip Hammer 60s T1 +1 byproduct Stamp mill crushes lump hematite to a fine fraction suitable for blast furnace burden or wet separation. ~25% losses to tailings. t1_crush_hematite CMcrushed magnetite×3 TAtailings×1 RMraw magnetite ore×4 → CMcrushed magnetite×3 TAtailings×1 Trip Hammer 60s T1 +1 byproduct Crushed magnetite can be magnetically separated at higher tiers; for now it serves as a direct blast furnace feed. t1_crush_magnetite CScrushed slag×3 SLslag×4 → CScrushed slag×3 Trip Hammer 60s T2 Bloomery and furnace slag retains trapped iron particles. Crushing and re-feeding recovers a useful fraction -- important when ore is scarce. t1_crush_slag LFlimestone flux×2 BLburned lime (quicklime)×1 RLraw limestone ore×3 → LFlimestone flux×2 BLburned lime (quicklime)×1 Trip Hammer 60s T1 +1 byproduct Crushed limestone (CaCO₃) is the flux for blast furnaces. Fines that pass through the screen partially calcine during crushing heat, yielding a quicklime byproduct (CaO). t1_crush_limestone_flux Wet High-Intensity Magnetic Separator (WHIMS) 2 IOiron ore concentrate×3 TAtailings×1 GMground magnetite×4 → IOiron ore concentrate×3 TAtailings×1 Wet High-Intensity Magnetic Separator (WHIMS) 60s T4 55 kJ +1 byproduct Pull a high-grade iron concentrate from finely ground magnetite in a strong electromagnetic field. Grinding ahead of separation recovers far more iron than running crushed ore through the basic drum LIMS. (wet high-intensity magnetic separation) t4_whims_magnetite IOiron ore concentrate×3 TAtailings×1 GHground hematite×4 → IOiron ore concentrate×3 TAtailings×1 Wet High-Intensity Magnetic Separator (WHIMS) 66s T4 60 kJ +1 byproduct Recover iron from finely ground hematite that the basic drum LIMS cannot catch -- the high-intensity field grabs the weakly magnetic oxide, no coke reduction roast needed. (wet high-intensity magnetic separation) t4_whims_hematite Workbench 4 FHforge hearth×1 EBearthenware brick×6 RCraw clay×3 CTclay tuyere×1 BEbellows×1 → FHforge hearth×1 Workbench transport=chute • Station: workbench itemdb_forge_hearth_workbench IAiron anvil block×1 WIwrought iron bar×4 → IAiron anvil block×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_iron_anvil_block_workbench SIstone ingot mold×1 RRrounded river stone×4 → SIstone ingot mold×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_stone_mold_ingot_workbench IHiron hammer×1 IHiron hammer head×1 ROrope×1 FFfine forest twigs×2 → IHiron hammer×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_tool_iron_hammer_workbench Zinc Distillation Retort 1 ZIzinc ingot×2 CDcarbon dioxide gas×2 ZOzinc oxide calcine×2 CFcoke fuel×2 → ZIzinc ingot×2 CDcarbon dioxide gas×2 Zinc Distillation Retort 180s T3 1010 kJ +1 byproduct The old way: reduce the calcine with coke and distil the zinc off as vapour (zinc boils at 907 C), condensing it to metal. Energy-hungry but needs no electricity -- the historic and Imperial-Smelting route. zn_retort_thermal

docs.title: Tier 3–4 — Steam, Coke & Steel
docs.text:
94 recipes

Bessemer Converter 1 Blast Furnace 1 Clay Casting Pit 1 Clay Crucible 2 Earthen Kiln 8 Electric Arc Furnace 3 Engine Lathe 4 Fire-Refining Furnace 1 Gathered from the world 1 Glass Bench 3 Iron Anvil 18 Lever Press 1 Open Hearth Furnace 1 Riveted Boiler 1 Steam Machine Shop 11 Steam Rolling Mill 5 Trip Hammer 2 Wire Drawing Bench 3 Workbench 27 Bessemer Converter 1 SIsteel ingot×3 SLslag×1 PIpig iron ingot×4 → SIsteel ingot×3 SLslag×1 Bessemer Converter 300s T2 +1 byproduct The Bessemer blow forces cold air through liquid pig iron from tuyeres at the converter base. Oxygen in the air burns out carbon, silicon, and manganese in a violent 15-20 minute exothermic reaction -- the heat released is so intense that no external fuel is needed after ignition. Carbon drops from ~4% to ~0.2-0.5%, producing steel. The blow must be stopped at the right carbon level; over-blowing produces wrought iron, under-blowing leaves cast iron. t2_bessemer_steel Blast Furnace 1 PIpig iron ingot×4 SLslag×3 CHcrushed hematite×6 CFcoke fuel×3 LFlimestone flux×2 → PIpig iron ingot×4 SLslag×3 Blast Furnace 480s T2 +1 byproduct The blast furnace drives a continuous reduction column: coke burns to CO at tuyere level (~1600 degC), CO reduces Fe2O3 to iron as the burden descends, and limestone flux (CaO after calcination) reacts with gangue silica to form liquid slag (CaSiO3) that floats above liquid pig iron. Pig iron absorbs ~4% C from coke -- it is hard but brittle. The slag must be tapped separately from the iron notch. t2_blast_furnace_pig_iron Clay Casting Pit 1 CIcast-iron firebox×1 PIpig iron ingot×1 → CIcast-iron firebox×1 Clay Casting Pit Station: clay casting pit itemdb_firebox_cast_iron_clay_casting_pit Clay Crucible 2 BTbronze tool blank×1 BIbronze ingot×1 BIbronze ingot×1 → BTbronze tool blank×1 Clay Crucible tier=2;material=bronze • Station: clay crucible (small) itemdb_bronze_tool_blank_clay_crucible_small RCrefined copper ingot×1 CCchalcopyrite concentrate×1 CHcharcoal×1 → RCrefined copper ingot×1 Clay Crucible Station: clay crucible (small) • Byproduct: slag itemdb_copper_ingot_refined_clay_crucible_small Earthen Kiln 8 CFcarbon filament preform×1 PFplant fibers×1 PIpitch×1 CHcharcoal×1 → CFcarbon filament preform×1 Earthen Kiln Station: earthen kiln (updraft) itemdb_carbon_filament_preform_earthen_kiln_updraft CCclay casting pit×1 EBearthenware brick×1 LSloose sand×1 RCraw clay×1 → CCclay casting pit×1 Earthen Kiln Station: earthen kiln (updraft) itemdb_clay_casting_pit_earthen_kiln_updraft SCsmall clay crucible×1 RCraw clay×1 G(grog (crushed ceramic temper)×1 → SCsmall clay crucible×1 Earthen Kiln Station: earthen kiln (updraft) itemdb_clay_crucible_small_earthen_kiln_updraft F(fireclay (refractory)×1 RCraw clay×1 T(temper (sand or grog)×1 → F(fireclay (refractory)×1 Earthen Kiln Station: earthen kiln (updraft) itemdb_fireclay_refractory_earthen_kiln_updraft PLporcelain lamp socket×1 F(fireclay (refractory)×1 ICinsulated copper wire×1 → PLporcelain lamp socket×1 Earthen Kiln Station: earthen kiln (updraft) itemdb_lamp_socket_porcelain_earthen_kiln_updraft PPporcelain pin insulator×1 F(fireclay (refractory)×1 → PPporcelain pin insulator×1 Earthen Kiln Station: earthen kiln (updraft) itemdb_porcelain_insulator_pin_earthen_kiln_updraft FRfireclay refractory brick×1 F(fireclay (refractory)×1 → FRfireclay refractory brick×1 Earthen Kiln Station: earthen kiln (updraft) itemdb_refractory_brick_fireclay_earthen_kiln_updraft SRsilica refractory brick×1 LSloose sand×1 F(fireclay (refractory)×1 → SRsilica refractory brick×1 Earthen Kiln Station: earthen kiln (updraft) itemdb_refractory_brick_silica_earthen_kiln_updraft Electric Arc Furnace 3 RCrefined copper ingot×2 CCcopper cathode×2 CHcharcoal×1 → RCrefined copper ingot×2 Electric Arc Furnace 80s T4 Melt the pure cathode sheets under a charcoal blanket that keeps air off the metal, then cast oxygen-free refined ingot. Keeping oxygen out matters: gassy copper turns brittle and conducts worse, so the charcoal cover is not optional for electrical-grade stock. copper_cathode_remelt_refined RCrefined copper ingot×1 CFcopper foil×3 CHcharcoal×1 → RCrefined copper ingot×1 Electric Arc Furnace 50s T4 Foil left over from stamping and cladding is thin and oxidizes fast, so it is bundled and melted back under charcoal into clean ingot rather than thrown away. Copper is worth too much to scrap. copper_foil_scrap_remelt RCrefined copper ingot×1 CDcopper dust×3 CHcharcoal×1 → RCrefined copper ingot×1 Electric Arc Furnace 50s T4 Machining swarf and grinding dust are swept up, pressed into a briquette, and melted back under charcoal into ingot. Loose copper powder burns and scatters, so compacting it first keeps the metal out of the slag. copper_dust_swarf_remelt Engine Lathe 4 TBturned bronze bearing×2 BIbronze ingot×1 SCsteam charge×1 → TBturned bronze bearing×2 Engine Lathe 70s T3 Bore and turn the sleeve to a precise sliding fit in one setup. Quick, repeatable, and clean enough that one ingot yields two bearings instead of half of it ending up as scrapings. lathe_bronze_bearing_machined DCdrawn copper wire×2 CRcopper rod×1 SCsteam charge×1 → DCdrawn copper wire×2 Engine Lathe 35s T3 Pull the round rod through a series of shrinking holes in a draw plate until it thins into wire. Each pass squeezes it smaller, so one rod gives a long run of wire. This is the standard way bar stock becomes the wire that windings and contacts need. copper_rod_draw_wire SLsteel lead screw×1 SIsteel ingot×1 SCsteam charge×1 → SLsteel lead screw×1 Engine Lathe 90s T3 The lathe's own lead screw and change gears drive the tool along at a fixed rate, cutting a perfectly even thread by itself. This is the whole reason the screw-cutting lathe was such a leap: it turns the hardest hand job into a routine one. lathe_lead_screw_machined TSturned steel shaft×2 SIsteel ingot×1 SCsteam charge×1 → TSturned steel shaft×2 Engine Lathe 60s T3 Clamp the bar between the lathe centres and let the tool peel it down to an exact, smooth diameter in one setup. Far faster than hand-filing and it wastes almost no metal, so one ingot gives two shafts. This is what machined means: cheaper and quicker than the same part made by hand. lathe_steel_shaft_machined Fire-Refining Furnace 1 RCrefined copper ingot×2 CCcopper cathode×2 → RCrefined copper ingot×2 Fire-Refining Furnace 120s T4 590 kJ Remelt cathode under charcoal cover and cast oxygen-free refined ingot for wire and electronics. cu_cathode_remelt Gathered from the world 1 BCbituminous coal×1 energyAmount=8000,energyUnit=kJ,formula=C,chemName=Bituminous Coal,obtain=coal_seam_node • ★ FLOOR PICKUP ITEM ★ -- coal_seam_node world_coal_bituminous Glass Bench 3 BGborosilicate glass tube×4 RSraw silica ore×2 BOborax×1 CHcharcoal×2 → BGborosilicate glass tube×4 Glass Bench 180s T3 Silica is fluxed with borax and melted in the lampworking burner flame, then drawn by hand into tube stock. The resulting borosilicate composition tolerates rapid thermal cycling without cracking. t3_glass_tube ILincandescent lamp (carbon)×2 BGborosilicate glass tube×1 CFcarbon filament preform×1 ICinsulated copper wire×1 → ILincandescent lamp (carbon)×2 Glass Bench 90s T3 Glass tube is collapsed over the filament mount, the air evacuated, and the stem sealed with a gas flame. Using pre-drawn tube stock is faster than blowing a bulb from scratch and gives consistent wall thickness. t3_glass_lamp_bulb ILincandescent lamp (carbon)×1 ICinsulated copper wire×1 CFcarbon filament preform×1 → ILincandescent lamp (carbon)×1 Glass Bench placeModel=incandescent_lamp_carbon • Station: glass bench itemdb_incandescent_lamp_carbon_glass_bench Iron Anvil 18 CIcast-iron belt pulley×1 PIpig iron ingot×1 CWconstruction wood×1 → CIcast-iron belt pulley×1 Iron Anvil placeModel=belt_pulley_iron,station=true,transport=shaft • Station: iron anvil block itemdb_belt_pulley_iron_iron_anvil_block TBturned bronze bearing×1 BIbronze ingot×2 → TBturned bronze bearing×1 Iron Anvil 150s T3 Cast a rough bronze sleeve, then hand-scrape the bore little by little until a shaft just slides through. A skilled hand can get a good fit, but it is slow and wastes metal as scrapings. anvil_bronze_bearing_manual WIwrought-iron connecting rod×1 WIwrought iron bar×1 PBplain bearing (wood)×1 → WIwrought-iron connecting rod×1 Iron Anvil Station: iron anvil block itemdb_connecting_rod_wrought_iron_anvil_block FIforged iron crank×1 WIwrought iron bar×1 → FIforged iron crank×1 Iron Anvil WIwrought-iron rod×1 Iron Anvil Station: iron anvil block itemdb_iron_rod_wrought_iron_anvil_block ISiron strap×1 WIwrought iron bar×1 → ISiron strap×1 Iron Anvil Station: iron anvil block
