ingot×1 → WSweighted safety valve×1 Iron Anvil Station: iron anvil block itemdb_safety_valve_weighted_iron_anvil_block SCsteam cylinder (cast iron)×1 PIpig iron ingot×1 → SCsteam cylinder (cast iron)×1 Iron Anvil Station: iron anvil block itemdb_steam_cylinder_cast_iron_iron_anvil_block SDsteam drill×1 WIwrought iron bar×2 IHiron hammer head×1 WIwrought-iron steam pipe×1 ROrope×2 → SDsteam drill×1 Iron Anvil placeModel=steam_drill,station=true,station_input=1,station_output=2,station_fuel=0,transport=chute • Station: iron anvil block itemdb_steam_drill_iron_anvil_block SPsteam piston and rod×1 WIwrought iron bar×1 → SPsteam piston and rod×1 Iron Anvil Station: iron anvil block itemdb_steam_piston_rod_iron_anvil_block SLsteel lead screw×1 SIsteel ingot×2 → SLsteel lead screw×1 Iron Anvil 240s T3 Before screw-cutting lathes, a long even thread was filed and chased into a bar by hand against a guide. It can be done, but it is painstaking and the thread is never quite uniform. Expect to spoil some steel getting one good screw. anvil_lead_screw_manual SRsteel rivet×1 SIsteel ingot×1 → SRsteel rivet×1 Iron Anvil Station: iron anvil block itemdb_steel_rivet_iron_anvil_block TSturned steel shaft×1 SIsteel ingot×2 → TSturned steel shaft×1 Iron Anvil 180s T3 You can make a shaft without a lathe: forge the bar round on the anvil, then file and grind it true by hand. It works, but it is slow and a lot of good steel ends up as filings on the floor -- which is why it takes two ingots to get one decent shaft this way. anvil_steel_shaft_manual Lever Press 1 LFleather flat belt×1 RAraw animal hide×1 G(grease (tallow)×1 → LFleather flat belt×1 Lever Press Station: lever press itemdb_flat_belt_leather_lever_press Open Hearth Furnace 1 SIsteel ingot×5 BFblast-furnace slag×2 PIpig iron ingot×6 BCbituminous coal×3 → SIsteel ingot×5 BFblast-furnace slag×2 Open Hearth Furnace 600s T3 5460 kJ +1 byproduct Regenerative open-hearth process: incoming cold gas heats through a brick checker-work that was itself heated by the last exhaust stroke. The reversal cycle maintains furnace temperature while using far less fuel than a non-regenerative flame. Operator controls carbon content precisely -- Bessemer is faster but open-hearth produces more consistent structural steel grades. t3_open_hearth_steel Riveted Boiler 1 SCsteam charge×1 RWraw water×1 CHcharcoal×1 → SCsteam charge×1 Riveted Boiler 20s T4 Boil the water until pressure builds, then tap one puff of steam into a holding flask. Water goes in the input slot, charcoal in the fuel slot. e5_boil_steam_charge Steam Machine Shop 11 LAlaminated armature core×1 RSrolled steel plate×1 TSturned steel shaft×1 → LAlaminated armature core×1 Steam Machine Shop Station: machine shop (steam) itemdb_armature_core_laminated_machine_shop_steam SCsegmented copper commutator×1 RCrefined copper ingot×1 TRtree resin×1 CWconstruction wood×1 → SCsegmented copper commutator×1 Steam Machine Shop Station: machine shop (steam) itemdb_commutator_copper_segmented_machine_shop_steam DDDC dynamo (workshop)×1 TSturned steel shaft×1 DCdrawn copper wire×1 WIwrought-iron magnet core×1 → DDDC dynamo (workshop)×1 Steam Machine Shop Station: machine shop (steam) itemdb_dynamo_dc_workshop_machine_shop_steam SDsmall DC motor×1 TSturned steel shaft×1 ICinsulated copper wire×1 WIwrought-iron magnet core×1 SNsintered NdFeB magnet×1 → SDsmall DC motor×1 Steam Machine Shop Station: machine shop (steam) itemdb_electric_motor_small_dc_machine_shop_steam GBglass bench×1 RSrolled steel plate×1 FRfireclay refractory brick×1 RSrolled steel beam×1 → GBglass bench×1 Steam Machine Shop placeModel=glass_bench • Station: machine shop (steam) itemdb_glass_bench_machine_shop_steam ELengine lathe (workshop)×1 RSrolled steel plate×1 TSturned steel shaft×1 PBplain bearing (wood)×1 → ELengine lathe (workshop)×1 Steam Machine Shop Station: machine shop (steam) itemdb_lathe_workshop_machine_shop_steam MSmachine shop (electric)×1 MSmachine shop (steam)×1 SDsmall DC motor×1 ICinsulated copper wire×1 → MSmachine shop (electric)×1 Steam Machine Shop placeModel=machine_shop_electric • Station: machine shop (steam) itemdb_machine_shop_electric_machine_shop_steam RMrolling mill (steam)×1 RSrolled steel beam×1 TSturned steel shaft×1 SBsimple beam engine×1 → RMrolling mill (steam)×1 Steam Machine Shop Station: machine shop (steam) itemdb_rolling_mill_steam_machine_shop_steam SDsteam drill×1 SCsteam cylinder (cast iron)×1 HIhammered iron plate×3 HFhand-forged iron rivet×16 → SDsteam drill×1 Steam Machine Shop 150s T1 Mount a cast-iron steam cylinder on a riveted iron frame so its piston hammers a drill rod against the rock face. Bolt it together at a steam machine shop; once placed it feeds on the boiler's steam to chew into ore that SteamDrillService tracks. This is the build route the drill was missing -- before this it could only be placed by service, never legitimately obtained. steam_drill_assemble TSturned steel shaft×1 SIsteel ingot×1 → TSturned steel shaft×1 Steam Machine Shop Station: machine shop (steam) itemdb_steel_shaft_machine_shop_steam WDwire-drawing bench×1 RSrolled steel plate×1 TSturned steel shaft×1 CWconstruction wood×1 → WDwire-drawing bench×1 Steam Machine Shop Station: machine shop (steam) itemdb_wire_drawing_bench_machine_shop_steam Steam Rolling Mill 5 RSrolled steel angle×1 SIsteel ingot×1 → RSrolled steel angle×1 Steam Rolling Mill Station: rolling mill (steam) itemdb_steel_angle_bar_rolling_mill_steam RSrolled steel beam×2 SIsteel ingot×3 SCsteam charge×1 → RSrolled steel beam×2 Steam Rolling Mill 120s T3 Universal H-beam section rolled from steel billets. Structurally superior to wrought-iron box girders for the same mass -- flanges carry bending load, web carries shear. t3_roll_steel_beam SGsteel gusset plate×1 RSrolled steel plate×1 → SGsteel gusset plate×1 Steam Rolling Mill Station: rolling mill (steam) itemdb_steel_gusset_plate_rolling_mill_steam RSrolled steel plate×2 SIsteel ingot×2 SCsteam charge×1 → RSrolled steel plate×2 Steam Rolling Mill 90s T3 Steel ingots are passed between hardened rolls under full steam pressure. The rolling action reduces cross-section, work-hardens the surface, and aligns the grain for better tensile properties. t3_roll_steel_plate SRsteel rail×2 PIpig iron ingot×4 SCsteam charge×1 → SRsteel rail×2 Steam Rolling Mill 120s T3 Hot pig iron rolled through a profiled rail die. The I-section profile distributes wheel load across the foot and head; air-cooling after rolling relieves thermal stress. t3_roll_steel_rail Trip Hammer 2 CScrushed slag×4 TAtailings×2 SLslag×6 → CScrushed slag×4 TAtailings×2 Trip Hammer 90s T2 +1 byproduct Blast furnace slag contains 0.5-1.5% trapped iron particles and glassy silicate phases. Crushing liberates the iron granules for magnetic or gravity separation in later tiers. The silicate fines become tailings; the iron-rich fraction can be re-fed to the blast furnace at small loss. t2_slag_reprocess LFlimestone flux×2 RLraw limestone ore×3 → LFlimestone flux×2 Trip Hammer formula=CaCO3,chemName=Limestone,elements=Ca:1|C:1|O:3 • Station: stamp mill (manual trip hammer) • Byproduct: burned_lime_quicklime itemdb_limestone_flux_stamp_mill_manual_trip_hammer Wire Drawing Bench 3 DCdrawn copper wire×3 RCrefined copper ingot×1 → DCdrawn copper wire×3 Wire Drawing Bench 90s T4 45 kJ Pull annealed copper rod through a series of progressively smaller dies to draw it down into wire. Each pass work-hardens the metal, so it is annealed between draws to keep it ductile. t3_draw_copper_wire DCdrawn copper wire×3 RCrefined copper ingot×1 → DCdrawn copper wire×3 Wire Drawing Bench 60s T3 Refined copper rod is pulled through progressively smaller draw-plate holes by a hand capstan. Each pass reduces cross-section by 20-30% and work-hardens the wire; anneal between stages to restore ductility. t3_draw_copper_wire__1 DCdrawn copper wire×1 RCrefined copper ingot×1 → DCdrawn copper wire×1 Wire Drawing Bench Station: wire-drawing bench itemdb_copper_wire_drawn_wire_drawing_bench Workbench 27 BFbeam frame×1 WIwrought iron bar×1 ISiron strap×1 CWconstruction wood×1 → BFbeam frame×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_beam_frame_wrought_workbench BCBessemer converter×1 FRfireclay refractory brick×1 PIpig iron ingot×1 → BCBessemer converter×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_bessemer_converter_basic_workbench SBsmall blast furnace×1 FRfireclay refractory brick×1 FIfieldstone×1 CFcoke fuel×1 LNlimestone nodule×1 → SBsmall blast furnace×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_blast_furnace_small_workbench BLboiler lagging×1 PFplant fibers×1 CWconstruction wood×1 TAtar×1 → BLboiler lagging×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_boiler_lagging_mat_workbench RBriveted boiler shell×1 HIhammered iron plate×4 HFhand-forged iron rivet×16 → RBriveted boiler shell×1 Workbench 90s T1 Curve four hammered wrought-iron plates over a form and lap their edges, then drive hot rivets through the overlaps and hammer them flat. As each rivet cools it shrinks and clamps the seam, giving a shell that holds low-pressure steam. This is the plate-by-plate way a riveted boiler is actually built. boiler_shell_rivet_construct RBriveted boiler shell×1 WIwrought iron bar×1 ISiron strap×1 → RBriveted boiler shell×1 Workbench placeModel=boiler_shell_riveted,station=true,station_input=1,station_fuel=1,station_output=2,transport=chute • Station: workbench itemdb_boiler_shell_riveted_workbench CBcarbon brush block×1 CFcoke fuel×1 PIpitch×1 → CBcarbon brush block×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_brush_block_carbon_workbench CBcopper bus bar×1 RCrefined copper ingot×1 → CBcopper bus bar×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_bus_bar_copper_workbench COcoke oven (beehive)×1 EBearthenware brick×1 FIfieldstone×1 BCbituminous coal×1 → COcoke oven (beehive)×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_coke_oven_beehive_workbench JCjet condenser×1 WIwrought-iron steam pipe×1 WTwooden tub×1 → JCjet condenser×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_condenser_jet_workbench WCwooden cooling tank×1 CWconstruction wood×1 WPwooden peg×1 → WCwooden cooling tank×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_cooling_tank_wooden_workbench ICinsulated copper wire×1 DCdrawn copper wire×1 TRtree resin×1 SCsail cloth×1 → ICinsulated copper wire×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_copper_wire_insulated_workbench SFsimple feedwater pump×1 WIwrought iron bar×1 WIwrought-iron steam pipe×1 → SFsimple feedwater pump×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_feedwater_pump_simple_workbench RSriveted steel frame×1 RSrolled steel beam×2 RSrolled steel angle×4 SRsteel rivet×24 → RSriveted steel frame×1 Workbench 110s T3 Stand two rolled beams as columns, brace them with steel angle, and join every connection with hot driven rivets. The finished bent carries engine-house roofs, mill floors, and the bearings for overhead line shafts. Riveted steel framing like this held up the first multi-story factories. frame_steel_rivet_assemble RFrewireable fuse×1 PPporcelain pin insulator×1 DCdrawn copper wire×1 → RFrewireable fuse×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_fuse_rewireable_workbench SGsteam gland packing×1 HFhemp fiber×1 G(grease (tallow)×1 → SGsteam gland packing×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_gland_packing_steam_workbench HBhot-blast stove×1 SBsmall blast furnace×1 FRfireclay refractory brick×1 → HBhot-blast stove×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_hot_blast_stove_workbench HKheavy knife switch×1 RSrolled steel plate×1 RCrefined copper ingot×1 → HKheavy knife switch×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_knife_switch_heavy_workbench ILindustrial lamp fixture×1 RSrolled steel plate×1 ILincandescent lamp (carbon)×1 PLporcelain lamp socket×1 → ILindustrial lamp fixture×1 Workbench placeModel=lamp_fixture_industrial • Station: workbench itemdb_lamp_fixture_industrial_workbench WIwrought-iron line shaft×1 WIwrought iron bar×1 PBplain bearing (wood)×1 → WIwrought-iron line shaft×1 Workbench placeModel=line_shaft_wrought,station=true,transport=shaft • Station: workbench itemdb_line_shaft_wrought_workbench MSmachine shop (steam)×1 RSrolled steel beam×1 WIwrought-iron line shaft×1 IAiron anvil block×1 → MSmachine shop (steam)×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_machine_shop_steam_workbench OHopen-hearth furnace×1 SRsilica refractory brick×1 BFblast-furnace slag×1 PIpig iron ingot×1 → OHopen-hearth furnace×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_open_hearth_furnace_basic_workbench SBsimple beam engine×1 SCsteam cylinder (cast iron)×1 SPsteam piston and rod×1 FLflywheel×1 BFbeam frame×1 WIwrought-iron connecting rod×1 SGsteam gland packing×1 WSweighted safety valve×1 → SBsimple beam engine×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_steam_engine_simple_workbench WIwrought-iron docs.title: Tier 1 — Stone & Early Materials
docs.text:
88 recipes

Camp Marker 37 Carving Bench 3 Charcoal Retort Kiln 1 Earthen Kiln 4 Gathered from the world 1 Hand Crafting 2 Survival Campfire 2 Workbench 38 Camp Marker 37 ADadze×1 SAstone adze head×1 WHwood haft×1 ROrope×1 → ADadze×1 Camp Marker 240s T0 Seat the bit transverse on a knee-bend haft and lash it tight; the edge faces the worker. e1_assemble_adze B(basket (coiled)×1 CBcurled bark strip×6 RCrawhide cord×2 → B(basket (coiled)×1 Camp Marker 35s T0 Coil long bark strips into a spiral and bind each turn to the last with cord. The result is a light carry-basket for forage, stone, and clay -- it hauls loose material your hands cannot. The first real upgrade to your carrying capacity. basket_coiled_build_manual RBrough board plank×2 SDsmall dry sticks×1 SLstripped log×1 SFstone flake×0 → RBrough board plank×2 SDsmall dry sticks×1 Camp Marker 200s T0 +1 byproduct Score and split a stripped log lengthwise into rough flat planks. Slow without a metal saw, but the planks unlock shovel scoops and barrel-making. e1_saw_planks_from_stripped BDbow drill×1 SDsmall dry sticks×1 ROrope×1 WSwooden shaft (round)×1 → BDbow drill×1 Camp Marker 150s T0 Loop the cord once around a straight spindle so sawing the bow spins it -- drills holes and starts fire. e1_build_bow_drill BSbow saw frame×1 CWconstruction wood×1 ROrope×1 → BSbow saw frame×1 Camp Marker 180s T0 Bend a green stave into a bow and string it tight; the cutting blade is fitted later when metal exists. e1_build_bow_saw_frame CRcharcoal retort kiln×1 EBearthenware brick×6 RCraw clay×4 FIfieldstone×4 → CRcharcoal retort kiln×1 Camp Marker 600s T0 Build a sealed brick chamber with a cooling pipe (so the gas turns back into liquid); the wood gas flows through it instead of escaping up a vent. e1_build_charcoal_retort_kiln CHchisel×1 SFstone flakes×1 SDsmall dry sticks×1 ROrope×1 → CHchisel×1 Camp Marker 180s T0 Set a narrow flake end-on in a split stick and bind it so a mallet blow drives straight through. e1_haft_chisel WCwooden chute×1 RBrough board plank×2 ROrope×1 → WCwooden chute×1 Camp Marker placeModel=chute_wood • Station: camp_marker_basic itemdb_chute_wood_camp_marker_basic RCraw clay×1 LDloose dirt×4 WFwater-filled bark bucket×1 PFplant fibers×1 → RCraw clay×1 Camp Marker 30s T0 Stir dirt into water in the bucket, let it settle, then strain the heavy clay out through plant fibers. Wet, dirty, and not much per batch -- but it works anywhere you have dirt and water. e1_wash_dirt_for_clay DRdrawknife×1 SFstone flakes×1 SDsmall dry sticks×2 ROrope×1 → DRdrawknife×1 Camp Marker 200s T0 Lash a long flake between two short grips so it can be pulled toward you to shave bark and round stock. e1_haft_drawknife FPfire pit×1 RRrounded river stone×8 SDsmall dry sticks×4 → FPfire pit×1 Camp Marker 45s T0 Ring eight river stones around a scraped hollow and lay kindling in the middle. The stones hold the heat and stop the fire from wandering, turning a one-off campfire into a fixed hearth you can cook over and harden clay against. No tools beyond your hands -- just gather and stack. fire_pit_build_manual FSflax seed×2 TSturnip seed×2 CScarrot seed×2 DFdry forest seed mix×3 → FSflax seed×2 TSturnip seed×2 CScarrot seed×2 Camp Marker 120s T0 +2 byproducts Winnow and pick the dry mix apart into seed you can actually sow on purpose. e1_sort_seed_mix GPgold pan×1 CWconstruction wood×1 SFstone flakes×1 → GPgold pan×1 Camp Marker 240s T0 Hollow a shallow batea from a wood round; the gentle dish lets heavy fines settle while you swirl off the light sand. e1_carve_gold_pan G(grog (crushed ceramic temper)×2 EBearthenware brick×1 SPstone pestle×0 → G(grog (crushed ceramic temper)×2 Camp Marker 180s T0 Pound a spare fired brick into coarse grit; mixed back into raw clay, it stops the cracks that form when a pot shrinks as it dries. e1_crush_grog HFhemp fiber×1 PFplant fibers×3 → HFhemp fiber×1 Camp Marker 360s T0 Soak the stalks until the woody core rots away, then beat and comb out the long stringy fibers -- the thread used for cloth and packing. e1_ret_hemp_fiber HOhoe×1 SHstone hoe blade×1 WHwood haft×1 ROrope×1 → HOhoe×1 Camp Marker 240s T0 Lash the blade across the haft at a chopping-down angle for turning soil. e1_assemble_hoe L(lever (pry bar)×1 CWconstruction wood×1 SFstone flakes×1 → L(lever (pry bar)×1 Camp Marker 150s T0 Strip a stout pole and flatten one end into a prying tip; the longer the pole the more it lifts. e1_carve_lever_pry_bar MAmallet×1 CWconstruction wood×1 WHwood haft×1 ROrope×1 → MAmallet×1 Camp Marker 240s T0 Bore a dense billet, drive the haft through, and bind the cheeks so it cannot split out. e1_assemble_mallet MCmeasuring cord×1 ROrope×1 SDsmall dry sticks×1 → MCmeasuring cord×1 Camp Marker 90s T0 Tie even knots along a stretched rope as repeatable length marks; wind it on a stick to carry. e1_knot_measuring_cord PBplain bearing (wood)×1 CWconstruction wood×1 G(grease (tallow)×1 → PBplain bearing (wood)×1 Camp Marker 200s T0 Drill a hardwood block to fit the shaft and pack it with grease (tallow); it stays cool enough for slow mill work. e1_fit_plain_bearing_wood PFplant fibers×3 FSflax stalks×2 → PFplant fibers×3 Camp Marker 300s T0 Soak the pulled stalks until the core rots free, then beat out the long bast. e1_ret_flax PBplumb bob×1 FIfieldstone×1 SFstone flakes×1 PFplant fibers×1 → PBplumb bob×1 Camp Marker 120s T0 Peck a groove round a small stone, tie on a line, and let it hang -- it finds true vertical every time. e1_shape_plumb_bob SCsail cloth×1 HFhemp fiber×4 → SCsail cloth×1 Camp Marker 480s T0 Weave the fibers tight on a loom; the close, even cloth blocks wind without tearing. e1_weave_sail_cloth CScrude shovel×1 RBrough board plank×1 WHwood haft×1 ROrope×1 → CScrude shovel×1 Camp Marker 300s T0 Dish a rough board into a scoop, split the haft to grip its neck, and lash it fast. e1_assemble_shovel_wood SDspindle drill×1 WSwooden shaft (round)×1 FIfieldstone×1 PFplant fibers×2 → SDspindle drill×1 Camp Marker 180s T0 Weight a spindle with a holed stone whorl so a flick of the cords keeps it turning through the bore. e1_build_spindle_drill SAstone adze head×1 SFstone flakes×2 FIfieldstone×2 HAhammerstone×0 → SAstone adze head×1 SFstone flakes×2 Camp Marker 540s T0 +1 byproduct Knap a core to a gouging lip set across the long axis, then grind it keen for transverse cuts. e1_knap_stone_adze_head GSground stone axe×1 SAstone axe head×1 WHwood haft×1 ROrope×1 → GSground stone axe×1 Camp Marker 240s T0 Seat the head in a split or notch in the haft and lash it tight; wet rope shrinks as it dries. e1_assemble_stone_axe SAstone axe head×1 SFstone flakes×3 RRrounded river stone×2 HAhammerstone×0 → SAstone axe head×1 SFstone flakes×3 Camp Marker 600s T0 +1 byproduct Hard-hammer percussion to rough the wedge, then grind the edge on coarse stone. e1_knap_stone_axe_head SHstone hoe blade×1 SFstone flakes×2 FIfieldstone×2 HAhammerstone×0 → SHstone hoe blade×1 SFstone flakes×2 Camp Marker 540s T0 +1 byproduct Flake a flat, broad blade and grind one long edge; it lashes broad-side to the haft. e1_knap_stone_hoe_blade SLstripped log×1 BSbark slabs×2 CBcurled bark strip×2 MDmedium dry log×1 SFstone flake×0 → SLstripped log×1 BSbark slabs×2 CBcurled bark strip×2 Camp Marker 180s T0 +2 byproducts Score the bark, peel it off in slabs, and curl the inner bark into strips -- the log is now clean and ready to use. e1_strip_log T(temper (sand or grog)×1 LSloose sand×2 → T(temper (sand or grog)×1 Camp Marker 90s T0 Sift river sand to an even grit -- use this when you have no spare fired clay to crush into grog. e1_screen_temper_sand WAwattle-and-daub wall×1 SDsmall dry sticks×8 RCraw clay×4 RWraw water×2 → WAwattle-and-daub wall×1 Camp Marker 55s T0 Weave a lattice of green sticks (the wattle), then pack it with a mix of wet clay and water (the daub) and let it dry hard. It is the oldest real wall there is: cheap, windproof, and strong enough to carry a thatch roof. Slow by hand because every panel has to be woven and packed. wall_wattle_and_daub_build_manual WEwedge×1 CWconstruction wood×1 SFstone flakes×1 → WEwedge×1 Camp Marker 120s T0 Whittle a hardwood billet to a clean taper; season it hard so it bites instead of crushing. e1_carve_wedge WHwood haft×1 SDsmall dry sticks×1 SFstone flakes×1 → WHwood haft×1 Camp Marker 300s T0 Strip the bark and whittle the stick straight and smooth with a fresh flake edge. e1_carve_wood_haft WHwood haft×3 SDsmall dry sticks×2 SLstripped log×1 SFstone flakes×1 → WHwood haft×3 SDsmall dry sticks×2 Camp Marker 240s T0 +1 byproduct Split the stripped log into handles (called hafts) and trim the leftover bits into kindling sticks. e1_carve_haft_from_stripped WPwooden peg×4 CWconstruction wood×1 SFstone flakes×1 → WPwooden peg×4 Camp Marker 150s T0 Split a small log and whittle a handful of tapered wooden pegs; hammered in dry, they swell tight in the hole and lock. e1_carve_wooden_peg WTwooden tub×1 RBrough board plank×2 ROrope×1 → WTwooden tub×1 Camp Marker 300s T0 Stand the split wood strips (called staves) in a ring and bind them with a tight rope hoop; the swelling wood seals the gaps. e1_build_wooden_tub Carving Bench 3 RBrough board plank×1 CWconstruction wood×1 → RBrough board plank×1 Carving Bench Station: carving bench itemdb_board_plank_rough_carving_bench WSwooden shaft (round)×1 CWconstruction wood×1 → WSwooden shaft (round)×1 Carving Bench Station: carving bench itemdb_shaft_wood_round_carving_bench RTrough timber beam×1 CWconstruction wood×1 → RTrough timber beam×1 Carving Bench Station: carving bench itemdb_timber_beam_rough_carving_bench Charcoal Retort Kiln 1 CHcharcoal×5 TAtar×1 CWconstruction wood×8 → CHcharcoal×5 TAtar×1 Charcoal Retort Kiln 720s T0 +1 byproduct Bake a sealed batch of wood so it turns to charcoal without burning up; the cooling pipe catches the wood tar that an open clamp would let escape. e1_char_retort Earthen Kiln 4 CTclay tuyere×1 RCraw clay×6 LSloose sand×2 RWraw water×1 CHcharcoal×2 → CTclay tuyere×1 Earthen Kiln 50s T1 A fired-clay nozzle that carries the bellows' blast into a furnace without burning away. Sand tempers the clay so it survives the thermal shock of repeated firings. You make this now so the bloomery has a throat when the Metal Ages arrive. clay_tuyere_fire_manual CTclay tuyere×1 RCraw clay×1 → CTclay tuyere×1 Earthen Kiln Station: earthen kiln (updraft) itemdb_clay_tuyere_earthen_kiln_updraft PIpitch×1 TRtree resin×2 → PIpitch×1 Earthen Kiln Station: earthen kiln (updraft) itemdb_pitch_earthen_kiln_updraft GSglazed stoneware jar×1 RCraw clay×2 CPcalcined potters’ flint×1 BSburnt shell lime×1 → GSglazed stoneware jar×1 Earthen Kiln Station: earthen_kiln_updraft itemdb_stoneware_glazed_jar_earthen_kiln_updraft Gathered from the world 1 COcobblestone×1 obtain=rock_outcrop • ★ FLOOR PICKUP ITEM ★ -- rock_outcrop world_cobblestone Hand Crafting 2 HShafted stone knife×1 SFstone flake×1 CWconstruction wood×1 RCrawhide cord×1 → HShafted stone knife×1 Hand Crafting Station: hand itemdb_tool_stone_knife_hafted_hand_crafting_2x2 WLwater level×1 CBcurled bark strip×1 ROrope×1 SDsmall dry sticks×1 → WLwater level×1 Hand Crafting Station: hand itemdb_water_level_hand_crafting_2x2 Survival Campfire 2 G(grease (tallow)×1 RAraw animal fat×2 → G(grease (tallow)×1 Survival Campfire 240s T0 Melt the trimmed fat slowly over low heat, skim the clean fat (called tallow) off the crispy leftover bits, and let it cool until hard. e1_render_grease_tallow PIpitch×1 TAtar×2 → PIpitch×1 Survival Campfire 240s T0 Boil the tar down until the thin, watery parts cook off and it sets stiff -- hard pitch that seals seams instead of staying sticky. e1_reduce_pitch Workbench 38 BEbellows×1 CWconstruction wood×1 RAraw animal hide×1 ROrope×1 → BEbellows×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_bellows_workbench BAblock-and-tackle×1 SHsheave×3 RBrough board plank×2 ROrope×4 ISiron strap×2 → BAblock-and-tackle×1 Workbench 60s T1 Two blocks of sheaves reeved with a single rope. Each fall of rope between the blocks divides the load again, so four falls let one person hold what four could not. The price is rope and travel: you haul four times the length to raise the load once. block_and_tackle_build_manual CAcapstan×1 RTrough timber beam×2 WSwooden shaft (round)×1 WPwooden power pole×2 ISiron strap×2 PBplain bearing (wood)×1 → CAcapstan×1 Workbench 70s T1 A vertical drum turned by push-bars walked in a circle. Several people throw their weight against the bars at once, so a gang can drag loads -- beached boats, raw timber, foundation stone -- that no rope-pull ever could. Iron straps keep the drum from splitting under the strain. capstan_build_manual CBcarving bench×1 RBrough board plank×3 RTrough timber beam×2 WPwooden peg×4 ROrope×1 → CBcarving bench×1 Workbench placeModel=workbench • Station: workbench itemdb_carving_bench_workbench CScoarse screen×1 RTrough timber beam×2 RBrough board plank×2 WIwrought iron bar×2 → CScoarse screen×1 Workbench 55s T1 An iron-barred grizzly screen in a heavy timber frame. Tip crushed ore or gravel across it and the fines fall through while the oversize rolls off to be crushed again -- the sorting step that keeps the later mills from choking on rock that is too big. coarse_screen_build_manual COcounterweight×1 RBrough board plank×3 RRrounded river stone×12 ROrope×2 → COcounterweight×1 Workbench 45s T1 A stone-filled crate that lifts for you: let the weight fall and it raises the load on the other end of the rope. Deliberately heavy on stone -- the whole point is the mass. Pair it with a gin pole or a treadle and gravity does the lifting. counterweight_build_manual FSfire-starter bundle×1 TItinder×1 DEdeadwood×2 ACalder catkin cone×1 → FSfire-starter bundle×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_fire_starter_bundle_workbench FLflywheel×1 CWconstruction wood×1 FIfieldstone×1 ROrope×1 → FLflywheel×1 Workbench placeModel=flywheel,station=true,transport=shaft • Station: workbench itemdb_flywheel_workbench GGgeared gristmill×1 CWconstruction wood×8 FIfieldstone×4 WAwheel-and-axle×2 → GGgeared gristmill×1 Workbench station=true,station_input=2,station_output=2 • Station: workbench itemdb_gristmill_geared_workbench HOholdfast×1 RTrough timber beam×1 WPwooden peg×3 RRrounded river stone×2 → HOholdfast×1 Workbench 35s T1 A wedge-and-peg arm that drops through a hole in the bench and pins your work flat with a tap, freeing both hands. Entirely wood and stone -- no iron needed yet -- but it is the difference between fighting a workpiece and working it. holdfast_build_manual LPlever press×1 CWconstruction wood×1 FIfieldstone×1 ROrope×1 → LPlever press×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_lever_press_workbench LWlime wash×1 BSburnt shell lime×2 RWraw water×1 → LWlime wash×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_lime_wash_workbench PMpost mill (windmill)×1 CWconstruction wood×10 ROrope×4 WAwheel-and-axle×2 T(tailpole (windmill yaw)×1 → PMpost mill (windmill)×1 Workbench Optional late-T1 • Station: workbench itemdb_post_mill_windmill_workbench PWpotter's wheel (kick)×1 RTrough timber beam×2 WSwooden shaft (round)×1 RRrounded river stone×6 PBplain bearing (wood)×1 WPwooden peg×4 → PWpotter's wheel (kick)×1 Workbench 80s T1 A heavy stone disk keyed to a vertical shaft on a wooden bearing. You kick the flywheel to keep it spinning and shape the clay on the head up top -- the momentum of the stone does the work your free hand cannot. Building it is the gate to throwing pots instead of coiling them. potters_wheel_kick_build_manual PWpotter's wheel (kick)×1 CWconstruction wood×1 FIfieldstone×1 → PWpotter's wheel (kick)×1 Workbench late-t0,quality-of-life • Station: workbench itemdb_potters_wheel_kick_workbench PUpulley×1 SHsheave×1 WDwooden dowel×1 ISiron strap×1 → PUpulley×1 Workbench 35s T1 A grooved sheave turning on a dowel pin in an iron-strapped block. On its own it just changes the direction of a rope -- but that is enough to pull down on a load instead of lifting up on it, and it is the building block every heavier rig is made from. pulley_build_manual PUpulley×1 SHsheave×1 CWconstruction wood×1 ROrope×1 → PUpulley×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_pulley_workbench Q(quern (rotary)×1 FIfieldstone×4 CWconstruction wood×2 → Q(quern (rotary)×1 Workbench station=true,station_input=2,station_output=1 • Station: workbench itemdb_quern_rotary_workbench RIriffle insert (sluice)×1 CWconstruction wood×2 → RIriffle insert (sluice)×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_riffle_insert_sluice_workbench RBrocker box×1 CWconstruction wood×8 FIfieldstone×2 → RBrocker box×1 Workbench station=true,station_input=1,station_output=2 • Station: workbench • Byproduct: tailings itemdb_rocker_box_workbench RWrope-walk jig×1 RTrough timber beam×3 WGwooden gear (peg-toothed)×2 ISiron strap×2 ROrope×1 → RWrope-walk jig×1 Workbench 70s T1 A long geared frame that twists many yarns into heavy rope in one pass, hook turning against hook down the length of the walk. It is the leap from hand-laid cordage to real line -- the rope a capstan or a gin pole actually needs. rope_walk_jig_build_manual SJscrew jack (wooden)×1 RTrough timber beam×1 WDwooden dowel×2 FIforged iron crank×1 ISiron strap×1 → SJscrew jack (wooden)×1 Workbench 60s T1 A hand-cut hardwood screw running in a timber nut. It lifts slowly but holds its load without slipping and without anyone holding the crank -- exactly what you want when truing up a beam or raising a sill a hair at a time. screw_jack_wooden_build_manual SHsheave×1 CWconstruction wood×1 FIfieldstone×1 → SHsheave×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_sheave_workbench SIsieve×1 RBrough board plank×2 PFplant fibers×6 RCrawhide cord×2 → SIsieve×1 Workbench 40s T1 A woven fiber mesh stretched in a wooden hoop. Shake flour, sand, or wet clay through it and it sorts the fine from the coarse -- the first tool that grades material by size instead of by eye. Tempering clay through a sieve is what stops fired pots from cracking. sieve_build_manual SBsluice box×1 CWconstruction wood×10 FIfieldstone×4 RIriffle insert (sluice)×1 → SBsluice box×1 Workbench station=true,station_input=1,station_output=2 • Station: workbench • Byproduct: tailings itemdb_sluice_box_workbench STsluice trough×1 RBrough board plank×5 WPwooden peg×4 ROrope×2 → STsluice trough×1 Workbench 55s T1 An inclined plank channel with riffle bars across the floor. Wash crushed material down it and the current carries the light waste away while the heavy fraction drops and lodges behind the riffles. It lets running water do the sorting your hands used to. sluice_trough_build_manual SMstamp mill (manual trip hammer)×1 CWconstruction wood×1 FIfieldstone×1 ROrope×1 → SMstamp mill (manual trip hammer)×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_stamp_mill_manual_trip_hammer_workbench TBtailings box×1 CWconstruction wood×6 → TBtailings box×1 Workbench station=true,station_input=1,station_output=1 • Station: workbench itemdb_tailings_box_workbench T(tailpole (windmill yaw)×1 CWconstruction wood×2 → T(tailpole (windmill yaw)×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_tailpole_windmill_yaw_workbench TRtreadle×1 RBrough board plank×2 WSwooden shaft (round)×1 WPwooden peg×3 ROrope×2 → TRtreadle×1 Workbench 45s T1 A foot pedal linked by a pitman to a crank. It turns the weight of your leg into rotary power and frees both hands -- the drive behind pole-lathes, looms, and grindstones. The first power source that is not somebody else's arms. treadle_build_manual WAwattle-and-daub wall×1 HRhazel rod×6 PEpacked earth block×4 GBgrass bundle×2 RWraw water×1 → WAwattle-and-daub wall×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_wall_wattle_and_daub_workbench W(waterwheel (overshot)×1 CWconstruction wood×1 ROrope×1 FIfieldstone×1 → W(waterwheel (overshot)×1 Workbench Optional late-T1 • Station: workbench itemdb_waterwheel_overshot_workbench W(waterwheel (undershot)×1 CWconstruction wood×6 ROrope×3 FIfieldstone×2 → W(waterwheel (undershot)×1 Workbench Optional late-T1 • Station: workbench itemdb_waterwheel_undershot_workbench WAwheel-and-axle×1 WGwooden gear (peg-toothed)×1 WSwooden shaft (round)×1 PBplain bearing (wood)×2 ISiron strap×1 → WAwheel-and-axle×1 Workbench 50s T1 A large wheel keyed to a thin axle on wooden bearings. A light pull on the rim becomes a strong turn at the core -- the same trick as a windlass, but continuous. It is the heart of every geared machine you will build after this. wheel_and_axle_build_manual WIwindlass×1 WSwooden shaft (round)×1 RTrough timber beam×2 FIforged iron crank×1 PBplain bearing (wood)×2 ROrope×2 → WIwindlass×1 Workbench 65s T1 A horizontal drum wound by a forged iron crank turning in wooden bearings. The crank's leverage turns a weak, steady arm into a controlled lift -- the standard way to raise water, ore, or a bucket from a shaft. windlass_build_manual WDwooden dowel×1 CWconstruction wood×1 → WDwooden dowel×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_wooden_dowel_workbench WGwooden gear (peg-toothed)×1 WDwooden dowel×1 CWconstruction wood×1 → WGwooden gear (peg-toothed)×1 Workbench Station: workbench itemdb_wooden_gear_peg_toothed_workbench WTwooden tub×1 RBrough board plank×6 ROrope×3 WPwooden peg×4 → WTwooden tub×1 Workbench 55s T1 Fit split planks into a ring of staves and bind them with rope hoops until the joints swell tight. It holds water, mash, dye, or hide-soak -- the big open vessel a camp industry runs on. Slow to build because every stave has to be shaped to close the circle. wooden_tub_build_manual

This makes two unfired (greenware) pots, ready to be hardened in a kiln. t0_form_clay_pot_large RCragged cloak×1 RAraw animal hide×1 RCrawhide cord×1 PFplant fibers×1 → RCragged cloak×1 Camp Marker 35s T0 210 kJ Trim a hide and punch holes so it can be tied as a rough weather cloak. t0_sew_cloak_ragged DSdebris shelter frame×1 BBbranch bundle×3 PFplant fibers×2 → DSdebris shelter frame×1 Camp Marker 45s T0 220 kJ Lean and lash branch bundles into a simple A-frame that can accept debris cover. t0_build_debris_shelter_frame DLdebris lean-to shelter×1 DSdebris shelter frame×1 BObundle of thatch×4 → DLdebris lean-to shelter×1 Camp Marker 60s T0 260 kJ Layer bundles of reeds and grass over the frame to shed rain and hold warmth. t0_build_debris_shelter_lean_to DRdrying rack (survival)×1 CWconstruction wood×2 BBbranch bundle×1 PFplant fibers×3 → DRdrying rack (survival)×1 Camp Marker 30s T0 190 kJ Lash uprights and crossbars into a rack that can hold meat, hides and cordage. t0_build_drying_rack_survival EUearthen updraft kiln×1 RCraw clay×10 LDloose dirt×8 RRrounded river stone×6 BBbranch bundle×2 → EUearthen updraft kiln×1 Camp Marker 120s T0 320 kJ Build a simple updraft kiln from clay, earth, and stone. t0_build_earthen_kiln_updraft SFstone fire ring×1 RRrounded river stone×6 → SFstone fire ring×1 Camp Marker 10s T0 140 kJ Arrange smooth cobbles into a ring that keeps coals and sparks contained. t0_build_fire_ring BFbasic fishing rod×1 SDsmall dry sticks×2 PFplant fibers×3 SFstone flake×1 → BFbasic fishing rod×1 Camp Marker 20s T0 180 kJ Notch two dry sticks together for the rod, lash plant-fiber line along the length, and fix a sharpened stone flake as the hook. Roughly 20 catches before it wears out. t0_make_fishing_rod_basic BFbasic fishing rod×1 SDsmall dry sticks×2 PFplant fibers×3 SFstone flake×1 → BFbasic fishing rod×1 Camp Marker engine_use=fish • Station: camp_marker_basic itemdb_fishing_rod_basic_camp_marker_basic WFwicker fish trap×1 WWwillow wands×4 PFplant fibers×2 → WFwicker fish trap×1 Camp Marker 40s T0 210 kJ Weave flexible rods into a funnelled trap that fish can swim into but not out. t0_build_fishing_trap_weir RBraw bear meat×5 TRthick raw hide×1 LRlarge raw bone×2 RAraw animal fat×2 BBblack bear carcass×1 SFstone flake×1 → RBraw bear meat×5 TRthick raw hide×1 LRlarge raw bone×2 RAraw animal fat×2 Camp Marker 90s T0 400 kJ +3 byproducts Enormous effort. Bear yields exceptional fat, rich dark meat, and a thick hide. t0_field_dress_bear RBraw beef×4 RCraw cow hide×1 LRlarge raw bone×1 RAraw animal fat×2 CCcow carcass×1 SFstone flake×1 → RBraw beef×4 RCraw cow hide×1 LRlarge raw bone×1 RAraw animal fat×2 Camp Marker 60s T0 300 kJ +3 byproducts Rough field dressing of a cow carcass. Use a Butchering Block for 3× the yield. t0_field_dress_cow RFraw fox meat×1 FPfox pelt×1 SRsmall raw bone×1 RFred fox carcass×1 SFstone flake×1 → RFraw fox meat×1 FPfox pelt×1 SRsmall raw bone×1 Camp Marker 20s T0 160 kJ +2 byproducts Skin the fox carefully to preserve the quality pelt, then gut for lean meat. t0_field_dress_fox RMraw mutton×2 LRlight raw hide×1 SRsmall raw bone×1 RAraw animal fat×1 SCsheep carcass×1 SFstone flake×1 → RMraw mutton×2 LRlight raw hide×1 SRsmall raw bone×1 RAraw animal fat×1 Camp Marker 45s T0 240 kJ +3 byproducts Field dress a sheep for mutton, light hide, and a small bone. t0_field_dress_sheep RPraw pork×3 LRlight raw hide×1 LRlarge raw bone×1 RAraw animal fat×2 TUtusk×1 WBwild boar carcass×1 SFstone flake×1 → RPraw pork×3 LRlight raw hide×1 LRlarge raw bone×1 RAraw animal fat×2 TUtusk×1 Camp Marker 50s T0 270 kJ +4 byproducts Field dress a wild boar. Quick work for a small but useful yield. t0_field_dress_rabbit RRraw raccoon meat×1 LRlight raw hide×1 SRsmall raw bone×1 RAraw animal fat×1 RCraccoon carcass×1 SFstone flake×1 → RRraw raccoon meat×1 LRlight raw hide×1 SRsmall raw bone×1 RAraw animal fat×1 Camp Marker 20s T0 150 kJ +3 byproducts Field dress a raccoon for dark meat, a light hide, and some fat. t0_field_dress_raccoon RSraw squirrel meat×1 SRsmall raw bone×1 SCsquirrel carcass×1 SFstone flake×1 → RSraw squirrel meat×1 SRsmall raw bone×1 Camp Marker 10s T0 90 kJ +1 byproduct A tiny carcass. Yields a small bite of meat and little else. t0_field_dress_squirrel RVraw venison×3 RDraw deer hide×1 LRlarge raw bone×1 RAraw animal fat×1 DCdeer carcass×1 SFstone flake×1 → RVraw venison×3 RDraw deer hide×1 LRlarge raw bone×1 RAraw animal fat×1 Camp Marker 45s T0 250 kJ +3 byproducts Field dress a deer for venison, supple hide, and a structural bone. t0_field_dress_deer RWraw wolf meat×2 WPwolf pelt×1 LRlarge raw bone×1 RAraw animal fat×1 WCwolf carcass×1 SFstone flake×1 → RWraw wolf meat×2 WPwolf pelt×1 LRlarge raw bone×1 RAraw animal fat×1 Camp Marker 35s T0 220 kJ +3 byproducts A wolf is hard work to field dress. Pelt, meat, and a solid structural bone. t0_field_dress_wolf SMstone mortar×1 FIfieldstone×1 H(hammerstone (hafted)×1 → SMstone mortar×1 Camp Marker 60s T0 240 kJ Peck a depression into a stone block to make a mortar for grinding grain or pigments. t0_peck_mortar_stone SPstone pestle×1 RRrounded river stone×1 → SPstone pestle×1 Camp Marker 30s T0 200 kJ Pick and trim a stone that fits the hand for pounding and grinding in the mortar. t0_shape_pestle_stone PSpit storage cache×1 LDloose dirt×4 WSwattle screen×1 RRrounded river stone×2 → PSpit storage cache×1 Camp Marker 70s T0 280 kJ Dig a deeper cache and line/cover it with wattle for longer-term storage. t0_build_pit_storage_cache SQsaddle quern×1 FIfieldstone×2 → SQsaddle quern×1 Camp Marker 80s T0 260 kJ Grind and smooth a stone into a saddle-shaped quern for more efficient milling. t0_shape_quern_saddle ROrope×1 DSdried sinew bundle×2 → ROrope×1 Camp Marker 30s T0 190 kJ Split, soften and reverse-twist dried leg sinew into a tough length of sinew rope. t0_twist_rope_from_sinew ROrope×1 PFplant fibers×3 → ROrope×1 Camp Marker 25s T0 190 kJ Strip, reverse-twist and braid plant fibres into a single length of rope. t0_twist_rope_from_plant_fibers BSbone shovel×1 LRlarge raw bone×1 SDsmall dry sticks×1 ROrope×1 → BSbone shovel×1 Camp Marker 25s T0 170 kJ Split a large bone flat and lash it to a stick as a crude scoop. t0_craft_shovel_bone SSsimple snare×1 RCrawhide cord×1 SDsmall dry sticks×2 → SSsimple snare×1 Camp Marker 18s T0 150 kJ Shape a loop of rawhide cord and stake it between small sticks as a trail snare. t0_set_snare_simple DKdry kindling sticks×2 SDsmall dry sticks×1 → DKdry kindling sticks×2 Camp Marker 5s T0 110 kJ Break or shave dry sticks into smaller, fast-lighting kindling pieces. t0_split_kindling GSground stone axe×1 FIfieldstone×1 SFstone flake×1 BBbranch bundle×1 PFplant fibers×2 → GSground stone axe×1 Camp Marker 45s T0 220 kJ Shape and grind a stone blank, then lash it to a branch handle for a proper camp axe. t0_grind_stone_axe WCwooden chest×1 CWconstruction wood×12 ROrope×4 → WCwooden chest×1 Camp Marker 90s T0 380 kJ Peg and lash split wood into a proper chest with a lid; weatherproof storage (27 slots). t0_build_storage_chest_wood BSbark storage crate×1 CBcurled bark strip×8 BBbranch bundle×2 ROrope×2 → BSbark storage crate×1 Camp Marker 50s T0 230 kJ Lash bark panels between sapling uprights; the simplest camp storage box (27 slots). t0_build_storage_crate_bark FSfirewood storage rack×1 CWconstruction wood×3 BBbranch bundle×1 PFplant fibers×3 → FSfirewood storage rack×1 Camp Marker 35s T0 200 kJ Prop poles into a rack and lash them so split logs can dry off the ground. t0_build_storage_firewood_rack FSfood storage pit×1 LDloose dirt×4 RRrounded river stone×4 CScrude shovel×1 → FSfood storage pit×1 Camp Marker 60s T0 260 kJ Excavate a shallow pit and line it with stone to keep food cool and off wet soil. t0_dig_storage_food_pit BObundle of thatch×1 CRcut reeds×3 PFplant fibers×1 → BObundle of thatch×1 Camp Marker 15s T0 160 kJ Align reed stems and tie them into a tight thatch bundle. t0_bundle_thatch_reeds BObundle of thatch×1 MMmoss mat×2 PFplant fibers×1 → BObundle of thatch×1 Camp Marker 18s T0 170 kJ Pack moss mats and lash them together -- softer than reed thatch, slower to dry but warmer. t0_bundle_thatch_moss BObundle of thatch×1 PLpacked leaf mat×2 PFplant fibers×1 → BObundle of thatch×1 Camp Marker 16s T0 165 kJ Layer packed leaf mats and tie them into a soft, slightly leaky thatch bundle. Use when reeds and moss are scarce. t0_bundle_thatch_leaves DFdry fiber tinder×2 PFplant fibers×1 → DFdry fiber tinder×2 Camp Marker 6s T0 120 kJ Tease and twist plant fibres into fluffy tinder bundles that catch fire easily. t0_bundle_tinder_fibers H(hammerstone (hafted)×1 HAhammerstone×1 PFplant fibers×1 → H(hammerstone (hafted)×1 Camp Marker 8s T0 120 kJ Wrap plant fibres around the grip of a raw hammerstone to seat it comfortably in the hand. t0_haft_hammerstone_to_tool SFstone flake×3 RRrounded river stone×1 H(hammerstone (hafted)×1 → SFstone flake×3 Camp Marker 12s T0 180 kJ Strike a rounded cobble with a hammerstone to knock off several sharp flakes. t0_knap_stone_flakes HShafted stone knife×1 SFstone flake×1 SDsmall dry sticks×1 PFplant fibers×2 → HShafted stone knife×1 Camp Marker 18s T0 190 kJ Notch a stick and lash a sharp flake into place with twisted plant fibres. t0_haft_stone_knife SPstone pick (lashed)×1 FIfieldstone×1 SDsmall dry sticks×1 ROrope×1 → SPstone pick (lashed)×1 Camp Marker 35s T0 210 kJ Peck a groove around a stone head and lash it tightly to a stick handle with rope. t0_craft_stone_pick_basic CWcamp waste midden×1 LDloose dirt×2 BBbranch bundle×1 → CWcamp waste midden×1 Camp Marker 20s T0 160 kJ Scrape a shallow hollow and ring it with branches as the camp's waste dump. t0_mark_waste_midden_basic RWrawhide waterskin×1 RAraw animal hide×2 RCrawhide cord×3 → RWrawhide waterskin×1 Camp Marker 40s T0 Fold a scraped hide into a bag and stitch the seams tight with rawhide cord, leaving a neck you can plug. It carries water away from the river so you are not pinned to the bank. Crude and a little leaky at first, but it is the difference between a day-trip and an expedition. waterskin_rawhide_build_manual RWrawhide waterskin×1 RAraw animal hide×1 RCrawhide cord×2 → RWrawhide waterskin×1 Camp Marker 40s T0 210 kJ Fold and stitch a softened hide into a crude waterskin, laced with rawhide cord. t0_sew_waterskin_rawhide WSwattle screen×1 WWwillow wands×3 PFplant fibers×2 → WSwattle screen×1 Camp Marker 30s T0 200 kJ Weave flexible willow rods and lash them into a panel that walls pits or breaks wind. t0_weave_wattle_screen WOworkbench×1 SDsmall dry log×2 MDmedium dry log×1 PFplant fibers×4 GSground stone axe×1 → WOworkbench×1 Camp Marker 70s T0 300 kJ Flatten logs with a ground axe and lash them into a sturdy raised work surface. t0_build_workbench Charcoal Clamp 1 CHcharcoal×40 WAwood ash×4 SDsmall dry log×8 MDmedium dry log×3 LDloose dirt×4 → CHcharcoal×40 WAwood ash×4 Charcoal Clamp 120s T0 320 kJ +1 byproduct Era 2 (~5-day real burn). Let the covered wood smoulder slowly with almost no air so it loses its water and gases and turns into nearly pure-carbon charcoal (about 33% of the wood's weight survives). docs.title: Plain-English glossary
docs.text:
Conduvia's crafting tree is really a tour of how people actually make things. This page explains the real trick behind each big jump on the climb — in plain words, in one place. We keep the real names (they're worth knowing!), but every one comes with a "here's what that actually means" right next to it. Where we simplify something for the game, we say so.

Honest by design

We speed up the timing and round off the amounts so it's fun to play. The way each thing works is the real way.

docs.title: Heat & carbon
docs.text:
Charcoal. Heat wood in a pile with almost no air. Instead of burning up, the wood cooks: the smoke and steam get driven off, leaving behind nearly pure carbon. Charcoal burns much hotter and cleaner than plain wood — hot enough to melt metal. (The "cook it with no air" trick has a name: pyrolysis.)

Coke. The same trick, but on coal instead of wood. Bake coal with no air and the tar and gas cook off, leaving a light, super-hot fuel called coke. It burns hot enough — and stays solid enough — to hold up a tall furnace packed full of ore.

Quicklime. Roast limestone (the white rock, also called calcium carbonate) and it puffs out a gas (carbon dioxide), leaving quicklime behind. Quicklime helps metal melting go smoothly, and it's the starting point for mortar — the paste that holds bricks together.

docs.title: Metals
docs.text:
Bronze. Melt copper, then stir in a little tin. The mix is harder than either metal alone and melts at a lower heat — so handy that a whole age is named after it. (Mixing metals like this makes an alloy.)

Wrought iron. A short stone furnace called a bloomery pulls iron out of ore without fully melting it. You're left with a hot, spongy lump of iron mixed with glassy waste, which you hammer over and over to squeeze the waste out. The result is soft, tough iron you can shape — wrought just means "worked."

Steel. A tall blast furnace melts iron together with coke and a bit of rock helper into a high-carbon iron. Carbon is what makes iron hard but also brittle — so you carefully burn some of it back out. Land in the sweet spot and you get steel: the strong, springy metal the modern world is built from.

docs.title: Power
docs.text:
Steam power. Burn fuel to boil water. The steam pushes hard as it expands, and that push shoves a piston or spins a wheel. Suddenly you have a worker that never gets tired — the engine that kicked off the factory age.

Generators (dynamos) and the grid. Spin a coil of wire near a magnet and you make electricity — that's a generator. A simple one (a DC dynamo) gets you started. A fancier kind (AC) can send power over long wires without wasting much, and run big motors. That's the moment electricity stops being a toy and starts running real factories.

docs.title: Electricity does chemistry (the Electric tier)
docs.text:
Aluminum. Aluminum clings to oxygen so tightly that fire can't pull them apart. Electricity can. You dissolve aluminum's powdery ore in a hot melted bath and push a huge current through it; pure aluminum collects at the bottom. People call this the Hall–Héroult process (named after its two inventors). Aluminum is basically electricity turned into metal — impossible before the power grid.

Chlor-alkali. Run electricity through salty water and it splits into three useful things at once: chlorine gas, hydrogen gas, and a strong cleaner/base called lye (sodium hydroxide). Tons of other chemistry starts from these three.

Ferroalloys and raw silicon. In a furnace heated by an electric arc (think lightning on tap), you roast quartz rock with carbon. The carbon steals the oxygen out of the rock and leaves behind about 98%-pure silicon — the seed of everything electronic. Doing it with carbon and heat like this is called carbothermic reduction (carbo = carbon, thermic = heat, reduction = pulling the oxygen off).

docs.title: Computer chips (the Solid State tier)
docs.text:
Super-pure silicon. That 98% silicon isn't nearly clean enough for chips. So you turn it into a gas, boil that gas over and over to scrub out the junk (like distilling), then turn it back into solid silicon that's something like a billion times purer. This clean-up is the Siemens process, and the result is polysilicon.

Growing a crystal wafer. Dip a tiny seed crystal into a pot of melted silicon and slowly pull it upward while spinning. The silicon freezes onto the seed in one flawless crystal, growing into a long shiny rod. Slice the rod into thin rounds — wafers — and you've got the blank discs every computer chip is printed on. This pulling trick is the Czochralski method.

docs.title: Space (the win)
docs.text:
Cold-trapping oxygen from air. Squeeze and chill air until it turns to liquid, then warm it back up bit by bit. Different gases boil off at different temperatures, so they separate — letting you collect pure liquid oxygen. That super-cold liquid oxygen is what your rocket breathes to burn its fuel. (Anything that cold is called cryogenic.)

Titanium. Titanium is light, super strong, and shrugs off heat — perfect for rockets — but it's a pain to make. You react its ore-gas with melted magnesium, which rips the metal free as a crumbly "sponge" you then melt down. This is the Kroll process.

Heat-proof metal mixes. A rocket engine has to survive its own fire. So you build it from special metal blends: tough nickel ones (like Inconel) for the pumps, and a copper-chromium mix for the chamber walls that get cooled from behind. Bolt on lightweight fuel tanks, fill them with chilled oxygen and rocket kerosene, stack it into a rocket — and launch.

docs.title: How this site is built
docs.text:
This manual is mostly generated from Conduvia’s own source data, then hand-tuned. The generator gives it a structure that tracks the game; the hand-tuning adds the extra detail (chemistry formulae, prose, diagrams) that the raw data doesn’t carry.

The committed data/*.json can lag the live game

The published site reflects the current world (832 items · 685 recipes · 153 stations). The JSON snapshot checked into data/ can be older than that, and the pages here include hand-added detail (e.g. the Formula column on item tables) that gen_pages.py does not emit. Treat the generator as a scaffolder, not the literal source of every page — re-running it against a stale snapshot would revert content, not refresh it. Always re-extract from the live game code first (see Regenerating below).

docs.title: Pipeline
docs.text:
Game Lua sources
   QuestBook/Content/Chapters/*.luau   (quests)
   ItemDatabase/Data/*.luau            (items)
   Stations/StationDefs.luau           (stations)
        |
        |  tools/lualit.py   (tolerant Lua-literal reader, no Lua runtime)
        |  tools/extract.py  ->  data/{quests,items,stations}.json
        v
   tools/gen_pages.py  ->  docs/  (the climb, field guides, stations, items, home)
        |
        v
   mkdocs build  ->  site/
  • tools/lualit.py parses Lua table literals directly in Python — strings, long-brackets, comments, computed keys, trailing commas, type casts, and string concatenation — so no Lua interpreter is needed to read the data.
  • tools/extract.py walks the chapters, item database, and station defs and emits clean JSON.
  • tools/gen_pages.py turns that JSON into the markdown you’re reading, plus the icon manifest.json.

docs.title: What’s hand-written
docs.text: The onboarding (Getting Started) and these Reference pages are written by hand — they’re explanation, not data. The pages under The Climb, Field Guides, Stations, and Items & Ores are scaffolded by the generator and then hand-augmented — the field-guide chemistry, the item-table Formula column, and the narrative notes are added on top of the generated structure and are not produced by gen_pages.py alone.

docs.title: Regenerating
docs.text:
python tools/extract.py --code /path/to/Conduvia/Code --out data
CONDUVIA_DATA=data CONDUVIA_DOCS=docs python tools/gen_pages.py
mkdocs build

docs.title: Custom components
docs.text: The look comes from docs/assets/css/conduvia.css (the field-manual design system) and tiers.css (per-tier accents). The :ci[item_id] item chips and :icon[item_id] glyphs are expanded by a native MkDocs hook, hooks/shortcodes.py — no third-party plugin required.

docs.title: Card & badge legend
docs.text: A quick key to the symbols used throughout this manual.

docs.title: Quest badges
docs.text: Badge Meaning Win-path On the critical line to orbit. Do these. Checkpoint Closes out a chapter / unlocks the next tier. Branch Optional. Quality-of-life, comfort, or a side-system. Briefing Read-only intro or explainer, no crafting. Exchange Token trade — upgrades and bundles.

docs.title: Quest card anatomy
docs.text:
Each quest card shows, top to bottom:

  • the badge and title;
  • Unlocks after — the quests you must finish first;
  • a short “what do I do” summary in the game’s own voice;
  • Do this — the concrete tasks, each with a kind tag (craft, retrieve, place …), what it needs (inputs) and what it yields (outputs);
  • Rewards (and Costs, for exchanges);
  • a collapsible Background with the real-world context.

docs.title: Item chips
docs.text: Items appear as chips: ROrope is an item, and PFplant fibers×10 is an item with a count (here, ten). The little square is the item’s icon — or a category-tinted monogram until its art is finished.

docs.title: Station slots
docs.text: in ×N input slots · fuel ×N fuel slots · out ×N output slots · no panel works just by being placed.

docs.title: Tier colours
docs.text: Each tier owns an accent colour, used on its chapter page, rungs, and cards: Survival (moss), Stone (ash), Metal Ages (copper), Steam (brass), Steel & DC (gunmetal/amber), Electric (arc-blue), Solid State (violet), Space (indigo).

docs.title: Stations
docs.text:
Stations are the machines and work surfaces you craft at. Each one is modeled on a piece of real-world equipment. Drop a station from your inventory to place it, then open it to queue recipes. Stations marked passive have no crafting panel — they work or monitor automatically once placed.

AC District Network network_ac_district in ×1 out ×2

Gathers the power from several generators and spreads it across a whole area — a hub where the wires meet before fanning out to the buildings around it.

Can't be opened — it just shows the network's state.

AC Grid Mimic Panel panel_mimic_ac_grid in ×1 out ×2

A board that shows your whole power grid at a glance — how much each generator is making, which switches are on or off, and where something has gone wrong. (A status board like this is called a mimic panel.)

Just for reading — there's nothing to click.

AC Induction Motor motor_ac_induction_small in ×1 out ×2

Plug it into back-and-forth electricity and it just spins — no contacts rubbing or wearing out, so it's tough and nearly maintenance-free. (Back-and-forth current is called AC, and this kind of motor is an induction motor.)

Needs an AC supply at the right frequency.

Air Separation Unit air_separation_unit in ×4 out ×2 Aluminothermic Crucible aluminothermic_crucible in ×4 out ×2 Amine Gas Treater amine_gas_treater in ×4 out ×2 Anode Baking Furnace anode_baking_furnace in ×4 out ×2 AOD Converter aod_converter in ×4 out ×2 APT Crystallizer apt_crystallizer in ×4 out ×2 Assembly Cell assembler_workshop in ×4 out ×2 Bark Storage Crate storage_crate_bark in ×27 Basic Bedroll bedroll_basic in ×1 out ×2 Basic Oxygen Furnace basic_oxygen_furnace in ×4 out ×2 Bayer Digester bayer_digester in ×4 out ×2 Beehive Coke Oven coke_oven_beehive in ×1 fuel ×3 out ×2 Bellows bellows in ×1 out ×2

Pump this air bag by hand to blast air into a fire. More air makes the fuel burn hotter — hot enough to work metal a plain fire can't touch. Set it next to a forge and it speeds up the heat.

Place it next to a Forge Hearth or Clay Bloomery.

Bessemer Converter bessemer_converter_basic in ×1 out ×2 Blast Furnace blast_furnace_small in ×9 out ×2 Brine Treatment Unit brine_treatment_unit in ×4 out ×2 Butchering Block butchering_block_basic in ×1 out ×2 Camp Marker camp_marker_basic in ×9 out ×2 Carbon Incandescent Lamp incandescent_lamp_carbon in ×1 out ×2

Runs a little electricity through a thin carbon thread until it glows warm amber. It's one of the first light bulbs — pretty, but fragile and quick to burn out. (A bulb that lights up by heating a thread like this is called incandescent.)

Screw it into a lamp fixture; swap it out when it burns out.

Carbonation Reactor carbonation_reactor in ×4 out ×2 Carving Bench carving_bench in ×9 out ×2 Cast Iron Firebox firebox_cast_iron in ×1

A part, not a machine. This is the iron box where the fire burns inside a boiler. Cast it at the Clay Casting Pit first, then use it as one of the pieces when you build the Riveted Boiler.

A part used to build the Riveted Boiler.

Caustic Digester Autoclave caustic_digester_autoclave in ×4 out ×2 Caustic Evaporator caustic_evaporator in ×4 out ×2 Cement Ball Mill cement_ball_mill in ×4 out ×2 Charcoal Clamp charcoal_clamp_earth_mound_kiln in ×1 fuel ×3 out ×2 Charcoal Drum Kiln charcoal_drum_kiln in ×6 out ×3 Charcoal Retort Kiln charcoal_retort_kiln in ×1 fuel ×3 out ×2 Claus Sulfur Unit claus_sulfur_unit in ×4 out ×2 Clay Bloomery clay_bloomery_furnace in ×1 fuel ×3 out ×2 Clay Casting Pit clay_casting_pit in ×1 out ×2 Clay Crucible clay_crucible_small in ×9 out ×2 Clay Pot clay_pot in ×1 out ×2 Contact Converter (V2O5 beds, DCDA) contact_converter in ×6 out ×3 Copper Converter (Peirce-Smith) copper_converter in ×6 out ×3 Cryogenic Helium Unit cryo_helium_unit in ×4 out ×2 Cupellation Hearth cupellation_hearth in ×4 out ×2 Czochralski Crystal Puller czochralski_crystal_puller in ×4 out ×2 DC Workshop Dynamo dynamo_dc_workshop in ×1 out ×2 Disc Pelletizer pelletizer_disc in ×4 out ×2 DRI Shaft Furnace dri_shaft_furnace in ×4 out ×2 Drying Rack drying_rack_survival in ×1 out ×2 Earthen Kiln earthen_kiln_updraft in ×4 fuel ×3 out ×2 Earthenware Vessel earthenware_vessel in ×1 out ×2 Electric Arc Furnace arc_furnace_electric in ×4 out ×2 Electric Ball Mill ball_mill_ore in ×4 out ×3 Electric Induction Furnace induction_furnace_electric in ×4 out ×2 Electric Jaw Crusher jaw_crusher_electric in ×4 out ×3 Electric Machine Shop machine_shop_electric in ×9 out ×2 Electrolytic Refining Cell electrolytic_refining_cell in ×6 out ×3 Electrowinning Cell electrowinning_cell in ×4 out ×2 Engine Lathe lathe_workshop in ×2 out ×2 Feedwater Pump feedwater_pump_simple in ×1

A part, not a machine. A pump that pushes the recovered water back into the boiler so it never runs dry. Used when building the Riveted Boiler.

A part used to build the Riveted Boiler.

Fire-Refining Furnace fire_refining_furnace in ×6 out ×3 Firewood Rack storage_firewood_rack in ×1

Stack split wood on this rack to keep it up off the wet ground. Air moves around it and dries it out faster, so it burns better and doesn't rot.

Shift-click firewood from your inventory to store it quickly.

Fishing Weir fishing_trap_weir in ×1 out ×2 Flash Smelting Furnace flash_smelting_furnace in ×4 out ×2 Fluid Handler station_fluid_handler in ×1 out ×2

A pipe fitting that splits, joins, and steers liquids and gases between machines. A valve sets how much gets through, and a gauge shows what's flowing and how fast.

Inspect it to see the flow rate and which fluid is moving.

Fluidized Bed Chlorinator fluidized_bed_chlorinator in ×4 out ×2 Fluoride Digestion Reactor fluoride_digestion_reactor in ×4 out ×2 Fluorine Cell fluorine_cell in ×4 out ×2 Flyball Governor flyball_governor passive Flywheel flywheel passive Food Storage Pit storage_food_pit in ×9 Forge Hearth forge_hearth in ×1 fuel ×3 out ×2 Francis Water Turbine water_turbine_francis in ×1 out ×1

Water under head from a penstock enters the spiral scroll case, spins the enclosed runner, and drains down the draft tube. The shaft delivers steady baseload power to a generator day and night, as long as the water keeps flowing.

Feed it penstock water; couple its shaft to a generator.

Froth Flotation Cell froth_flotation_cell in ×6 out ×3 Fuel Rod Assembly Line fuel_rod_assembly_line in ×4 out ×2 Gas Centrifuge Cascade gas_centrifuge_cascade in ×4 out ×2 Gas Compressor station_gas_compressor in ×1 out ×2

Squeezes a gas into a much smaller space, packing it under high pressure so it can be piped onward or stored. A spinning shaft drives the piston that does the squeezing.

Sends pressurized gas on to the next machine.

Gas Generator Set genset_gas_engine in ×1 out ×1

A gas engine bolted to an alternator. Load raw natural gas into the input slot and it burns it to put AC onto the line on demand, venting CO2 to the output. It only supplies power while it is running and fuelled -- your dispatchable backup for when the wind drops and the sun is down.

Load natural gas to run; it feeds AC to the wired network.

Gas Well Derrick gas_well_derrick in ×4 out ×2 Geared Gristmill gristmill_geared in ×2 out ×2 Glass Bench glass_bench in ×1 out ×2 Haber-Bosch Reactor haber_bosch_reactor in ×4 out ×2 Hall-Heroult Cell hall_heroult_cell in ×4 out ×2 Hand Crafting hand_crafting_2x2 in ×4 out ×1 Heap Leach Pad heap_leach_pad in ×4 out ×2 Helium Purifier helium_purifier in ×4 out ×2 Hf-Zr Separation Column hf_zr_separation_column in ×4 out ×2 Hot Blast Stove hot_blast_stove in ×1 out ×2

Burns the blast furnace's own waste gas to heat a tall stack of firebrick red-hot. Then the draft is switched: cold air is blown through the hot brick and comes out scorching. Feeding the furnace hot air instead of cold lets it run far hotter on much less fuel. This isn't a crafting station -- it's a helper that makes the furnace next to it work better. (It stores heat in brick and hands it back to the air -- a regenerative, or 'Cowper', stove.)

Place it next to a Blast Furnace to preheat its air blast.

Hunter Reduction Retort hunter_reduction_retort in ×4 out ×2 Hydrogen Reduction Furnace hydrogen_reduction_furnace in ×4 out ×2 Industrial Lamp Fixture lamp_fixture_industrial in ×1 out ×2

A hanging lamp that throws bright light down over a work area so you can see what you're doing after dark. Holds either a glowing-thread bulb or a brighter arc lamp.

Needs power on the circuit to light up.

Iron Anvil iron_anvil_block in ×1 out ×2 Iron Belt Pulley belt_pulley_iron passive Jet Condenser condenser_jet in ×1

A part, not a machine. It cools the used-up steam leaving an engine and turns it back into water, so that water can be boiled again instead of wasted — which saves fuel. Used when building the Riveted Boiler. (Turning steam back into water is called condensing.)

A part used to build the Riveted Boiler.

Knapping Station knapping_station in ×4 out ×2 Lab Vacuum Pump vacuum_pump_lab in ×1 out ×2

Sucks air out of a sealed space until there's almost nothing left inside — a vacuum. That low pressure helps you filter liquids, boil things at lower heat, and seal glass tubes.

Needs a mechanical power input (a spinning shaft).

A tall wooden pole that holds electric wires up in the air and out of the way. Line up a row of them to carry power across your base — the simplest way to move electricity around.

Run wire from the top of one pole to the next.

Stand up this wooden frame first, then lean branches and dried grass against it to fill in the walls. It's the skeleton your shelter is built around.

Add thatch (bundled dried grass) to fill in the walls.

Feed in steady one-way electricity and this motor spins a shaft, turning that power into motion to run small machines. (Steady one-way current like this is called DC, direct current.)

Needs a DC (one-way current) supply.

Solar PV Array solar_pv_array in ×1 out ×1

A field of photovoltaic modules that turns sunlight straight into low-voltage DC, exported from the cable on its underside. No fuel, no moving parts, no emissions -- but it only makes power while the sun is up.

Wire the DC output into your network. Daylight only.

Solvent Extraction Mixer-Settler sx_mixer_settler in ×4 out ×2 Steam Cylinder steam_cylinder_cast_iron in ×1

A part, not a machine. This is the metal tube that steam pushes inside to drive an engine. Bore it out smooth at the Steam Machine Shop, then use it to build the Simple Steam Engine.

A part used to build the Simple Steam Engine.

Steam Drill steam_drill in ×1 out ×2

Steam hammers a hard steel bit into solid rock over and over, punching holes far faster than swinging a hammer by hand. Use it to drill the holes you pack with explosives.

Needs a steam supply under pressure and a hardened drill bit.

Steam Machine Shop machine_shop_steam in ×9 out ×2 Steam Reformer steam_reformer in ×4 out ×2 Steam Rolling Mill rolling_mill_steam in ×4 out ×2 Steam Turbine steam_turbine in ×1 out ×1

Pipe high-pressure steam into the top inlet and it expands through the bladed rotor, spinning the shaft far faster and more efficiently than a piston engine. Couple the shaft to an AC generator to make plant-scale power; the exhaust runs out the side to a condenser.

Feed it steam; couple its shaft to a generator.

A carved post you put up after hunting 5 birds. It does nothing but look good -- a way to show what you have done and decorate your camp.

Awarded for hunting 5 birds.

Trophy: Builder trophy_builder passive

A little plaque you earn for placing 25 stations -- proof you have built up a real working camp. It is for decoration only.

Awarded for placing 25 stations.

Trophy: Deerstalker trophy_deerstalker passive

Mounted antlers on a polished slab, earned by hunting 10 deer. Stand it up to show off -- it is decoration, not a crafting station.

Awarded for hunting 10 deer.

Trophy: First Iron trophy_first_iron passive

A small plaque marking the very first iron you pulled from your bloomery -- a big jump from stone to metal. A keepsake to show off.

Awarded for smelting your first iron.

Trophy: Lumberjack trophy_lumberjack passive

A stump-and-axe display for chopping down 50 trees. Set it up to show you are a full-fledged lumberjack. Decoration only.

Awarded for felling 50 trees.

Trophy: Master Farmer trophy_master_farmer passive

A carved wooden bushel (a big harvest basket) celebrating 100 crops gathered. Put it on display to show off your green thumb.

Awarded for harvesting 100 crops.

Trophy: Miner trophy_miner passive

A little stack of carved stones (a cairn) marking long hours with a pick and shovel -- 250 blocks of earth dug out. A camp decoration.

Awarded for mining 250 blocks of earth.

Trophy: Wanderer trophy_wanderer passive

A weathered traveller's marker for walking 5000 studs across the world -- a few hours of unhurried exploring. Stand it up as a memento.

Awarded for walking 5000 studs.

Trophy: Wolfbane trophy_wolfbane passive

A wolf-pelt drum stand earned the hard way, by taking down 3 wolves. A trophy that shows you held your own against the pack.

Awarded for defeating 3 wolves.

A tall chimney that carries the smoke and hot fumes from your furnaces and boilers up and away, well above where you're working, so you're not breathing them in. (A tall exhaust chimney like this is called a vent stack.)

Connect it to a furnace or kiln's smoke port (the flue).

Wafer Fab Line wafer_fab_line in ×4 out ×2 Waste Midden waste_midden_basic in ×1

Toss your camp scraps here and they slowly rot away in one spot, instead of piling up and drawing hungry animals to your base. (An old name for a trash heap like this is a midden.)

Drop guts, failed crafts, and bone meal here.

Wattle Screen wattle_screen in ×1 out ×2

Weave thin springy rods over and under each other into a stiff panel you can stand up, like a flat basket. Use it to block wind, screen off an area, or split an animal pen. (This woven-rod panel is called wattle.)

Place it next to animal pens or open camp edges.

A tall tower with a three-blade rotor driving an internal gearbox and generator. It feeds bulk AC down the tower into the grid whenever the wind blows -- clean and fuel-free, but only as strong as the breeze.

Wire the AC output into your network. Windy weather only.

Drop this wooden slide between two stations that are touching, and items slide from one into the next on their own. Gravity does the carrying, so you never have to move them by hand once it's placed.

Line up one station's output with the next station's input. Items roll downhill.

A part, not a machine. A wooden tank that holds the cool water the condenser needs to chill steam back into water. Used when building the Riveted Boiler — set it near the boiler so the steam plant looks right.

A part used to build the Riveted Boiler.

Flip the switches here to send power from the generator out to whatever you want to run, and the dials show how much current each line is pulling. A fuse cuts a line if it pulls too much and gets dangerous. (The dials that read current are called ammeters.)

Open it to connect circuits and read the live current.

A part, not a machine. A heavy iron beam that rocks up and down on a pivot like a giant see-saw, passing the engine's push along to the machinery. Used when building the Simple Steam Engine.

A part used to build the Simple Steam Engine.
